documents.title: Real Numbers & the Number Line
documents.subtitle: The universe calculus lives in
documents.tags: real numbers number line integers rationals irrationals inequalities absolute value intervals
documents.content: Imagine a perfectly straight ruler stretching infinitely in both directions. Every single point on that ruler corresponds to exactly one real number, and every real number corresponds to exactly one point. No gaps, no overlaps. This is the **real number line**, and it is the stage on which all of calculus plays out. The real numbers are built up in layers ‚ÄĒ like Russian nesting dolls, each family of numbers fitting inside the next. **Natural numbers ‚Ąē = {1, 2, 3, 4, ‚Ä¶}** ‚ÄĒ the counting numbers. These are the numbers you used first as a child. Note: some books include 0, some don't. For calculus, it rarely matters. **Integers ‚Ą§ = {‚Ä¶, ‚ąí3, ‚ąí2, ‚ąí1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ‚Ä¶}** ‚ÄĒ extend the naturals by including zero and negatives. The ‚Ą§ comes from the German word "Zahlen" meaning "numbers." You can add and subtract freely within ‚Ą§. **Rational numbers ‚Ąö = {p/q : p, q ‚ąą ‚Ą§, q ‚Č† 0}** ‚ÄĒ all fractions. These include 1/2, ‚ąí3/7, 5 (which equals 5/1), and 0.333‚Ä¶ (which equals 1/3). The key test: a decimal is rational if and only if it terminates (like 0.25 = 1/4) or eventually repeats in a cycle (like 0.142857142857‚Ä¶ = 1/7). The ‚Ąö comes from "quotient." **Irrational numbers** ‚ÄĒ the rest. Numbers like ‚ąö2, ‚ąö3, ŌÄ, e, and ‚ąõ5 whose decimal expansions never terminate and never settle into a repeating pattern. Between any two rational numbers there is an irrational, and between any two irrationals there is a rational ‚ÄĒ they interleave everywhere, infinitely densely. **Real numbers ‚ĄĚ = ‚Ąö ‚ą™ irrationals** ‚ÄĒ everything. The real line has no "holes." This is the key property that separates ‚ĄĚ from ‚Ąö and makes calculus possible: every infinite decimal that seems like it "should" converge to something actually does converge to a real number. Why does this matter for calculus? Because limits, derivatives, and integrals all depend on the idea of numbers getting arbitrarily close together. If the number line had holes (like ‚Ąö does), limits might "fall through" those holes and fail to exist. The completeness of ‚ĄĚ is what prevents this. We describe subsets of the real line using **interval notation**. An interval is a connected set of real numbers ‚ÄĒ no gaps. There are four types, distinguished by whether the endpoints are included. **Open interval (a, b)** = {x ‚ąą ‚ĄĚ : a < x < b}. Endpoints NOT included. Draw with open circles on the number line. Think of "open" as "the door is open ‚ÄĒ the endpoints escaped." **Closed interval [a, b]** = {x ‚ąą ‚ĄĚ : a ‚Č§ x ‚Č§ b}. Both endpoints included. Draw with filled circles. Think of "closed" as "the door is shut ‚ÄĒ the endpoints are trapped inside." **Half-open intervals**: [a, b) includes a but not b; (a, b] includes b but not a. **Infinite intervals**: (a, ‚ąě) means all x > a; (‚ąí‚ąě, b] means all x ‚Č§ b; (‚ąí‚ąě, ‚ąě) = ‚ĄĚ. We always use parentheses next to ‚ąě because infinity is not a number ‚ÄĒ you can never "include" it. The **absolute value** |x| captures the idea of distance. It strips away the sign, keeping only the magnitude. This is why |x| ‚Č• 0 always. The absolute value satisfies the crucial **triangle inequality** |a + b| ‚Č§ |a| + |b|, which you will use constantly in epsilon-delta proofs. Solving an absolute value inequality requires careful case analysis because the absolute value function behaves differently for positive and negative inputs: ‚ÄĘ |A| < c (for c > 0) means ‚ąíc < A < c (a single interval) ‚ÄĘ |A| > c (for c > 0) means A > c OR A < ‚ąíc (two separate rays) ‚ÄĘ |A| = c means A = c or A = ‚ąíc (two solutions) The property that distinguishes ‚ĄĚ from ‚Ąö is called the **Completeness Axiom** (also called the Least Upper Bound Property). It is not a theorem ‚ÄĒ it is an axiom, a foundational assumption we take as the definition of the real numbers. First, some vocabulary: a set S is **bounded above** if there exists some number M with x ‚Č§ M for all x ‚ąą S. We call M an **upper bound**. The **supremum** (least upper bound, written sup S) is the smallest upper bound ‚ÄĒ the tightest ceiling. For example, S = {x : x¬≤ < 2} = (‚ąí‚ąö2, ‚ąö2). The number 5 is an upper bound. So is 2. So is 1.5. But the smallest upper bound is ‚ąö2 itself. Is ‚ąö2 in ‚Ąö? No. So if we were working in ‚Ąö, this set would have no supremum in ‚Ąö ‚ÄĒ the "tightest ceiling" falls through a hole. The Completeness Axiom says this never happens in ‚ĄĚ: every non-empty set bounded above has a supremum that is itself a real number. This is the engine behind the Intermediate Value Theorem, the Extreme Value Theorem, and ultimately the existence of definite integrals. Without completeness, the derivative might not equal zero at a local maximum (because the maximum might fall through a hole). Every major theorem in calculus depends on this axiom. A consequence: every **Cauchy sequence** (a sequence where terms get arbitrarily close to each other) converges to a real number. In ‚Ąö, the sequence 1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, 1.4142, ‚Ä¶ is Cauchy but has no rational limit. In ‚ĄĚ, it converges to ‚ąö2. Writing Sets in Interval Notation The interval (‚ąí3, 5] has length 5 ‚ąí (‚ąí3) = 8. It contains infinitely many real numbers, including irrationals like ‚ąö5 ‚Čą 2.236. Read the inequality carefully: x is STRICTLY greater than ‚ąí3 (open left endpoint) and LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO 5 (closed right endpoint). Parenthesis at ‚ąí3: not included. Bracket at 5: included. Always write left endpoint first.   Solving an Absolute Value Equation Two solutions: x = 5 and x = ‚ąí2. Geometrically: |2x‚ąí3| = 7 means the distance from the point (2x‚ąí3) to 0 is 7, i.e., 2x‚ąí3 is 7 units from the origin on the number line. |A| = 7 means A = 7 or A = ‚ąí7. There are always at most two solutions.  Add 3 to both sides. Divide both sides by 2.  Add 3 to both sides. Divide both sides by 2.   Absolute Value Inequality ‚ÄĒ "Less Than" Case Solution: (1, 7). Geometrically, this is all points on the number line within distance 3 of x = 4. Center 4, radius 3 ‚Üí interval (4‚ąí3, 4+3) = (1, 7). Geometric reading: the distance from x to 4 is less than 3. So x is within 3 units of 4. Algebraic rule: |A| < c (c > 0) becomes ‚ąíc < A < c. Add 4 to all three parts of the compound inequality. Simplify. Interval notation. Both endpoints open. Confirms 1 and 7 are correctly excluded. The center x = 4 satisfies the inequality. Absolute Value Inequality ‚ÄĒ "Greater Than" Case The solution is two rays pointing outward: everything far enough from the "center" of the expression. Notice this is the complement of the open interval (‚ąí5/3, 1), which would be the solution to |3x+1| < 4. |A| ‚Č• c means A ‚Č• c OR A ‚Č§ ‚ąíc. This gives two separate pieces, not a single interval.  Subtract 1 from both sides. Divide by 3 (positive ‚ÄĒ direction unchanged).  Subtract 1. Divide by 3. Combine both cases with the union symbol ‚ą™. Both endpoints are closed (‚Č• and ‚Č§). Inequality Involving Absolute Value and Fractions Solution: [‚ąí5, 1]. Rule: always isolate the absolute value before applying the case analysis. Isolate the absolute value expression first. Multiply both sides by 3 (positive, so direction unchanged). Apply the rule: |A| ‚Č§ c becomes ‚ąíc ‚Č§ A ‚Č§ c. Subtract 2 from all three parts. Simplify. Both endpoints included (‚Č§). Finding a Supremum sup S = 1, but max S is undefined because 1 is never actually achieved. This is a clean example of why sup and max are different concepts. Rewrite by dividing: n/(n+1) = (n+1‚ąí1)/(n+1) = 1 ‚ąí 1/(n+1). This form is easier to analyze. The terms get closer and closer to 1. Since 1/(n+1) > 0 for all finite n, we always have a_n = 1 ‚ąí (positive) < 1. So 1 is an upper bound. We want to show 1 is the LEAST upper bound. Solve for which n makes a_n exceed M. We can always find a large enough natural number. So M is NOT an upper bound. No smaller upper bound works, so 1 is the supremum. Note: 1 ‚ąČ S, so max S does not exist. Solve |5 - 2x| \leq 3 and express the solution set as an interval. Prove the triangle inequality: |a + b| \leq |a| + |b| for all a, b \in \mathbb{R}. Solve the compound inequality |x - 1| + |x + 1| = 4.

documents.title: Functions
documents.subtitle: Machines that take inputs and produce exactly one output
documents.tags: functions domain range composition inverse graphs transformations piecewise even odd
documents.content: Think of a function as a machine with a single input slot and a single output slot. You feed in an input, and the machine reliably spits out exactly one output. Same input ‚Üí same output, every time. No randomness, no ambiguity, no "it depends." The crucial word is **exactly one**. A machine that sometimes gives two different outputs for the same input is not a function. This is why a circle is not a function of x: for x = 0, a circle of radius 1 gives both y = 1 and y = ‚ąí1. The **vertical line test** for graphs: draw every possible vertical line. If any vertical line intersects the graph at more than one point, the graph does not represent a function (because that x-value maps to multiple y-values). The set of all allowed inputs is called the **domain**. The set of all actual outputs is called the **range** (or image). These might be smaller than you expect: the function f(x) = x¬≤ has domain ‚ĄĚ, but its range is [0, ‚ąě) ‚ÄĒ you can never get a negative output by squaring a real number. We write **f : A ‚Üí B** to say "f is a function from domain A to codomain B." We write **f(x) = [rule]** to specify the rule. The notation f(x) is read "f of x" ‚ÄĒ it means the output of the function f when given input x. It does NOT mean f times x. When no domain is stated, we use the **natural domain**: the largest set of real numbers for which the formula produces a well-defined real output. Three things can go wrong: 1. **Division by zero**: exclude x values making the denominator zero. 2. **Even roots of negatives**: the expression under ‚ąö (or ‚Āī‚ąö, ‚Ā∂‚ąö, etc.) must be ‚Č• 0. 3. **Logarithm of a non-positive number**: log requires a strictly positive argument. Given a function, the first questions to ask are: what is its domain? What is its range? How does its graph look? **Finding the natural domain**: set up inequalities to avoid all problems. If there is both a square root AND a denominator, both conditions must hold simultaneously (intersection of the constraint sets). **Even and odd functions** ‚ÄĒ these symmetries simplify calculus significantly: ‚ÄĘ A function is **even** if f(‚ąíx) = f(x) for all x in the domain. The graph is symmetric about the y-axis. Examples: f(x) = x¬≤, f(x) = cos x, f(x) = |x|. ‚ÄĘ A function is **odd** if f(‚ąíx) = ‚ąíf(x) for all x in the domain. The graph is symmetric about the origin (180¬į rotational symmetry). Examples: f(x) = x¬≥, f(x) = sin x, f(x) = x. ‚ÄĘ Most functions are neither even nor odd. **Composition** f ‚ąė g means "apply g first, then apply f to the result": (f ‚ąė g)(x) = f(g(x)). The output of g becomes the input to f. Composition is generally not commutative: f ‚ąė g ‚Č† g ‚ąė f. **Inverse functions**: f has an inverse f‚ĀĽ¬Ļ if and only if f is **one-to-one** (injective) ‚ÄĒ distinct inputs always produce distinct outputs. Graphically: the graph passes the horizontal line test. The inverse satisfies f‚ĀĽ¬Ļ(f(x)) = x and f(f‚ĀĽ¬Ļ(y)) = y. Geometrically, the graph of f‚ĀĽ¬Ļ is the reflection of the graph of f across the line y = x. **Graph transformations** ‚ÄĒ understanding these lets you graph complicated functions from simple ones: ‚ÄĘ y = f(x) + k: vertical shift UP by k ‚ÄĘ y = f(x) ‚ąí k: vertical shift DOWN by k ‚ÄĘ y = f(x + h): horizontal shift LEFT by h (counterintuitive!) ‚ÄĘ y = f(x ‚ąí h): horizontal shift RIGHT by h ‚ÄĘ y = af(x): vertical stretch by factor |a|; reflects over x-axis if a < 0 ‚ÄĘ y = f(bx): horizontal compression by factor |b|; reflects over y-axis if b < 0 In set theory, a function f : A ‚Üí B is formally defined as a **relation** ‚ÄĒ a set of ordered pairs (x, y) with x ‚ąą A and y ‚ąą B ‚ÄĒ such that for each x ‚ąą A, there is **exactly one** y with (x, y) in the relation. No formula required. This allows us to speak of functions that have no closed-form expression. The **Dirichlet function** D: ‚ĄĚ ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ defined by D(x) = 1 if x ‚ąą ‚Ąö, D(x) = 0 if x ‚ąČ ‚Ąö, is a perfectly valid function. It is discontinuous at every single real number ‚ÄĒ impossible to draw, but rigorously well-defined. The precise taxonomy of functions is defined as follows: ‚ÄĘ **Injective (one-to-one)**: f(a) = f(b) ‚üĻ a = b. Different inputs always give different outputs. ‚ÄĘ **Surjective (onto)**: for every y ‚ąą B, there exists x ‚ąą A with f(x) = y. Every element of the codomain is hit. ‚ÄĘ **Bijective**: both injective and surjective. These are exactly the functions that have inverses. A bijection f : A ‚Üí B means A and B have the same "size" (cardinality). This is how we formalize the idea that the real numbers in (0,1) are just as numerous as all of ‚ĄĚ ‚ÄĒ there exists a bijection between them. Calculus uses bijections constantly when making substitutions. Finding the Natural Domain Domain = [‚ąí3, 1) ‚ą™ (1, +‚ąě). Always solve for each constraint separately, then intersect. f(0) = ‚ąö3/(-1) = ‚ąí‚ąö3 is defined. f(1) is undefined (zero denominator). f(‚ąí4) is undefined (negative radicand). Two potential problems: a square root (requires non-negative argument) and division (requires nonzero denominator). Both conditions must hold simultaneously. The radicand must be non-negative. Subtract 3. Denominator cannot be zero.  Both conditions must hold simultaneously ‚ÄĒ take the intersection. All x ‚Č• ‚ąí3 with x = 1 punched out. Write as a union of two intervals. Function Composition ‚ÄĒ Order Matters f‚ąėg ‚Č† g‚ąėf: composition is generally NOT commutative. Notice how (f‚ąėg)(x) = 4x¬≤‚ąí12x+10 is completely different from (g‚ąėf)(x) = 2x¬≤‚ąí1. Apply g first, then f. g(x) = 2x‚ąí3 becomes the input to f. Apply f: replace x in f(x) = x¬≤+1 with the entire expression (2x‚ąí3). Expand (2x‚ąí3)¬≤: use (a‚ąíb)¬≤ = a¬≤‚ąí2ab+b¬≤ with a=2x, b=3. Simplify. ‚ÄĒ Now reverse the order ‚ÄĒ Apply f first, then g. Apply g: replace x in g(x) = 2x‚ąí3 with (x¬≤+1). Distribute and simplify. ‚ÄĒ Composing f with itself ‚ÄĒ  Replace x with (x¬≤+1) in f(x) = x¬≤+1. Expand (x¬≤+1)¬≤ = x‚Āī+2x¬≤+1. Testing Even/Odd/Neither (a) even, (b) neither, (c) odd. Quick rule: a polynomial with only even-degree terms (x¬≤, x‚Āī, ‚Ä¶) is even; only odd-degree terms (x, x¬≥, x‚ĀĶ, ‚Ä¶) is odd; mixed degrees ‚Üí neither (usually). Compute f(‚ąíx). (‚ąíx)‚Āī = x‚Āī and (‚ąíx)¬≤ = x¬≤. Passes even function test. Graph symmetric about y-axis.  Compute g(‚ąíx). (‚ąíx)¬≥ = ‚ąíx¬≥, (‚ąíx)¬≤ = x¬≤. Not equal to g(x). Also not equal to ‚ąíg(x). Neither even nor odd.   Compute h(‚ąíx).   h(‚ąíx) exactly equals ‚ąíh(x). Graph has 180¬į rotational symmetry about origin. Graphing via Transformations The vertex form y = a(x‚ąíh)¬≤ + k gives: vertex at (h, k), opens up if a > 0, down if a < 0, narrower if |a| > 1, wider if 0 < |a| < 1. Here a = ‚ąí2, h = ‚ąí3, k = 1. Start with the basic parabola. Vertex at (0, 0), opens upward, symmetric about y-axis. Replace x with (x+3): horizontal shift LEFT by 3. The "+3 inside" means left, because we need x+3 = 0 ‚Üí x = ‚ąí3. New vertex: (‚ąí3, 0). Multiply by 2: vertical STRETCH by factor 2. Parabola is now narrower (points are twice as far from x-axis). Vertex unchanged: (‚ąí3, 0). Multiply by ‚ąí1 (the negative sign): REFLECT over x-axis. Parabola now opens DOWNWARD. Vertex unchanged: (‚ąí3, 0). Add 1: vertical shift UP by 1. Vertex moves to (‚ąí3, 1).   Evaluating and Graphing a Piecewise Function f is not one-to-one ‚ÄĒ the constant piece "7 for x ‚Č• 3" makes many inputs share the same output. For a function to have an inverse, every output must come from exactly one input.           Two different inputs (3 and 5) give the same output (7). It fails the horizontal line test: the horizontal line y = 7 hits the graph at every x ‚Č• 3. Finding an Inverse Function f‚ĀĽ¬Ļ(x) = (3x+1)/(x‚ąí2), domain all x ‚Č† 2. The verification confirms the algebra is correct. Replace f(x) with y. SWAP x and y. This is the key step that finds the inverse. Multiply both sides by (y‚ąí3). Distribute x. Move all y-terms to the left, all non-y terms to the right. Factor y from the left side. Divide by (x‚ąí2), valid when x ‚Č† 2. Domain of f‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = range of f. Since f has a horizontal asymptote at y = 2, f never outputs 2, so x = 2 is excluded. Check by plugging f‚ĀĽ¬Ļ(x) into f. Simplify over common denominator (x‚ąí2). Result is x. ‚úď Position as a Function of Time (Physics) The graph of x(t) = t¬≥ ‚ąí 3t records the car's complete history. The height gives position, the slope gives velocity (which we'll compute in Chapter 2 as x'(t) = 3t¬≤‚ąí3). The car starts at the origin, moves backward to x = ‚ąí2 at t=1, reverses, and passes the origin again at t=‚ąö3. The slope of x(t) is zero at t=1 (the turning point) ‚ÄĒ this will be the derivative equal to zero. At t=0, the car is AT the reference point. At t=1, the car is 2 metres BEHIND the reference point (negative = behind). At t = ‚ąö3 ‚Čą 1.73 s, the car is back at the reference point. At t=2, the car is 2 metres AHEAD of the reference point. Factor to find when the car is at the origin. Set x=0 and factor. Two times when the car passes the reference point. (t = -‚ąö3 is excluded since t ‚Č• 0.) Between t=0 and t=‚ąö3, x is negative ‚ÄĒ the car is behind the reference. Between t=‚ąö3 and t=2.5, x is positive ‚ÄĒ the car is ahead. Find the domain and range of f(x) = \sqrt{4 - x^2}. Show that the composition of two odd functions is odd. Every function f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} can be written as the sum of an even function and an odd function. Find the decomposition for f(x) = e^x.

documents.title: Trigonometry
documents.subtitle: Circles, angles, and the most important functions in calculus
documents.tags: trigonometry sine cosine tangent unit circle radians identities inverse trig
documents.content: Start with a circle of radius 1 centered at the origin ‚ÄĒ the **unit circle**. Pick any angle őł measured counterclockwise from the positive x-axis. The point where the terminal ray hits the circle has coordinates (cos őł, sin őł). Full stop. That's the definition. Everything else follows from this. Think of cos őł as the **horizontal shadow** and sin őł as the **vertical shadow** that the unit-circle point casts. As őł increases from 0 to 2ŌÄ (one full revolution), the point traces the whole circle. cos őł starts at 1, decreases to 0 at ŌÄ/2, hits ‚ąí1 at ŌÄ, returns to 0 at 3ŌÄ/2, and comes back to 1 at 2ŌÄ. sin őł starts at 0, rises to 1 at ŌÄ/2, returns to 0 at ŌÄ, dips to ‚ąí1 at 3ŌÄ/2, and comes back to 0 at 2ŌÄ. Angles in calculus are measured in **radians**, not degrees. 2ŌÄ radians = 360¬į. One radian is the angle that cuts off an arc equal to the radius. Radians make calculus formulas clean (derivatives of sin and cos have no ugly conversion factors). The Pythagorean theorem ‚ÄĒ a¬≤ + b¬≤ = c¬≤ ‚ÄĒ underpins all of trigonometry. The unit circle equation is x¬≤ + y¬≤ = 1, which is just Pythagoras applied to a right triangle with hypotenuse 1. From this, sin¬≤őł + cos¬≤őł = 1 follows immediately. That one identity generates all others. The six trig functions are defined as: sin őł and cos őł come directly from the unit circle. The rest are ratios: **Degrees ‚ÜĒ Radians**: multiply by ŌÄ/180 to convert degrees to radians. The **Pythagorean Identity** is the most important trig identity ‚ÄĒ it follows directly from the unit circle equation x¬≤ + y¬≤ = 1: From the Pythagorean identity, we derive two others by dividing through by cos¬≤őł or sin¬≤őł. The **even/odd identities**: cos(‚ąíőł) = cos őł (cosine is even), sin(‚ąíőł) = ‚ąísin őł (sine is odd). The **angle addition formulas** are the source of all product-to-sum and double-angle formulas: In analysis, the trig functions can be defined rigorously without reference to geometry, using power series: These series converge for all real x (the ratio test confirms this). From these definitions, one can prove all trig identities purely algebraically, and prove that the derivatives are d/dx[sin x] = cos x and d/dx[cos x] = ‚ąísin x. The Pythagorean theorem itself has over 370 known proofs. The most beautiful is the animated rearrangement proof: take four identical right triangles and arrange them two different ways inside the same (a+b)¬≤ square. In one arrangement, the remaining area is the tilted c¬≤ square. In the other, it is the two squares a¬≤ and b¬≤. Since the four triangles are identical in both arrangements, the remaining areas must be equal: c¬≤ = a¬≤ + b¬≤. The remarkable identity e^(iőł) = cos őł + i sin őł (Euler's formula) connects trig to the complex exponential ‚ÄĒ this is used constantly in engineering and physics. Finding Exact Trig Values Use: (1) find the quadrant, (2) find the reference angle, (3) look up the reference angle values, (4) apply the correct signs. 5ŌÄ/6 is in the second quadrant (between ŌÄ/2 and ŌÄ). In Q2, reference angle = ŌÄ ‚ąí őł. Known values for ŌÄ/6. Adjust signs for the quadrant.  Rationalize by multiplying by ‚ąö3/‚ąö3. Reciprocals. Proving a Trig Identity Strategy for proving identities: work on one side only, use known identities, factor, and simplify. Start with the left side. We try to transform it into the right side. Use the Pythagorean identity: sin¬≤x = 1 ‚ąí cos¬≤x. Factor the numerator as a difference of squares: 1 ‚ąí cos¬≤x = (1‚ąícosx)(1+cosx). Cancel (1 ‚ąí cos x), valid when cos x ‚Č† 1 (i.e., x ‚Č† 0, 2ŌÄ, ...). This equals the RHS. ‚ąé Solving a Trig Equation Three solutions on [0, 2ŌÄ): x = ŌÄ/2, 7ŌÄ/6, 11ŌÄ/6. This looks like a quadratic! Let u = sin x. Quadratic in u. Factor. Check: 2u¬≤ ‚ąí 2u + u ‚ąí 1 = 2u(u‚ąí1) + 1(u‚ąí1) = (2u+1)(u‚ąí1). ‚úď Solve each factor. Replace u with sin x. sin = 1 only at ŌÄ/2 on [0, 2ŌÄ). sin = ‚ąí1/2 in Q3 and Q4: reference angle ŌÄ/6, so x = ŌÄ + ŌÄ/6 = 7ŌÄ/6 and x = 2ŌÄ ‚ąí ŌÄ/6 = 11ŌÄ/6. Prove the identity: \tan x + \cot x = \sec x \csc x Find all x \in [0, 2\pi) such that \cos(2x) + \cos(x) = 0.

documents.title: Exponentials & Logarithms
documents.subtitle: Functions that grow (and shrink) at rates proportional to their size
documents.tags: exponentials logarithms natural log e base growth decay inverse compound interest
documents.content: You already understand exponential growth ‚ÄĒ you just might not know it yet. If someone offered you a choice between $1,000,000 today or a penny that doubles every day for 30 days, which would you choose? The penny starts laughably small: 1¬Ę, 2¬Ę, 4¬Ę, 8¬Ę‚Ä¶ After 10 days you have only $5.12. After 20 days, $5,242.88. But after 30 days? $5,368,709.12 ‚ÄĒ over five million dollars. That is the power of exponential growth: it starts slow and then explodes. Doubling repeatedly: 1 ‚Üí 2 ‚Üí 4 ‚Üí 8 ‚Üí 16 ‚Üí ‚Ä¶ After n doublings you have 2‚ĀŅ. This is exponential growth with base 2. The base determines how fast things grow. Base 3 would give you 1 ‚Üí 3 ‚Üí 9 ‚Üí 27 ‚Üí 81 (faster), while base 1.1 gives you 1 ‚Üí 1.1 ‚Üí 1.21 ‚Üí 1.331 (slower but still exponential). Here is the key idea that makes exponential functions special: **the rate of growth is proportional to the current size**. A colony of 1,000 bacteria doubling means 1,000 new bacteria. A colony of 1,000,000 doubling means 1,000,000 new bacteria. The bigger it is, the faster it grows. This is fundamentally different from linear growth (adding the same amount each time) or quadratic growth (adding an increasing but predictable amount). Now consider: is there a base b such that the function b^x grows at exactly the same rate as its own value? Yes ‚ÄĒ and that base is e ‚Čą 2.71828. This is why e^x is called the **natural exponential**. It is the one exponential function where the derivative equals the function itself. In calculus, this makes e^x far easier to work with than 2^x or 10^x. The **logarithm** is the inverse of exponentiation. If b^x = y, then log_b(y) = x. The logarithm asks: "what power do I raise b to in order to get y?" For example, log‚āā(8) = 3 because 2¬≥ = 8. And log‚āĀ‚āÄ(1000) = 3 because 10¬≥ = 1000. The **natural logarithm** ln(x) = log_e(x) is the inverse of e^x. If you want to "undo" e^x, apply ln. If you want to "undo" ln(x), apply e^x. These two functions are perfect mirrors: ln(e^x) = x and e^(ln x) = x. Logarithms turn multiplication into addition, division into subtraction, and powers into multiplication. This made them the primary tool for computation before calculators ‚ÄĒ and they still appear everywhere in calculus, physics, information theory, and music (decibels and pitch intervals are logarithmic). Exponential **decay** is just growth with a negative exponent: e^(-x) shrinks as x increases. Radioactive half-lives, cooling objects, and drug concentration in the bloodstream all follow exponential decay. If something has a half-life of T hours, after t hours you have (1/2)^(t/T) of the original ‚ÄĒ which is the same as e^(-t¬∑ln2/T). The **laws of exponents** (for any base b > 0, b ‚Č† 1): The **laws of logarithms** follow directly from the exponent laws. Each log law is just an exponent law rewritten in logarithmic form: The **change of base formula** lets you compute any log using ln or log‚āĀ‚āÄ: $\log_b x = \frac{\ln x}{\ln b}$. This is essential when your calculator only has ln and log‚āĀ‚āÄ buttons. For solving exponential equations, apply ln to both sides. For solving logarithmic equations, exponentiate both sides. The key principle: **ln and e^x are inverse operations**, just like addition and subtraction. The **domain** of ln(x) is x > 0 (you cannot take the logarithm of zero or a negative number). The **range** of ln(x) is all real numbers. The **domain** of e^x is all real numbers, and its **range** is (0, ‚ąě) ‚ÄĒ exponentials are always positive. The number e can be defined rigorously in several equivalent ways. We present the three most important: The **limit definition**: $e = \lim_{n \to \infty}(1 + 1/n)^n$. This comes from compound interest: if $1 is invested at 100% annual interest compounded n times per year, the balance after one year is $(1 + 1/n)^n$. As n ‚Üí ‚ąě (continuous compounding), this approaches e. The **series definition**: $e = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 1/n! = 1 + 1 + 1/2 + 1/6 + 1/24 + \cdots$ This series converges extremely fast ‚ÄĒ just 10 terms gives e accurate to 7 decimal places. The **integral definition of ln**: $\ln(x) = \int_1^x (1/t)\, dt$ for x > 0. This definition makes proving logarithm properties completely rigorous without circular reasoning. We then define e as the unique number satisfying ln(e) = 1. The fact that these three definitions all give the same number (e ‚Čą 2.71828182845904‚Ä¶) is a theorem that requires proof. The equivalence is usually shown in a first course in real analysis. An important fact: e is **irrational** (proved by Euler in 1737) and **transcendental** (proved by Hermite in 1873). This means e cannot be expressed as a fraction or as a root of any polynomial with integer coefficients. Solving an Exponential Equation x = 2. Check: 3^(2¬∑2‚ąí1) = 3¬≥ = 27. ‚úď Write 27 as a power of 3. 27 = 3¬≥. Since the bases are equal and b > 0, b ‚Č† 1: the exponents must be equal. Add 1 to both sides. Divide by 2. Solving with Logarithms x = ln(17)/ln(5) ‚Čą 1.760. This can also be written log‚āÖ(17). The bases cannot be matched, so take ln of both sides. Apply ln to both sides. Use the power rule of logs: ln(5^x) = x ¬∑ ln 5. Divide both sides by ln 5. Numerical evaluation. Solving a Logarithmic Equation One solution: x = (3 + ‚ąö17)/2 ‚Čą 3.56. Always check that your solution is in the domain of each logarithm. Use the log product rule on the left. Product rule: ln A + ln B = ln(AB). Since ln is one-to-one: if ln(A) = ln(B) then A = B. Expand the left side. Subtract 4x from both sides. Quadratic formula with a=1, b=‚ąí3, c=‚ąí2. Check domains: we need x+2 > 0, x‚ąí1 > 0, 4x > 0. So x > 1. The negative root (‚Čą ‚ąí0.56) fails this. Compound Interest The balance after 6 years is approximately $8,042.19. Compare to simple interest: 5000 + 5000(0.08)(6) = $7,400 ‚ÄĒ compounding earned an extra $642.19. The compound interest formula: P = principal, r = annual rate, n = compounds per year, t = years. Substitute: P = 5000, r = 0.08, n = 4 (quarterly), t = 6. 0.08/4 = 0.02 and 4√ó6 = 24 compounding periods.  (1.02)^24 ‚Čą 1.60844. Radioactive Half-Life The fossil is approximately 8,679 years old. This is how archaeologists date ancient organic materials. Half-life formula: after t years, the fraction remaining is (1/2)^(t/5730). 35% remaining means N(t)/N‚āÄ = 0.35. Take ln of both sides, apply the power rule. Solve for t. Evaluate: ln(0.35) ‚Čą ‚ąí1.0498, ln(0.5) ‚Čą ‚ąí0.6931. Graph Transformations of Exponentials The graph is a reflected, stretched exponential shifted right 1 and up 4. The horizontal asymptote is y = 4, and the graph approaches it from above as x ‚Üí ‚ąě. Start with the parent function: exponential with base 3. Horizontal shift RIGHT by 1 unit (x ‚ąí 1 inside the exponent). Vertical stretch by factor 2 (multiply by 2). Reflect across the x-axis (multiply by ‚ąí1). The function now DECREASES. Vertical shift UP by 4. The horizontal asymptote moves from y = 0 to y = 4. Using Log Properties to Simplify The expression simplifies to exactly 3. Logarithm properties often lead to dramatic simplifications ‚ÄĒ always look for cancellations. Apply log rules one at a time. ln(e‚ĀŅ) = n, so ln(e¬≥) = 3. Power rule: 2ln(5) = ln(5¬≤). 5¬≤ = 25. The ln(25) terms cancel. \text{Evaluate without a calculator: } \log_2(32), \quad \log_3(81), \quad \ln(e^5). A population P of bacteria starts at 1000 and doubles every 2 hours. Write an equation for P(t) where t is in hours, then find when P = 10{,}000. \text{Use the compound interest formula to show that } \lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n \text{ is at least 2 and at most 3, using the binomial theorem.}

documents.title: Introduction to Limits
documents.subtitle: Getting arbitrarily close without arriving
documents.tags: limits approaching informal one-sided two-sided DNE indeterminate form 0/0
documents.content: Imagine walking toward a wall, cutting the remaining distance in half each step. You get closer and closer ‚ÄĒ 1 m, 0.5 m, 0.25 m, 0.125 m ‚ÄĒ but you never quite touch it. A **limit** describes the value a function approaches as its input gets close to some target value. The key insight: the function does not need to be defined *at* the target ‚ÄĒ only *near* it. Consider the function f(x) = (x¬≤ ‚ąí 4)/(x ‚ąí 2). At x = 2, we get 0/0, which is completely undefined. But watch what happens when x gets close to 2 from either side: **From the left (x ‚Üí 2‚ĀĽ):**  x = 1.9 ‚Üí f = 3.9,  x = 1.99 ‚Üí f = 3.99,  x = 1.999 ‚Üí f = 3.999 **From the right (x ‚Üí 2‚Āļ):**  x = 2.1 ‚Üí f = 4.1,  x = 2.01 ‚Üí f = 4.01,  x = 2.001 ‚Üí f = 4.001 From both sides, f(x) is approaching 4. We write: lim(x‚Üí2) (x¬≤‚ąí4)/(x‚ąí2) = 4. The limit is 4, even though f(2) is undefined. Why can we simplify (x¬≤‚ąí4)/(x‚ąí2)? Because x¬≤‚ąí4 = (x+2)(x‚ąí2), and for x ‚Č† 2 we can cancel: (x+2)(x‚ąí2)/(x‚ąí2) = x+2. At x = 2 this gives 4. The limit captured the "natural" value of this function at the hole. **The limit is about the journey, not the destination.** Whether f(2) is undefined, or defined but equal to something else ‚ÄĒ the limit doesn't care. It only asks: what value does f(x) approach as x gets close to 2? There is also the possibility of **one-sided limits**: the function might approach a different value from the left than from the right. If that happens, the two-sided limit does not exist (DNE). Think of a cliff edge: approaching from land gives height H, approaching from sea gives height 0. The "height" limit at the cliff edge DNE. The formal limit notation is: lim(x‚Üíc) f(x) = L. **One-sided limits**: We distinguish approaching from the left versus the right: ‚ÄĘ **Left-hand limit**: lim(x‚Üíc‚ĀĽ) f(x) = L‚āč means x approaches c while staying strictly less than c (x < c). ‚ÄĘ **Right-hand limit**: lim(x‚Üíc‚Āļ) f(x) = L‚āä means x approaches c while staying strictly greater than c (x > c). The two-sided limit exists if and only if both one-sided limits exist AND they are equal. Written precisely: lim(x‚Üíc) f(x) = L  ‚üļ  lim(x‚Üíc‚ĀĽ) f(x) = L = lim(x‚Üíc‚Āļ) f(x) When the one-sided limits are unequal, or when either fails to exist, we say the limit **Does Not Exist (DNE)**. **Three techniques for evaluating limits** when direct substitution fails (giving 0/0 or similar): 1. **Factoring and canceling**: factor the numerator and/or denominator to cancel the problem term. The most common technique for rational functions. 2. **Rationalizing** (multiplying by conjugate): used when a square root appears in a numerator or denominator. 3. **The squeeze theorem**: used when the function is bounded between two simpler functions that share the same limit. **Limits at infinity**: lim(x‚Üí‚ąě) f(x) = L means f(x) approaches L as x grows without bound. This describes **horizontal asymptotes**. The key technique: for rational functions, divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of x in the denominator. The informal idea "f(x) gets close to L as x gets close to c" is made mathematically precise by the **epsilon-delta definition**, developed by Augustin-Louis Cauchy and Karl Weierstrass in the 19th century. Before this definition, calculus was powerful but logically shaky ‚ÄĒ mathematicians could compute correctly but couldn't fully justify why. The definition uses two small positive numbers: **őĶ (epsilon)**: a small tolerance on the *output*. We specify how close f(x) must be to L. **őī (delta)**: a small tolerance on the *input*. This is what we choose to guarantee the output tolerance. The game works like this: an adversary picks any őĶ > 0 (no matter how tiny). You must respond with a őī > 0 such that: whenever x is within őī of c (but x ‚Č† c), f(x) is within őĶ of L. If you can always win this game ‚ÄĒ for every possible őĶ ‚ÄĒ then the limit is L. The condition |x ‚ąí c| < őī (with x ‚Č† c, written 0 < |x ‚ąí c| < őī) translates to "x is within őī of c but not equal to c." The condition |f(x) ‚ąí L| < őĶ translates to "f(x) is within őĶ of L." Crucially, őī is allowed to depend on őĶ. For a linear function like f(x) = 2x + 1, if you want |f(x) ‚ąí 5| < őĶ near c = 2, you can set őī = őĶ/2. For nonlinear functions, finding őī requires more algebra ‚ÄĒ but the definition guarantees one exists whenever the limit truly holds. When Direct Substitution Works The limit is 5/6. Direct substitution works whenever the denominator is nonzero at the target point ‚ÄĒ which is exactly the definition of the function being continuous there. Check: is the denominator zero at x = 4? x + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 ‚Č† 0. Direct substitution is valid. Substitute x = 4 everywhere. Arithmetic in numerator: 16 ‚ąí 12 = 4, then 4 + 1 = 5.  Evaluating a 0/0 Form by Factoring The limit is 6. The original function has a "hole" at x = 3 (it's undefined there), but the limit fills in that hole: the function wants to be 6 at x = 3, even though it isn't defined there. Try direct substitution: numerator = 9‚ąí9 = 0, denominator = 3‚ąí3 = 0. This is the indeterminate form 0/0. We cannot divide by zero ‚ÄĒ but we can factor. Factor numerator: x¬≤‚ąí9 = (x‚ąí3)(x+3). Recognize this as a difference of squares: a¬≤‚ąíb¬≤ = (a‚ąíb)(a+b) with a=x, b=3. Cancel (x‚ąí3). This cancellation is VALID because in a limit, x ‚Üí 3 but x ‚Č† 3, so (x‚ąí3) ‚Č† 0 and we can divide by it. Now substitute x = 3. No problem ‚ÄĒ the simplified function x+3 is defined there. Evaluating a 0/0 Form by Rationalizing The limit is 1/4. The conjugate technique is the go-to method whenever you have (‚ąösomething ‚ąí constant) or (‚ąösomething + constant) in the numerator or denominator. Direct sub gives 0/0: numerator = ‚ąö4 ‚ąí 2 = 0, denominator = 0. Factoring won't help here. Strategy: multiply by the conjugate to eliminate the square root. Multiply top and bottom by the conjugate of the numerator: (‚ąö(x+4) + 2). This equals 1, so we haven't changed the expression. Difference of squares pattern: (a‚ąíb)(a+b) = a¬≤‚ąíb¬≤ with a = ‚ąö(x+4), b = 2. The square root disappears! (‚ąö(x+4))¬≤ = x+4 and 2¬≤ = 4. Numerator is now just x. Cancel x (valid since x ‚Č† 0 in the limit). Now substitute x = 0 safely. One-Sided Limits from a Piecewise Function The limit exists (= 3), but f is not continuous because f(2) = 5 ‚Č† 3. This is a "removable" discontinuity ‚ÄĒ redefining f(2) = 3 would make it continuous. Approaching from the left means x < 2, so use the first piece: f(x) = x + 1.  Approaching from the right means x > 2, so use the third piece: f(x) = x¬≤ ‚ąí 1.  Both one-sided limits are equal. Two-sided limit exists and equals 3. The function value at x = 2 is explicitly defined as 5. The limit (3) does not equal the function value (5). Continuity requires that the limit equals the function value. Here they differ. A Limit That Does Not Exist ‚ÄĒ Jump This function is the "sign function" ‚ÄĒ it equals +1 for positive x and ‚ąí1 for negative x. It has a jump discontinuity at x = 0, which is why the limit fails to exist. The absolute value function has different formulas depending on the sign of x. For x < 0 (approaching from the left), |x| = ‚ąíx. ‚ąíx/x = ‚ąí1 for all x ‚Č† 0. For x > 0 (approaching from the right), |x| = x. x/x = 1 for all x ‚Č† 0. The two one-sided limits are different. Since the one-sided limits disagree, the two-sided limit does not exist. Limit at Infinity ‚ÄĒ Horizontal Asymptote The limit is 3/5. This means the function has a horizontal asymptote at y = 3/5. Quick rule for rational functions: when the degrees are equal, the limit at ¬Ī‚ąě is the ratio of the leading coefficients. Both numerator and denominator grow without bound ‚ÄĒ this is the indeterminate form ‚ąě/‚ąě. Strategy: divide every term by the highest power of x in the denominator, which is x¬≤. Divide every term (numerator and denominator) by x¬≤. Simplify each fraction. Terms with x in the denominator vanish as x grows.  An Epsilon-Delta Proof The scratchwork shows you what őī to choose. The formal proof then chains the inequalities to verify the choice works. For any tolerance őĶ in the output, choosing őī = őĶ/5 guarantees f(x) lands within őĶ of 8. We must prove: for every őĶ > 0, we can find őī > 0 such that 0 < |x‚ąí3| < őī implies |(5x‚ąí7) ‚ąí 8| < őĶ. Before writing the proof, figure out what őī should be. |5x ‚ąí 15| = |5(x‚ąí3)| = 5|x‚ąí3|. The output error is exactly 5 times the input error. So choosing őī = őĶ/5 will work. This is the start of the formal proof. Assume x is within őī of 3 but x ‚Č† 3. Chain the inequalities: output error = 5 √ó input error < 5őī = őĶ. QED. Average Velocity ‚Üí Instantaneous Velocity (Physics) The instantaneous velocity at t=1 is ‚ąí32 ft/s (the ball is falling at 32 ft/s). The negative sign means the height is decreasing ‚ÄĒ the ball is moving downward. This is precisely the derivative h'(1) = ‚ąí32t|_{t=1} = ‚ąí32, which we'll compute using rules in Chapter 2. The limit is the engine that converts "average over an interval" into "instantaneous at a point." Average velocity = change in height √∑ change in time. This is the difference quotient ‚ÄĒ the slope of the secant line. Height at t=1 second. Expand (1+őĒt)¬≤. Distribute and simplify: 80 - 16 = 64. Subtract h(1)=64 and form the quotient. Factor őĒt from numerator and cancel (valid for őĒt ‚Č† 0). As őĒt ‚Üí 0, the term ‚ąí16őĒt ‚Üí 0. The instantaneous velocity at t=1 is exactly ‚ąí32 ft/s. Numerical check: the average velocities converge to ‚ąí32 ft/s from above. Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 1} \frac{x^3 - 1}{x^2 - 1}. Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to \infty} \left(\sqrt{x^2 + x} - x\right). Use the epsilon-delta definition to prove \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 2} x^2 = 4.

documents.title: Limit Laws
documents.subtitle: Algebraic rules so you don't need tables every time
documents.tags: limit laws sum rule product rule quotient rule squeeze theorem sandwich theorem direct substitution sin(x)/x
documents.content: The limit laws capture an intuitive idea: if two functions are each settling down to their respective limits, then their combinations (sum, product, etc.) should settle to the corresponding combination of those limits. Think about it physically: if the temperature at location A approaches 20¬įC and the temperature at location B approaches 30¬įC, then the average of the two temperatures approaches (20 + 30)/2 = 25¬įC. No surprise. The Sum Law and Constant Multiple Law say the same thing for limits. The **Squeeze Theorem** (also called the Sandwich Theorem) is a different kind of tool. Sometimes a function is too complicated to evaluate directly, but you can pin it between two simpler functions that both approach the same value. Like a hot dog in a bun ‚ÄĒ the hot dog is forced to stay where the bun takes it. The canonical example: f(x) = x¬∑sin(1/x). As x ‚Üí 0, sin(1/x) oscillates infinitely rapidly ‚ÄĒ it doesn't have a limit on its own. But |sin(anything)| ‚Č§ 1, so ‚ąí|x| ‚Č§ x¬∑sin(1/x) ‚Č§ |x|. Both ‚ąí|x| and |x| approach 0, so x¬∑sin(1/x) is squeezed to 0. The most important limits in calculus are the two "fundamental trig limits": lim(x‚Üí0) sin(x)/x = 1 and lim(x‚Üí0) (1‚ąícos x)/x = 0. These cannot be proved by algebra alone ‚ÄĒ they require the Squeeze Theorem plus a geometric argument about areas on the unit circle. They come up in every derivative of a trig function. Assume lim(x‚Üíc) f(x) = L and lim(x‚Üíc) g(x) = M. Then the limit laws say we can compute limits of combinations. **Direct Substitution Property**: if p(x) is a polynomial, lim(x‚Üíc) p(x) = p(c). If r(x) = p(x)/q(x) is a rational function and q(c) ‚Č† 0, then lim(x‚Üíc) r(x) = r(c). This also extends to trig, exponential, and logarithmic functions at points where they are defined. **The two fundamental trig limits** cannot be derived from the laws above ‚ÄĒ they require a geometric proof or the definition of the derivative. They are: ‚ÄĘ lim(x‚Üí0) sin(x)/x = 1 ‚ÄĘ lim(x‚Üí0) (1 ‚ąí cos x)/x = 0 These appear constantly. A useful technique: whenever you see sin(ax)/x or sin(ax)/(bx), force the pattern sin(u)/u by multiplying and dividing by a: lim(x‚Üí0) sin(5x)/x = lim(x‚Üí0) [sin(5x)/(5x)] ¬∑ 5 = 1 ¬∑ 5 = 5 **Limits of composite functions**: if lim(x‚Üíc) g(x) = M and f is continuous at M, then lim(x‚Üíc) f(g(x)) = f(M). This is why lim(x‚Üí0) ‚ąö(x¬≤+1) = ‚ąö(0+1) = 1 ‚ÄĒ the square root function is continuous at 1. Each limit law is a provable theorem. The proofs all follow the same template: assume the limits of f and g exist, invoke the definitions to get őī‚āĀ and őī‚āā for each, then combine them (usually taking őī = min(őī‚āĀ, őī‚āā)). The proof of the Sum Law (given below) uses the **triangle inequality** |a + b| ‚Č§ |a| + |b|. This is the fundamental tool in analysis ‚ÄĒ it lets you break one error estimate into the sum of two smaller ones. The Squeeze Theorem proof is especially clean: if g(x) ‚Č§ f(x) ‚Č§ h(x) and both g and h are within őĶ of L, then g(x) > L ‚ąí őĶ and h(x) < L + őĶ, which forces L ‚ąí őĶ < f(x) < L + őĶ, i.e., |f(x) ‚ąí L| < őĶ. The proof that lim(x‚Üí0) sin(x)/x = 1 uses a geometric argument: comparing the areas of two triangles and a circular sector on the unit circle. This gives the inequality cos(x) < sin(x)/x < 1 for 0 < x < ŌÄ/2, and the Squeeze Theorem finishes it. Using Direct Substitution and Limit Laws 11/7. Since the denominator is nonzero, this is simply function evaluation. Check denominator: x + 5 = 7 ‚Č† 0 at x = 2. Direct substitution is valid. Substitute x = 2 in both numerator and denominator.  The Squeeze Theorem in Action The limit is 0. The key was recognizing that the complicated oscillating part (sin(1/x)) is bounded, and the bounding function (x¬≤) controls the overall limit. sin(1/x) is undefined at x = 0, and oscillates infinitely rapidly near 0. We cannot substitute or factor. The sine function is always between ‚ąí1 and 1, regardless of its argument. Multiply all three parts by x¬≤ ‚Č• 0. Multiplying by a non-negative number preserves inequalities. Both bounding functions approach 0 (direct substitution for polynomials). By the Squeeze Theorem: our function is sandwiched between ‚ąíx¬≤ and x¬≤, both ‚Üí 0, so it is squeezed to 0. Using the Fundamental Limit sin(x)/x (a) 5, (b) 3/7. General pattern: lim(x‚Üí0) sin(ax)/(bx) = a/b. Direct substitution: 0/0. The fundamental limit is sin(u)/u = 1, so we need to create the pattern sin(5x)/(5x). Multiply and divide by 5 (multiply by 1 = 5/5). Factor out 5. Let u = 5x. As x ‚Üí 0, u ‚Üí 0. So lim sin(5x)/(5x) = lim sin(u)/u = 1.  Divide top and bottom by x to create two copies of the pattern. Divide numerator and denominator by x. Factor to create sin(3x)/(3x) and sin(7x)/(7x). Both ‚Üí 1 as x ‚Üí 0. Using the Second Fundamental Trig Limit 1/2. This limit appears when computing d/dx[cos x] from the definition of the derivative. 0/0 form. Strategy: multiply by (1+cos x)/(1+cos x) to use the Pythagorean identity.  Numerator: (1‚ąícos x)(1+cos x) = 1 ‚ąí cos¬≤x. 1 ‚ąí cos¬≤x = sin¬≤x (Pythagorean identity). Split into recognizable pieces. sin(x)/x ‚Üí 1, and cos(0) = 1 by direct substitution. Limit of a Composite Function cos(1) ‚Čą 0.5403. Rule: for a continuous outer function, lim f(g(x)) = f(lim g(x)) ‚ÄĒ you can pull continuous functions out of limits. This is a composition: cos applied to sin(x)/x.  The fundamental trig limit. Since cos is continuous everywhere, we can "pass the limit through" the outer function.  Numerical value for reference. Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\tan x}{x}. Prove the Product Law: if \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L and \lim_{x \to c} g(x) = M, then \lim_{x \to c} f(x)g(x) = LM.

documents.title: Continuity
documents.subtitle: Functions you can draw without lifting your pencil
documents.tags: continuity continuous discontinuity removable jump infinite IVT intermediate value theorem
documents.content: A function is **continuous at a point c** if three things are all true: (1) f(c) is defined, (2) the limit exists at c, and (3) the limit equals f(c). Intuitively: no holes, no jumps, no vertical asymptotes at x = c. Continuity is local: a function can be continuous at one point and discontinuous at another. Always ask: continuous where? There are three types of discontinuity: **Removable discontinuity** (hole): the limit exists, but either f(c) is undefined or f(c) ‚Č† limit. Example: f(x) = (x¬≤‚ąí1)/(x‚ąí1) has a hole at x = 1. Fixable by redefining f(1) = 2. **Jump discontinuity**: the left and right limits both exist but are different. The function "jumps." Example: the floor function ‚Ćäx‚Ćč at every integer. **Infinite discontinuity**: the function blows up (goes to ¬Ī‚ąě). Example: 1/x at x = 0. A fourth common behavior to watch: oscillatory non-limit behavior, such as sin(1/x) near x = 0. The function keeps oscillating and never settles, so continuity fails because the limit does not exist. Continuity does not mean smoothness. The function |x| is continuous at 0 but has a sharp corner, so it is not differentiable there. Differentiability is a stronger condition than continuity. A function is **continuous on an interval** if it's continuous at every point of that interval. Polynomials, trig functions, and exponentials are continuous everywhere on their domains. The formal definition uses limits. Continuity is preserved under arithmetic operations: sums, products, quotients (if denominator ‚Č† 0), and compositions of continuous functions are continuous. The **Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)** is one of the most useful theorems in all of calculus: A function is called **uniformly continuous** on an interval if the őī in the őĶ-őī definition can be chosen to work for all points simultaneously (not just near one point). The **Extreme Value Theorem**: if f is continuous on a closed interval [a,b], then f attains its maximum and minimum values somewhere on [a,b]. These theorems (IVT, EVT) are consequences of the completeness of ‚ĄĚ ‚ÄĒ they fail for rational numbers. Checking Continuity at a Point Not continuous at x = 2 ‚ÄĒ removable discontinuity. Define f(2) = 4 to make it continuous. Substitute x = 2: (4‚ąí4)/(2‚ąí2) = 0/0. Undefined. ‚úó Factor and cancel: (x¬≤‚ąí4)/(x‚ąí2) = (x+2)(x‚ąí2)/(x‚ąí2) = x+2 for x ‚Č† 2. The limit exists and equals 4. Condition (1) fails. The limit exists ‚ÄĒ we just have a missing point. Fixable by defining f(2) = 4. Applying the Intermediate Value Theorem The equation has at least one root in (1, 2). IVT guarantees existence ‚ÄĒ it doesn't find the root explicitly. Define f. It is a polynomial, hence continuous everywhere. Evaluate at x = 1. Evaluate at x = 2. f changes sign on [1, 2]. The value 0 is between f(1) and f(2). f is continuous on [1,2] and 0 is between f(1) = ‚ąí1 and f(2) = 29. Making a Piecewise Function Continuous k = 5/3 makes the function continuous at x = 2. Check: left limit = 4(5/3)‚ąí3 = 20/3‚ąí9/3 = 11/3. Right limit = 2 + 5/3 = 11/3. ‚úď Left-hand limit: use the first piece. Right-hand limit: use the second piece. Function value at x = 2 (use piece defined for x ‚Č§ 2). Set left limit = right limit = f(2). Solve for k. Physics: Is There a Jump in Velocity When a Parachute Opens? In this model the velocity has a jump discontinuity at deployment time. Real systems usually smooth this transition over a short interval, but piecewise models often include intentional jumps to represent abrupt regime changes. Compute the left-hand limit from the pre-deployment model. Compute the right-hand limit from the post-deployment model. Left and right limits disagree, so the two-sided limit does not exist. The model predicts a jump in velocity at deployment. Use IVT to prove that every cubic polynomial has at least one real root.

documents.title: The Epsilon-Delta Definition
documents.subtitle: Making "close to" mathematically airtight
documents.tags: epsilon-delta őĶ-őī formal definition proof technique Cauchy Weierstrass rigor
documents.content: Imagine you're playing a game against a skeptic. You claim that lim(x‚Üí3) (2x+1) = 7. The skeptic doesn't believe you. So you agree on rules: **Round 1**: The skeptic says "I bet f(x) isn't always within 0.5 of 7." You respond: "Keep x within 0.25 of 3, and I guarantee |f(x) ‚ąí 7| < 0.5." You're right: if |x‚ąí3| < 0.25, then |2x+1‚ąí7| = 2|x‚ąí3| < 2(0.25) = 0.5. ‚úď **Round 2**: The skeptic tightens: "Within 0.01 of 7." You respond: "Keep x within 0.005 of 3." Check: 2(0.005) = 0.01. ‚úď **Round 3**: "Within 0.0001 of 7." You: "Within 0.00005 of 3." Check: 2(0.00005) = 0.0001. ‚úď The skeptic can pick ANY positive number őĶ (the output tolerance), no matter how tiny. You must respond with a őī (the input tolerance) that guarantees every x within őī of 3 has f(x) within őĶ of 7. If you can ALWAYS win ‚ÄĒ for EVERY possible őĶ ‚ÄĒ then the limit is truly 7. For this linear function, the winning strategy is simple: set őī = őĶ/2. For other functions, finding őī is harder ‚ÄĒ that's where the algebra comes in. But the game is always the same. The beautiful thing about this definition: it never mentions infinity, it never mentions "approaching," and it never mentions motion or time. It replaces all of those fuzzy ideas with a single, airtight logical statement about numbers. This is what mathematician Morris Kline called "the arithmetization of analysis" ‚ÄĒ rebuilding all of calculus on nothing more than the properties of real numbers. The formal definition replaces "f(x) approaches L" with a precise inequality statement. No motion, no time, no infinity ‚ÄĒ just numbers. The key symbols: ‚ąÄ means "for all" (or "for every"), ‚ąÉ means "there exists" (at least one), and ‚üĻ means "implies" (if ... then ...). The definition reads: lim(x‚Üíc) f(x) = L if and only if: for every őĶ > 0, there exists őī > 0 such that whenever 0 < |x ‚ąí c| < őī, we have |f(x) ‚ąí L| < őĶ. The condition 0 < |x ‚ąí c| means x ‚Č† c ‚ÄĒ we explicitly exclude the point c itself. The limit only cares about what happens NEAR c, not AT c. This is why limits can exist at holes, jumps, and even undefined points. The **order of quantifiers matters critically**: first ‚ąÄőĶ, then ‚ąÉőī. This means őī is allowed to depend on őĶ (and it usually does ‚ÄĒ smaller őĶ generally requires smaller őī). If the order were reversed (‚ąÉőī such that ‚ąÄőĶ...), a single őī would need to work for every őĶ simultaneously, which is almost never possible. **One-sided őĶ-őī definitions**: For the left-hand limit lim(x‚Üíc‚ĀĽ) f(x) = L, replace 0 < |x‚ąíc| < őī with c‚ąíőī < x < c (x approaches from below only). For the right-hand limit, use c < x < c+őī. **How to construct an őĶ-őī proof** ‚ÄĒ the two-phase strategy: PHASE 1 (Scratch work): Start from |f(x) ‚ąí L| and simplify algebraically. Express it in terms of |x ‚ąí c|. Find what őī needs to be (in terms of őĶ). PHASE 2 (Formal proof): "Given őĶ > 0, let őī = [your expression]. Suppose 0 < |x‚ąíc| < őī. Then... [chain of inequalities]... < őĶ. ‚ąé" The scratch work is where you figure out the answer. The formal proof is where you verify it. Students get confused because textbooks only show Phase 2 ‚ÄĒ the polished proof ‚ÄĒ hiding the discovery process. Let us prove the Sum Law using őĶ-őī, to see how these proofs work at the foundational level. CLAIM: If lim(x‚Üíc) f(x) = L and lim(x‚Üíc) g(x) = M, then lim(x‚Üíc) [f(x)+g(x)] = L+M. PROOF: Given őĶ > 0. We need |(f(x)+g(x)) ‚ąí (L+M)| < őĶ. By the triangle inequality: |(f+g) ‚ąí (L+M)| = |(f‚ąíL) + (g‚ąíM)| ‚Č§ |f‚ąíL| + |g‚ąíM|. So if we can make each term less than őĶ/2, the sum will be less than őĶ. Since lim f = L: ‚ąÉőī‚āĀ > 0 such that 0 < |x‚ąíc| < őī‚āĀ ‚üĻ |f(x)‚ąíL| < őĶ/2. Since lim g = M: ‚ąÉőī‚āā > 0 such that 0 < |x‚ąíc| < őī‚āā ‚üĻ |g(x)‚ąíM| < őĶ/2. Let őī = min(őī‚āĀ, őī‚āā). Then 0 < |x‚ąíc| < őī implies BOTH conditions hold simultaneously: |(f+g) ‚ąí (L+M)| ‚Č§ |f‚ąíL| + |g‚ąíM| < őĶ/2 + őĶ/2 = őĶ. ‚ąé This proof illustrates several key techniques: (1) the **triangle inequality** |a+b| ‚Č§ |a|+|b| to split the error; (2) the **őĶ/2 trick** ‚ÄĒ budget half the tolerance for each term; (3) **taking the minimum** of multiple őī values so all conditions hold simultaneously. These three tools appear in nearly every őĶ-őī proof. Historically, this level of rigor was developed because of actual mathematical crises. In the 1800s, mathematicians discovered continuous functions that are nowhere differentiable (Weierstrass, 1872), convergent series of continuous functions with discontinuous limits (Cauchy got this wrong!), and space-filling curves (Peano, 1890). These "pathological" examples showed that geometric intuition could not be trusted ‚ÄĒ only rigorous definitions could prevent errors. őĶ-őī Proof for a Linear Function (Scratchwork + Proof) For linear functions f(x) = mx + b with m ‚Č† 0, őī = őĶ/|m| always works. The proof is essentially one line: |f(x)‚ąíL| = |m|¬∑|x‚ąíc| < |m|¬∑őī = őĶ. The scaling factor is the slope. Figure out what őī should be before writing the formal proof. Simplify the output error. It factors as 3 times the input error. So őī = őĶ/3 should work. Now write the formal proof.  State your choice of őī upfront. Assume x is within őī of 4 (but x ‚Č† 4). The chain: output error = 3¬∑(input error) < 3¬∑őī = 3¬∑(őĶ/3) = őĶ. őĶ-őī Proof for a Quadratic (The min Trick) The min(1, őĶ/7) trick is the standard technique for nonlinear őĶ-őī proofs: restrict őī once to bound variable factors, then solve. This method works for any polynomial ‚ÄĒ the only change is the specific bound M for the variable factor. Factor the output error using the difference of squares. Unlike the linear case, we can't immediately solve for őī. By keeping x within 1 of 3, we bound the variable factor. Now |x+3| has a constant upper bound on this interval. Substitute the bound.  The min ensures BOTH conditions: őī ‚Č§ 1 (so the bound holds) AND őī ‚Č§ őĶ/7 (so the product is small).   őĶ-őī Proof for 1/x (Bounding Away from Zero) For rational functions, the extra challenge is keeping the denominator away from zero. The first restriction (őī ‚Č§ 1) solves this. Once you have a lower bound on |x|, the rest follows the same pattern. Combine fractions and simplify. The output error involves 1/|x|, which blows up near 0. This keeps x away from 0, preventing 1/|x| from exploding. On (1,3), the factor 1/|x| is bounded by 1.      Using őĶ-őī to Prove a Limit Does NOT Exist The function oscillates between ‚ąí1 and +1 infinitely often near x=0, so no single value L can satisfy the őĶ-őī condition. This is the formal version of "the function has no settling point." The key technique: finding two sequences approaching 0 whose function values disagree by more than 2őĶ. Try to derive a contradiction from the őĶ-őī definition. If the limit existed, we could satisfy this tolerance. For large enough n, x‚āĀ is within őī of 0. x‚āā is also within őī of 0 (for large n). From the őĶ-őī condition applied to x‚āĀ. From the őĶ-őī condition applied to x‚āā. Contradiction. No single L can be within 1/2 of both 1 and ‚ąí1. Physics: Precision Measurement as an őĶ-őī Problem The voltage must be controlled within ¬Ī1 mV of 10 mV to guarantee the temperature reading is within ¬Ī0.5¬įC. This is a real engineering őĶ-őī problem: given an output tolerance, find the required input precision. Every calibration procedure in science and manufacturing follows this pattern. The output error: how far is the reading from the true value? Simplify. Factor: x¬≤‚ąí30x+200 = (x‚ąí10)(x‚ąí20). The error is proportional to |x‚ąí10| (the input deviation). Bound the variable factor |x‚ąí20|.    The restriction őī ‚Č§ 1 is the binding constraint. Use őĶ-őī to prove \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 5} (4x + 1) = 21. Prove \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 1} (x^2 + 2x) = 3 using őĶ-őī. Prove \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 4} \sqrt{x} = 2 using őĶ-őī.

documents.title: The Squeeze Theorem
documents.subtitle: Trapping a wild function between two tame ones
documents.tags: squeeze theorem sandwich theorem pinching theorem sin(x)/x bounded oscillation comparison
documents.content: Picture a hot dog inside a bun. As you carry the bun to your mouth, the hot dog comes along for the ride ‚ÄĒ it has no choice. It doesn't matter if the hot dog is wiggling, spinning, or vibrating inside the bun. If the top and bottom of the bun both arrive at the same destination, the hot dog arrives there too. Mathematically: suppose g(x) ‚Č§ f(x) ‚Č§ h(x) for all x near c (except possibly at c itself). If both g(x) ‚Üí L and h(x) ‚Üí L as x ‚Üí c, then f(x) ‚Üí L as well. The function f is "squeezed" between g and h. The theorem is most powerful when f(x) is too complicated to evaluate directly. Perhaps it oscillates (like sin(1/x)), or has no closed-form simplification. But if you can find simpler functions g and h that bound f from below and above, and both converge to the same limit, you're done. **The standard pattern**: if q(x) is bounded (|q(x)| ‚Č§ M for some constant M) and p(x) ‚Üí 0, then p(x)¬∑q(x) ‚Üí 0. This is because ‚ąíM¬∑|p(x)| ‚Č§ p(x)¬∑q(x) ‚Č§ M¬∑|p(x)|, and both bounds ‚Üí 0. This handles cases like x¬∑sin(1/x), x¬≤¬∑cos(1/x), and ‚ąöx¬∑sin(1/x). Harold Jacobs likened this to a child walking between two parents who both turn into a doorway: the child enters the doorway too, no matter how much they zigzag. The key insight is that the child's freedom shrinks to zero ‚ÄĒ the two boundaries converge to the same point, leaving no room for anything else. The formal statement uses the concepts from the őĶ-őī lesson: **Squeeze Theorem**: Suppose g(x) ‚Č§ f(x) ‚Č§ h(x) for all x in some open interval containing c (except possibly at c itself). If lim(x‚Üíc) g(x) = L and lim(x‚Üíc) h(x) = L, then lim(x‚Üíc) f(x) = L. **Proof using őĶ-őī**: Given őĶ > 0. Since lim g(x) = L, ‚ąÉőī‚āĀ: 0 < |x‚ąíc| < őī‚āĀ ‚üĻ |g(x)‚ąíL| < őĶ, which means L‚ąíőĶ < g(x) < L+őĶ. Since lim h(x) = L, ‚ąÉőī‚āā: 0 < |x‚ąíc| < őī‚āā ‚üĻ L‚ąíőĶ < h(x) < L+őĶ. Let őī = min(őī‚āĀ, őī‚āā). Then for 0 < |x‚ąíc| < őī: L‚ąíőĶ < g(x) ‚Č§ f(x) ‚Č§ h(x) < L+őĶ, so |f(x)‚ąíL| < őĶ. ‚ąé This proof is elegant: the squeeze hypothesis g ‚Č§ f ‚Č§ h, combined with g and h being within őĶ of L, forces f to be within őĶ of L. No information about f itself is needed ‚ÄĒ the bounds do all the work. **The most important limit in calculus**: lim(x‚Üí0) sin(x)/x = 1. This cannot be proved by algebra alone (you cannot cancel the x). It requires a geometric argument: On the unit circle with angle 0 < x < ŌÄ/2: the area of the inscribed triangle is (1/2)sin(x). The area of the circular sector is (1/2)x. The area of the circumscribed triangle is (1/2)tan(x). Since the inscribed triangle fits inside the sector, which fits inside the circumscribed triangle: (1/2)sin(x) ‚Č§ (1/2)x ‚Č§ (1/2)tan(x) Dividing by (1/2)sin(x) > 0: 1 ‚Č§ x/sin(x) ‚Č§ 1/cos(x). Inverting (and flipping inequalities): cos(x) ‚Č§ sin(x)/x ‚Č§ 1. As x ‚Üí 0‚Āļ: cos(x) ‚Üí 1 and 1 ‚Üí 1. By the Squeeze Theorem: sin(x)/x ‚Üí 1. Since sin(x)/x is an even function [sin(‚ąíx)/(‚ąíx) = sin(x)/x], the limit from the left also equals 1. ‚ąé This one result unlocks every trig derivative: d/dx[sin x] = cos x, d/dx[cos x] = ‚ąísin x, and all the rest follow from it. The second fundamental trig limit follows from the first: lim(x‚Üí0) (1 ‚ąí cos x)/x = 0. Proof: Multiply numerator and denominator by (1 + cos x): (1‚ąícos x)/x ¬∑ (1+cos x)/(1+cos x) = (1‚ąícos¬≤x)/[x(1+cos x)] = sin¬≤x/[x(1+cos x)] = [sin(x)/x] ¬∑ [sin(x)/(1+cos x)] As x ‚Üí 0: sin(x)/x ‚Üí 1 (by the first fundamental limit), and sin(x)/(1+cos x) ‚Üí 0/2 = 0. Product: 1 ¬∑ 0 = 0. ‚ąé These two limits ‚ÄĒ lim sin(x)/x = 1 and lim (1‚ąícos x)/x = 0 ‚ÄĒ are the twin pillars of trigonometric calculus. Every trig derivative proof requires them. They connect geometry (areas on the unit circle) to algebra (limit computations) through the Squeeze Theorem. This is a beautiful example of what Harold Jacobs called "mathematics as a seamless whole" ‚ÄĒ geometry and analysis working together. Historically, the sin(x)/x limit appears implicitly in Euler's work on infinite series (1748) and was made rigorous by Cauchy (1821). The geometric proof via areas on the unit circle is attributed to several mathematicians; it became standard in calculus textbooks by the late 19th century. Squeezing x¬∑sin(1/x) to 0 Even though sin(1/x) oscillates infinitely rapidly as x ‚Üí 0, the factor x shrinks the oscillation amplitude to zero. The Squeeze Theorem captures this precisely: "bounded times vanishing equals vanishing." The sine function is bounded between ‚ąí1 and 1, always. This is the key observation. Multiply the bound by |x|: since |sin(1/x)| ‚Č§ 1, the product is between ‚ąí|x| and |x|. Both bounds approach 0 as x ‚Üí 0. The function is trapped between two bounds that both converge to 0. Squeezing x¬≤¬∑cos(1/x¬≤) to 0 The quadratic x¬≤ damps the oscillation even faster than x does. In general, x^n ¬∑ (bounded function) ‚Üí 0 for any n > 0. Cosine is bounded, just like sine. Since |cos(1/x¬≤)| ‚Č§ 1 and x¬≤ ‚Č• 0.   The Most Important Limit: sin(x)/x ‚Üí 1 (Full Geometric Proof) lim(x‚Üí0) sin(x)/x = 1. This is arguably the most important limit in calculus. Every derivative of a trig function traces back to it: the proof that d/dx[sin x] = cos x uses this limit in its last step. Without the Squeeze Theorem, we couldn't prove it ‚ÄĒ and without it, we couldn't do trig calculus. We'll compare three areas: inscribed triangle, circular sector, and circumscribed triangle. Base = 1 (radius), height = sin(x). Triangle fits inside the sector. A fraction x/(2ŌÄ) of the full circle area ŌÄ(1)¬≤ = ŌÄ, so area = x/2. Base = 1, height = tan(x). The sector fits inside this larger triangle. The three regions are nested: inscribed ‚äÜ sector ‚äÜ circumscribed. Multiply through by 2. Use tan x = sin x / cos x. Divide everything by sin x (positive for 0 < x < ŌÄ/2). Invert all three expressions (flipping the inequality direction). Both bounds approach 1 from the same side.  sin(x)/x is an even function, so the left-hand limit equals the right-hand limit. Physics: Small-Angle Pendulum (the sin őł ‚Čą őł Approximation) The Squeeze Theorem justifies the physics approximation sin őł ‚Čą őł for small őł: since cos őł ‚Č§ sin(őł)/őł ‚Č§ 1 and cos őł ‚Üí 1, the ratio sin(őł)/őł ‚Üí 1, meaning sin őł and őł become indistinguishable for small angles. The resulting pendulum formula T = 2ŌÄ‚ąö(L/g) is one of the most famous results in physics ‚ÄĒ and it rests entirely on the Squeeze Theorem. This is the inequality we just proved. Multiply through by őł > 0. Compute exact and approximate values. The approximation sin őł ‚Čą őł gives 0.1745. Less than 1% error at 10¬į! The approximation worsens for larger angles. Replace sin őł with őł in the pendulum equation. Simple harmonic motion with period T = 2ŌÄ‚ąö(L/g). Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} x^2\sin\!\left(\frac{1}{x}\right). Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin(5x)}{x}. Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\tan x - \sin x}{x^3}.

documents.title: Limits at Infinity
documents.subtitle: End behavior, asymptotes, and growth-rate dominance
documents.tags: limits at infinity horizontal asymptote oblique asymptote end behavior epsilon-N growth rates
documents.content: A limit at infinity asks what f(x) does as x grows without bound. It does NOT mean plugging in x = infinity; infinity is not a real number you can substitute. Instead, it asks for long-run behavior. For rational functions, the highest powers dominate for large |x|. Lower powers become negligible. This gives the three core cases: numerator degree smaller than denominator degree gives 0; equal degrees gives ratio of leading coefficients; larger numerator degree gives growth without bound or an oblique asymptote after division. A horizontal asymptote y = L means f(x) gets arbitrarily close to L for large positive or negative x. The graph can cross a horizontal asymptote; asymptote means end behavior, not a barrier. An oblique (slant) asymptote appears when deg(numerator) = deg(denominator) + 1. Polynomial long division gives f(x) = mx + b + remainder/denominator, and the remainder term vanishes at infinity, so the graph hugs y = mx + b. Formal definition (epsilon-N): lim(x->infinity) f(x) = L means for every epsilon > 0, there exists N such that x > N implies |f(x) - L| < epsilon. For rational functions p(x)/q(x), divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of x in q(x). Terms like 1/x and 1/x^2 vanish as x -> infinity. Degree rules for rational functions: 1) deg(p) < deg(q): limit is 0. 2) deg(p) = deg(q): limit is ratio of leading coefficients. 3) deg(p) > deg(q): no finite horizontal asymptote; use long division to identify polynomial asymptote behavior. The degree rules come from factoring out the dominant power of x and applying limit laws. For example, if deg(p)=deg(q)=n, then p(x)/q(x) = (a_n + lower terms/x)/(b_n + lower terms/x) -> a_n/b_n. Growth-rate comparisons can be proved with lHopital, series, or inequalities: ln(x)/x^a -> 0 for a>0, and x^a/b^x -> 0 for b>1. These results justify why exponentials dominate polynomials and why polynomials dominate logarithms. Equal Degrees The limit is 3/5, so y = 3/5 is a horizontal asymptote (as x -> infinity). Equal degrees (2 and 2), so expect ratio of leading coefficients. Divide top and bottom by x^2. All 1/x terms vanish. Numerator Degree Smaller When denominator degree is larger, the limit is 0. Degree 1 over degree 2. Divide by x^2. Numerator vanishes, denominator approaches 1. Slant Asymptote by Long Division The oblique asymptote is y = x + 4. Polynomial long division. Remainder term vanishes. The graph approaches the line y = x + 4 for large |x|. Exponential vs Polynomial Growth Exponentials outgrow every power of x. Exponential eventually dominates polynomial growth. Can be shown by repeated lHopital in later chapters. Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to -\infty} \frac{4x^3-1}{2x^3+7x}. Find the horizontal or slant asymptote of f(x)=\frac{2x^2-5x+3}{x+1}.

documents.title: The Derivative ‚ÄĒ From Average to Instantaneous Change
documents.subtitle: How a single limiting process unlocks the instantaneous rate of change of any function
documents.tags: derivative limit definition difference quotient tangent line instantaneous rate of change differentiability notation
documents.content: Start with something we already understand: average rate of change. If you drive 120 miles in 2 hours, your average speed is 60 mph. If a population grows from 1,000 to 1,500 individuals over 5 years, the average growth rate is 100 individuals per year. In each case, we compute (change in output) divided by (change in input). This is the slope of the line connecting two points on the graph ‚ÄĒ called a secant line. Formally, the average rate of change of a function f over the interval from x = a to x = a + h is the slope of the secant line through the points (a, f(a)) and (a+h, f(a+h)). We write this as [f(a+h) - f(a)] / h. This expression has a name: the difference quotient. It is the single most important formula in differential calculus. Now ask: what happens as h gets smaller and smaller? The second point (a+h, f(a+h)) slides along the curve toward (a, f(a)). The secant line that once crossed the curve at two distinct points begins to rotate. In the limit, as h approaches 0, the secant line approaches a unique limiting position ‚ÄĒ the tangent line. The slope of that tangent line is the derivative. Here is why we cannot simply plug in h = 0 directly. At h = 0, the difference quotient becomes [f(a) - f(a)] / 0 = 0/0, which is the indeterminate form from Chapter 1. This is not a number ‚ÄĒ it is a failure of division. But taking the LIMIT as h approaches 0 is something entirely different from evaluating AT h = 0. The limit asks: what value does the expression approach as h gets arbitrarily close to 0? That question has a perfectly well-defined, finite answer for most functions. Think about what the difference quotient measures geometrically. The numerator f(a+h) - f(a) is the vertical rise from one point to the other. The denominator h is the horizontal run. So the ratio is rise over run ‚ÄĒ the slope of the secant line. As h shrinks, the two points get closer and closer together, but the slope of the line between them settles into a limiting value. That limiting slope is the derivative. The derivative tells us two things simultaneously, and they are really the same thing: (1) it is the slope of the curve at the point x = a, meaning the slope of the tangent line to the graph there, and (2) it is the instantaneous rate of change of the function at x = a. If f(t) is position, the derivative is instantaneous velocity. If f(x) is a population, the derivative is the instantaneous growth rate. If f(x) is profit as a function of units sold, the derivative is marginal profit. Mathematicians have invented several notations for the derivative, each with its own strengths. If y = f(x), then the derivative can be written as f'(x) (read "f prime of x", due to Lagrange), or as dy/dx (Leibniz notation, emphasizing the ratio of infinitesimal changes), or as d/dx[f(x)] (operator notation), or as Df(x) (operator notation due to Euler), or even as yŐá (Newton's dot notation, used in physics for time derivatives). All of these mean exactly the same thing: the limit of the difference quotient. Leibniz notation dy/dx is especially useful when doing related rates and chain rule problems, because it behaves somewhat like a fraction (though it is not exactly one). Lagrange notation f'(x) is compact and convenient for most algebraic work. We are now ready to state the formal definition. There are two closely related versions: the derivative at a specific point a, and the derivative as a function of x. The derivative of f at the specific point x = a is the number f'(a) defined by the limit of the difference quotient as h approaches 0. If this limit exists (is a finite real number), we say f is differentiable at a. The notation f'(a) emphasizes that the derivative at a point is a single number ‚ÄĒ the slope at that point. The derivative function f'(x) is obtained by letting a vary. Instead of computing the slope at one specific point, we compute it at every point where the limit exists. The result is a new function whose output at any x is the slope of the original function at that x. There is an alternative but equivalent form of the derivative at a point a, using x directly as the variable that approaches a: the limit as x approaches a of [f(x) - f(a)] / (x - a). This form is sometimes cleaner algebraically ‚ÄĒ you can factor (x - a) out of the numerator. Once we know the derivative f'(a), we can write the equation of the tangent line to the graph of f at the point (a, f(a)). The tangent line passes through (a, f(a)) with slope f'(a), so its equation is y - f(a) = f'(a)(x - a). This is simply the point-slope form of a line. We also define one-sided derivatives, called the left-hand derivative and the right-hand derivative. The left-hand derivative at a is the limit of the difference quotient as h approaches 0 from the negative side (h ‚Üí 0‚ĀĽ). The right-hand derivative is the limit as h ‚Üí 0‚Āļ. The derivative f'(a) exists if and only if both one-sided derivatives exist and are equal. One-sided derivatives are essential for analyzing functions defined by cases or functions with corners. A function is called differentiable on an open interval (c, d) if it is differentiable at every point in that interval. It is differentiable on a closed interval [c, d] if it is differentiable on the open interior (c, d) and both one-sided derivatives exist at the endpoints. There is a profound relationship between differentiability and continuity. Intuitively, if a function has a well-defined tangent slope at a point, it cannot have a jump or hole there ‚ÄĒ a broken function cannot have a smooth tangent. This is made precise by an important theorem. THEOREM (Differentiability Implies Continuity): If f is differentiable at a, then f is continuous at a. PROOF: We want to show that the limit of f(x) as x approaches a equals f(a), or equivalently that the limit of [f(x) - f(a)] as x approaches a is 0. We write f(x) - f(a) = [f(x) - f(a)] / (x - a) times (x - a). The first factor, [f(x)-f(a)]/(x-a), approaches f'(a) as x ‚Üí a (by the alternative definition of the derivative). The second factor, (x - a), approaches 0. By the product law for limits, the product approaches f'(a) ¬∑ 0 = 0. Therefore f(x) - f(a) ‚Üí 0, which means f(x) ‚Üí f(a). This proves continuity. The converse of this theorem is FALSE. A function can be continuous at a point without being differentiable there. The classic example is f(x) = |x| at x = 0. This function is continuous everywhere ‚ÄĒ there are no jumps or holes. But it has a "corner" at x = 0 where the graph changes direction sharply, and at that corner the derivative does not exist. PROOF that |x| is not differentiable at 0: We compute the left and right derivatives. From the right (h ‚Üí 0‚Āļ): [|0+h| - |0|] / h = |h|/h = h/h = 1, so the right-hand derivative is 1. From the left (h ‚Üí 0‚ĀĽ): [|0+h| - |0|] / h = |h|/h = (-h)/h = -1, so the left-hand derivative is -1. Since 1 ‚Č† -1, the two-sided limit does not exist, and f is not differentiable at 0. There are exactly three geometric ways a function can fail to be differentiable at a point, even while remaining continuous there. First: a corner, where the left and right derivatives both exist but are unequal (example: |x| at 0). Second: a cusp, where one or both one-sided derivatives are infinite but with opposite signs, causing the curve to come to a sharp pointed tip (example: x^(2/3) at 0 ‚ÄĒ the tangent line becomes vertical). Third: a vertical tangent, where the difference quotient grows without bound as h ‚Üí 0, meaning the tangent line would be vertical (example: x^(1/3) at 0). In all three cases, the function is continuous but the limit of the difference quotient fails to be a finite real number. A function can also fail to be differentiable at a point of discontinuity, of course ‚ÄĒ but that failure is less subtle, since differentiability requires continuity. Derivative of x¬≤ from the Definition The derivative of x¬≤ is 2x. At x = 3, the curve has slope 6, and the tangent line is y = 6x - 9. Notice the pattern: the exponent 2 came down as a coefficient, and the new exponent is 2 - 1 = 1. This is a preview of the power rule. Write the limit definition of the derivative. We want to find the slope of the tangent to y = x¬≤ at a general point x. Substitute f(x+h) = (x+h)¬≤ and f(x) = x¬≤ into the definition. Expand (x+h)¬≤ using the binomial formula (a+b)¬≤ = a¬≤ + 2ab + b¬≤. This is the key algebraic step. The x¬≤ terms cancel: x¬≤ - x¬≤ = 0. We now have a polynomial in h in the numerator, which allows us to factor. Factor h out of the numerator. This is the crucial factoring step that cancels the problematic h in the denominator. Cancel the common factor of h. This is valid because in a limit as h ‚Üí 0, we require h ‚Č† 0, so division by h is legal. Now we can safely substitute h = 0, since the indeterminate form 0/0 has been resolved by the algebraic simplification. The derivative is 2x. To find the slope at x = 3, substitute x = 3 into the derivative function f'(x) = 2x. Use point-slope form of a line: y - y‚āÄ = m(x - x‚āÄ), with the point (3, 9) and slope m = 6. Distribute the 6 and simplify to get the slope-intercept form of the tangent line. Derivative of x¬≥ from the Definition The derivative of x¬≥ is 3x¬≤. The exponent 3 dropped down as a coefficient, and the new exponent is 3 - 1 = 2. The pattern x¬≤ ‚Üí 2x and x¬≥ ‚Üí 3x¬≤ strongly suggests the general power rule: d/dx[x‚ĀŅ] = nx‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. Write the difference quotient. We need to expand (x+h)¬≥, which requires the full binomial expansion. Expand (x+h)¬≥ using the binomial theorem or repeated multiplication: (x+h)¬≥ = (x+h)(x+h)(x+h). Each term is identified: the leading x¬≥, three terms with one factor of h (giving 3x¬≤h), three terms with two factors of h (giving 3xh¬≤), and one term with three factors of h (giving h¬≥). Substitute the expanded form of (x+h)¬≥ into the difference quotient. Cancel x¬≥ - x¬≥ = 0 in the numerator. We are left with only terms containing h. Factor out h from every term in the numerator. This is possible because every remaining term has at least one factor of h. Cancel the common h. Now the expression is a polynomial in h with no division, so we can directly substitute h = 0. Substitute h = 0. The terms containing h vanish, leaving only 3x¬≤. Derivative of ‚ąöx from the Definition The derivative of ‚ąöx = x^(1/2) is 1/(2‚ąöx) = (1/2)x^(-1/2). This again follows the power rule pattern: the exponent 1/2 came down as a coefficient, and the new exponent is 1/2 - 1 = -1/2. Set up the difference quotient. The numerator is the difference of two square roots, which creates an indeterminate form 0/0 as h ‚Üí 0. Multiply the numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the numerator: ‚ąö(x+h) + ‚ąöx. This is the key technique for dealing with square root differences. Multiplying by the conjugate uses the identity (a - b)(a + b) = a¬≤ - b¬≤.
