Apply the difference of squares identity to the numerator. The conjugate multiplication eliminates the square roots in the numerator. Simplify the numerator: (‚ąö(x+h))¬≤ = x+h and (‚ąöx)¬≤ = x. Simplify (x + h) - x = h in the numerator. We now have h in both numerator and denominator. Cancel the common factor of h (valid since h ‚Č† 0 in the limit). The indeterminate form has been resolved. Now we can substitute h = 0 safely, since there is no longer any division by h. The result is 1/(2‚ąöx). Derivative of 1/x from the Definition The derivative of 1/x = x^(-1) is -1/x¬≤ = -x^(-2). Once again, the power rule pattern: exponent -1 comes down as coefficient, new exponent is -1 - 1 = -2. Write the difference quotient. We have a compound fraction (a fraction within a fraction), which we need to simplify. Rewrite the compound fraction by pulling 1/h out front. Now we need to combine the two fractions in the parentheses. Combine the fractions with a common denominator of x(x+h): the first fraction becomes x/[x(x+h)] and the second becomes (x+h)/[x(x+h)]. Subtract numerators. Distribute the negative sign in the numerator: x - (x + h) = x - x - h = -h. Simplify: x - x - h = -h. Combine into a single fraction. Cancel the common factor of h (valid since h ‚Č† 0 in the limit). The -h/h cancels to give -1. Substitute h = 0. The denominator becomes x¬∑x = x¬≤. Derivative of a Linear Function The derivative of 3x + 5 is 3, which is exactly the slope of the line. This makes perfect sense: a straight line has a constant slope at every point, so its derivative (the instantaneous slope) is that same constant everywhere. The +5 intercept term disappeared because it shifts the line up and down but does not affect its steepness. Write the limit definition. Substitute f(x+h) = 3(x+h) + 5 and f(x) = 3x + 5. Distribute the 3 and expand the brackets. Simplify: 3x - 3x = 0 and 5 - 5 = 0, leaving only 3h in the numerator. Cancel h/h = 1. The resulting expression is the constant 3, which equals 3 regardless of how h approaches 0. Is |x| Differentiable at x = 0? f(x) = |x| is NOT differentiable at x = 0. The graph has a sharp corner there ‚ÄĒ the slope jumps from -1 (for x < 0) to +1 (for x > 0) with no smooth transition. There is no single well-defined tangent line at the origin. This is the canonical example of a function that is continuous but not differentiable. Set up the right-hand derivative: h approaches 0 from the positive side, meaning h > 0 throughout. When h > 0, |h| = h, so |h|/h = h/h = 1. The right-hand derivative is 1. Set up the left-hand derivative: h approaches 0 from the negative side, meaning h < 0 throughout. When h < 0, |h| = -h (the negative of h, which is positive), so |h|/h = -h/h = -1. The left-hand derivative is -1. The right-hand derivative (1) and left-hand derivative (-1) are not equal. Since the one-sided limits are different, the two-sided limit does not exist. Therefore f'(0) does not exist. Instantaneous Velocity from the Definition The instantaneous velocity at t = 2 is 19 m/s. The general velocity function v(t) = 8t + 3 shows that the particle is accelerating ‚ÄĒ its speed increases linearly with time. For comparison, the average velocity over [1, 3] would be [s(3)-s(1)]/(3-1) = [45 - 7]/2 = 19 m/s. This agreement is not a coincidence ‚ÄĒ the midpoint of [1, 3] is t = 2, and for a quadratic position function (uniform acceleration), the average velocity over any interval equals the instantaneous velocity at the midpoint. Instantaneous velocity is the derivative of position with respect to time. Set up the difference quotient. Compute s(t+h) by replacing t with t+h in the position formula. Expand (t+h)¬≤ = t¬≤ + 2th + h¬≤ and distribute the 4 and 3. Distribute to get the fully expanded form of s(t+h). Form the difference s(t+h) - s(t) by subtracting the original position. Simplify: 4t¬≤ - 4t¬≤ = 0 and 3t - 3t = 0. Only the h-containing terms remain. Divide by h to form the difference quotient. Factor h from the numerator and cancel with the denominator h. Take the limit as h ‚Üí 0. The term 4h vanishes, leaving the instantaneous velocity function v(t) = 8t + 3. Substitute t = 2 to find the instantaneous velocity at t = 2 seconds. The particle is moving at exactly 19 meters per second at that moment. Galileo's Falling Ball: Derivative from First Principles (Physics) The velocity v(t) = gt says: velocity grows linearly with time, at rate g = 9.8 m/s per second. This is uniform (constant) acceleration. In the limit definition, the key step is canceling h to remove the 0/0 indeterminate form ‚ÄĒ identical algebra to the tangent line problem. Galileo discovered this law experimentally in 1604; Newton's calculus gave it a mathematical foundation 60 years later. The velocity at time t is the derivative of position ‚ÄĒ defined as the limit of average velocities. Expand (t+h)¬≤. Subtract y(t). The t¬≤ terms cancel. Divide by h (cancel for h ‚Č† 0). Take the limit: h ‚Üí 0 eliminates the h term. The velocity increases linearly with time ‚ÄĒ uniform acceleration. At t=3 s, the ball is falling at 29.4 m/s ‚Čą 106 km/h. Use the limit definition of the derivative to find the equation of the tangent line to f(x) = 2x^2 - 3x at x = 1. Prove from the limit definition that \dfrac{d}{dx}[x^4] = 4x^3. You will need the expansion (x+h)^4 = x^4 + 4x^3h + 6x^2h^2 + 4xh^3 + h^4. Let f(x) = x\sin(1/x) for x \neq 0 and f(0) = 0. Prove that f is differentiable at x = 0 by evaluating the limit definition directly. Use the Squeeze Theorem.

documents.title: Differentiation Rules ‚ÄĒ Power, Product, Quotient
documents.subtitle: Shortcuts that transform hours of limit-computing into seconds of pattern-matching
documents.tags: power rule product rule quotient rule differentiation rules constant rule sum rule polynomial derivatives
documents.content: Look at the pattern from the previous lesson: the derivative of x¬Ļ is 1, the derivative of x¬≤ is 2x, the derivative of x¬≥ is 3x¬≤, and the derivative of x‚Āī is 4x¬≥. In every case, the exponent "drops down" to become the coefficient, and the exponent decreases by one. This pattern is the power rule, and it holds for every real number exponent ‚ÄĒ not just positive integers. Before diving into formulas, let us address a common misconception about the product rule. Students sometimes guess that the derivative of a product f(x)¬∑g(x) should be f'(x)¬∑g'(x) ‚ÄĒ just differentiate each factor. This guess is WRONG. A simple counterexample: take f(x) = x and g(x) = x. Then f(x)¬∑g(x) = x¬≤, whose derivative is 2x. But f'(x)¬∑g'(x) = 1¬∑1 = 1. Since 2x ‚Č† 1 (except at x = 1/2), the naive guess fails. The actual product rule is more subtle. Why does the product rule have the form it does? Think of f(x)¬∑g(x) as the area of a rectangle with side lengths f(x) and g(x). When x changes by a small amount h, both f and g change: f changes by approximately f'(x)¬∑h and g changes by approximately g'(x)¬∑h. The change in the area is the change in the rectangle. The new rectangle has area (f + őĒf)(g + őĒg) = fg + f¬∑őĒg + g¬∑őĒf + őĒf¬∑őĒg. The total change in area is f¬∑őĒg + g¬∑őĒf + őĒf¬∑őĒg. When we divide by h and let h‚Üí0, the őĒf¬∑őĒg term (which is proportional to h¬≤) becomes negligible, and we are left with f¬∑g'(x) + g¬∑f'(x). That is the product rule. The quotient rule can be remembered with the mnemonic: "low d-high minus high d-low, over low squared." Here "high" is the numerator and "low" is the denominator. So if h(x) = f(x)/g(x), then h'(x) = [g(x)¬∑f'(x) - f(x)¬∑g'(x)] / [g(x)]¬≤. The order matters: it is not symmetric like the product rule. Notice the minus sign in the numerator ‚ÄĒ this makes the quotient rule impossible to guess from the product rule without proof. Why does the quotient rule have a minus sign? Think of f/g: if the denominator g increases, the ratio f/g decreases. So an increase in g contributes negatively to the rate of change of the ratio. This negative contribution is captured by the minus sign in front of f(x)¬∑g'(x). The sum and difference rules say that differentiation is linear: you can differentiate term by term. This follows directly from the linearity of limits. The constant multiple rule follows similarly. These two rules together mean: to differentiate a polynomial, just differentiate each term independently. Second and higher derivatives are just what you get by differentiating again. If f'(x) is the derivative of f, then f''(x) is the derivative of f', called the second derivative. It measures the rate of change of the rate of change ‚ÄĒ geometrically, it measures concavity. The notation f''(x) = d¬≤y/dx¬≤ (read "d two y d x squared") looks like a fraction raised to a power but is actually a separate operator applied twice. We now state all five differentiation rules precisely. Each is a theorem ‚ÄĒ a mathematical fact that can be proved and that holds without exception wherever the functions involved are differentiable. The constant rule says the derivative of any constant function is zero. This makes geometric sense: a constant function is a horizontal line with slope 0 everywhere. The power rule is the most-used rule in calculus: d/dx[x‚ĀŅ] = nx‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. This holds for any real number n ‚ÄĒ positive, negative, fractional, or irrational. For the moment we prove it for positive integers; the general case follows from logarithmic differentiation or the generalized binomial theorem. The constant multiple rule lets constants pass through the derivative. If c is a constant, d/dx[cf(x)] = c¬∑f'(x). This follows because constants can be factored out of limits. The sum/difference rules say d/dx[f(x) ¬Ī g(x)] = f'(x) ¬Ī g'(x). Derivatives of sums and differences are computed term by term. The product rule: d/dx[f(x)¬∑g(x)] = f'(x)¬∑g(x) + f(x)¬∑g'(x). In words: the derivative of a product is (derivative of first)(second) plus (first)(derivative of second). The quotient rule: if g(x) ‚Č† 0, then d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)¬∑g(x) - f(x)¬∑g'(x)] / [g(x)]¬≤. Higher-order derivatives are defined by repeated differentiation. The second derivative is f''(x) = d/dx[f'(x)], the third derivative is f'''(x) = d/dx[f''(x)], and so on. For the nth derivative we write fŠīľ‚ĀŅ‚ĀĹ(x) or d‚ĀŅy/dx‚ĀŅ. We prove three of the six rules rigorously from the limit definition. The others follow as corollaries. PROOF OF THE POWER RULE (for positive integers n): We use the algebraic factoring identity: a‚ĀŅ - b‚ĀŅ = (a - b)(a‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ + a‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬≤b + a‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬≥b¬≤ + ... + b‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ), which has n terms in the second factor. Using the alternative form of the derivative definition with x approaching a: d/dx[x‚ĀŅ] = lim(x‚Üía) [x‚ĀŅ - a‚ĀŅ]/(x - a). By the factoring identity, (x‚ĀŅ - a‚ĀŅ)/(x - a) = x‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ + x‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬≤a + x‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬≥a¬≤ + ... + a‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. This sum has exactly n terms. As x ‚Üí a, each term approaches a‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. There are n such terms, so the total limit is n¬∑a‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. Replacing a with x gives d/dx[x‚ĀŅ] = nx‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. Alternatively, using the binomial theorem: (x+h)‚ĀŅ = x‚ĀŅ + nx‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļh + C(n,2)x‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬≤h¬≤ + ... + h‚ĀŅ. When we form the difference quotient [(x+h)‚ĀŅ - x‚ĀŅ]/h, the x‚ĀŅ terms cancel, and every remaining term in the numerator has at least one factor of h. After dividing by h, every term still has at least one factor of h EXCEPT the first term, which gives nx‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. All higher-order terms vanish as h ‚Üí 0. PROOF OF THE PRODUCT RULE: Let p(x) = f(x)¬∑g(x). The difference quotient for p is [f(x+h)g(x+h) - f(x)g(x)] / h. This is not yet in a usable form. The key algebraic trick is to add and subtract f(x+h)g(x) in the numerator: [f(x+h)g(x+h) - f(x+h)g(x) + f(x+h)g(x) - f(x)g(x)] / h. Regroup: f(x+h)¬∑[g(x+h)-g(x)]/h + g(x)¬∑[f(x+h)-f(x)]/h. As h ‚Üí 0: the first factor f(x+h) approaches f(x) (because f is differentiable, hence continuous); [g(x+h)-g(x)]/h approaches g'(x); g(x) is constant with respect to h; [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h approaches f'(x). By the product limit law, the result is f(x)¬∑g'(x) + g(x)¬∑f'(x). This is the product rule. PROOF OF THE QUOTIENT RULE: Write f/g = f¬∑(1/g) and apply the product rule. We need d/dx[1/g(x)]. By the chain rule (or directly from the definition): d/dx[1/g] = -g'(x)/[g(x)]¬≤. Then d/dx[f/g] = f¬∑(-g'/g¬≤) + (1/g)¬∑f' = -fg'/g¬≤ + f'g/g¬≤ = (f'g - fg')/g¬≤. This is the quotient rule. (Note: the chain rule is introduced in the next lesson; one can also prove the quotient rule directly from the definition using the algebraic trick of combining fractions, similar to the 1/x derivative in the previous lesson.) Differentiating a Polynomial f'(x) = 20x¬≥ - 9x¬≤ + 14x - 2. Notice that the degree dropped by 1 (from 4 to 3) and the constant term vanished ‚ÄĒ this always happens when differentiating a polynomial. Apply the sum and difference rules to differentiate term by term. Each term can be handled independently. Apply the constant multiple rule to pull the numeric coefficients outside each derivative. Apply the power rule to each power of x: d/dx[x‚Āī]=4x¬≥, d/dx[x¬≥]=3x¬≤, d/dx[x¬≤]=2x, d/dx[x]=1. The constant 9 has derivative 0. Multiply each coefficient: 5¬∑4=20, 3¬∑3=9, 7¬∑2=14, 2¬∑1=2. The derivative is fully simplified. Negative and Fractional Exponents The power rule applies equally well to negative and fractional exponents. The key is to write the function in the form x‚ĀŅ before differentiating. The function is already written in power form, so the power rule applies directly to each term. No rewriting needed. Apply the sum rule: differentiate term by term. Apply the power rule with n = -3: bring down the exponent as a coefficient, then subtract 1 from the exponent. -3 - 1 = -4. Apply the power rule with n = 1/2: the new exponent is 1/2 - 1 = -1/2. Apply the power rule with n = 3/2: the new exponent is 3/2 - 1 = 1/2. Collect all three derivatives. Optionally rewrite using radical and fraction notation: x^(-4) = 1/x‚Āī, x^(-1/2) = 1/‚ąöx, x^(1/2) = ‚ąöx. Product Rule: Two Methods When the factors are easy to multiply out, expanding first is fine. But when factors are complicated (like (x¬≤+1)(e^x sin x)), the product rule is the only practical approach. Both methods always yield the same answer. Identify f(x) = x¬≤ + 3 and h(x) = 2x‚Āī - x + 5 as the two factors. Differentiate the first factor using the power and sum rules. Differentiate the second factor: d/dx[2x‚Āī] = 8x¬≥, d/dx[-x] = -1, d/dx[5] = 0. Apply the product rule: (first)'(second) + (first)(second)'. Substitute f'(x) = 2x, h(x) = 2x‚Āī-x+5, f(x) = x¬≤+3, h'(x) = 8x¬≥-1. Expand each product by distributing: 2x¬∑(2x‚Āī-x+5) = 4x‚ĀĶ-2x¬≤+10x, and (x¬≤+3)(8x¬≥-1) = 8x‚ĀĶ-x¬≤+24x¬≥-3. Combine like terms: 4x‚ĀĶ+8x‚ĀĶ=12x‚ĀĶ, 24x¬≥ alone, -2x¬≤-x¬≤=-3x¬≤, 10x alone, -3 alone. Multiply out the original expression, then differentiate the resulting polynomial. Expand by distributing x¬≤ across 2x‚Āī-x+5 (giving 2x‚Ā∂-x¬≥+5x¬≤) and distributing 3 across 2x‚Āī-x+5 (giving 6x‚Āī-3x+15). Rewrite in standard decreasing-degree form. Differentiate term by term using the power rule: d/dx[2x‚Ā∂]=12x‚ĀĶ, d/dx[6x‚Āī]=24x¬≥, d/dx[-x¬≥]=-3x¬≤, d/dx[5x¬≤]=10x, d/dx[-3x]=-3, d/dx[15]=0. The two answers agree, confirming the product rule is correct. For more complicated factors where expanding is impractical, the product rule is far more efficient. Quotient Rule h'(x) = (x¬≤ - 6x - 1) / (x-3)¬≤. Note that the quotient rule answer is undefined at x = 3, which makes sense ‚ÄĒ the original function h(x) has a vertical asymptote there. Identify the numerator as f(x) and the denominator as g(x) for use in the quotient rule formula. Differentiate numerator and denominator separately. d/dx[x¬≤+1] = 2x, d/dx[x-3] = 1. Write the quotient rule formula: (numerator prime)(denominator) minus (numerator)(denominator prime), all over (denominator) squared. Substitute the expressions for f, f', g, g' into the quotient rule. Expand the numerator: (2x)(x-3) = 2x¬≤-6x, and (x¬≤+1)(1) = x¬≤+1. Distribute the minus sign. Combine like terms in the numerator: 2x¬≤ - x¬≤ = x¬≤. The denominator stays factored as (x-3)¬≤. Finding Horizontal Tangent Lines The function has horizontal tangents at (1, -2) and (-1, 2). These are the local minimum and local maximum of the cubic curve. Setting f'(x) = 0 and solving is the fundamental technique for finding local extrema. Differentiate using the power and difference rules. A horizontal tangent occurs where the slope is 0, i.e., where f'(x) = 0. Set the derivative equal to 0, since a horizontal tangent line has slope 0. Add 3 to both sides. Divide both sides by 3. Take the square root of both sides. There are two solutions. Evaluate f at each critical point to find the y-coordinates of the tangent points. Higher-Order Derivatives f''(x) = 12x¬≤ - 12 tells us about concavity (where the function curves up or down). f''(x) > 0 when x¬≤ > 1, i.e., when |x| > 1 (concave up). f'''(x) = 24x tells us how the concavity is changing. Start with the original function. Differentiate once: d/dx[x‚Āī]=4x¬≥, d/dx[-6x¬≤]=-12x, d/dx[1]=0. Differentiate again to get the second derivative: d/dx[4x¬≥]=12x¬≤, d/dx[-12x]=-12. Differentiate a third time: d/dx[12x¬≤]=24x, d/dx[-12]=0. Tangent Line Using Quotient Rule The tangent line is y = (16/9)x - 10/9 at the point (1, 2/3). Identify numerator and denominator. Differentiate numerator and denominator. Apply the quotient rule. Expand the numerator: 6x(x+2) = 6x¬≤+12x, and -(3x¬≤-1) = -3x¬≤+1. Combine: 6x¬≤-3x¬≤=3x¬≤. Evaluate the derivative at x = 1 to get the slope of the tangent line. Evaluate the original function at x = 1 to find the y-coordinate of the tangent point. Write the tangent line in point-slope form using point (1, 2/3) and slope 16/9. Simplify: convert 2/3 = 6/9, then -16/9 + 6/9 = -10/9. Product Rule on a Square Both methods agree: f'(x) = 4x¬≥ + 4x = 4x(x¬≤+1). Note that 2u¬∑u' = 2(x¬≤+1)¬∑2x is actually a preview of the chain rule result d/dx[u‚ĀŅ] = nu‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļu'. Write f(x) = u(x)¬∑u(x) where u(x) = x¬≤ + 1. Identify the repeated factor and differentiate it. Apply the product rule. Since both factors are the same, both terms in the product rule are identical, giving 2¬∑u(x)¬∑u'(x). Substitute u(x) = x¬≤+1 and u'(x) = 2x. Expand for the standard polynomial form. Expand (x¬≤+1)¬≤ = x‚Āī + 2x¬≤ + 1, then differentiate. Expand using (a+b)¬≤ = a¬≤+2ab+b¬≤ with a=x¬≤ and b=1. Differentiate: d/dx[x‚Āī]=4x¬≥, d/dx[2x¬≤]=4x, d/dx[1]=0. Same answer as Method 1. Complete Kinematics: Position, Velocity, Acceleration (Physics) The particle moves right on (0,1), reverses at t=1, moves left on (1,2), reverses again at t=2, then moves right forever. The acceleration changes sign at t=1.5 s (inflection point). This example demonstrates the full kinematic stack: differentiation once gives velocity, twice gives acceleration. The physics tells you what the math means: where f'=0 is a physical rest; where f' and f'' agree in sign is where the object speeds up. Power rule: d/dt[2t¬≥]=6t¬≤, d/dt[-9t¬≤]=-18t, d/dt[12t]=12. Differentiate v(t) again: d/dt[6t¬≤]=12t, d/dt[-18t]=-18. Factor to find when velocity is zero. Set v=0 and factor the quadratic. The particle momentarily stops at t=1 and t=2 ‚ÄĒ the turning points in its motion. Sign chart: v = 6(t-1)(t-2) is positive when both factors agree in sign. Moving right for t<1 and t>2; moving left for 1<t<2. Acceleration changes sign at t=1.5 s. This is the inflection point of the position curve. On (1,1.5): v<0 and a<0 (both negative ‚Üí speeding up leftward). On (2,‚ąě): v>0 and a>0 ‚Üí speeding up rightward. On (0,1) and (1.5,2): v and a have opposite signs ‚Üí slowing down. Find the equation of the tangent line to y = \dfrac{x^2+3}{x-1} \text{ at } x = 2. Find all points on f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2 where (a) the tangent is horizontal (parallel to y = 3, i.e., slope 0) and (b) the tangent is parallel to y = 3x (slope 3). If f and g are differentiable at x, define h(x) = f(x) \cdot g(x). Use the limit definition of h'(x) to derive the product rule. Do not assume the product rule ‚ÄĒ prove it.

documents.title: The Chain Rule ‚ÄĒ Differentiating Compositions
documents.subtitle: How to differentiate a function inside another function ‚ÄĒ the most widely used rule in all of calculus
documents.tags: chain rule composite functions composition outside-inside Leibniz form triple composition
documents.content: Let's build intuition for composition before worrying about differentiation. The expression f(g(x)) means: first apply g to x, get an intermediate result, then apply f to that intermediate result. For example, if g(x) = x¬≤ + 1 and f(u) = ‚ąöu, then f(g(x)) = ‚ąö(x¬≤+1). The function g acts "on the inside" and f acts "on the outside." To evaluate at x = 3: first g(3) = 10, then f(10) = ‚ąö10. This two-step process is composition. Now think about rates. If u = g(x) changes by a certain rate as x changes, and y = f(u) changes at a certain rate as u changes, then what is the rate of change of y with respect to x? The answer is found by multiplying the rates: if u increases 3 times as fast as x, and y increases 4 times as fast as u, then y increases 12 times as fast as x. Rates along a chain multiply. The gear analogy makes this precise. Suppose gear A meshes with gear B, and gear B meshes with gear C. If gear A turns 3 times for every revolution of gear B (dA/dB = 3), and gear B turns 2 times for every revolution of gear C (dB/dC = 2), then gear A turns 6 times for every revolution of gear C: dA/dC = (dA/dB)¬∑(dB/dC) = 3¬∑2 = 6. The rates multiply along the chain. This is the chain rule in its essence. In Leibniz notation, the chain rule is dy/dx = (dy/du)¬∑(du/dx). If you squint, it looks like the du terms "cancel" ‚ÄĒ and while this is not literally true (dy/du and du/dx are limits, not fractions), it serves as a perfect mnemonic and is essentially justified by the fact that in the limit, these ratio quantities do behave multiplicatively. There is a beautifully practical way to execute the chain rule called the "outside-inside" method. To differentiate f(g(x)): (1) Differentiate the outside function f, evaluated at the inside g(x), leaving the inside completely unchanged. (2) Multiply by the derivative of the inside g'(x). In symbols: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) ¬∑ g'(x). The key is to NOT differentiate the inside first ‚ÄĒ leave it intact and only differentiate the outer wrapper. Let's see this with a concrete example: d/dx[(3x¬≤-1)‚ĀĶ]. The outside function is u‚ĀĶ (raise to the fifth), and the inside is u = 3x¬≤-1. Differentiate the outside: the derivative of u‚ĀĶ is 5u‚Āī, evaluated at u = 3x¬≤-1, giving 5(3x¬≤-1)‚Āī. Multiply by the derivative of the inside: d/dx[3x¬≤-1] = 6x. Final answer: 5(3x¬≤-1)‚Āī ¬∑ 6x = 30x(3x¬≤-1)‚Āī. This two-step process ‚ÄĒ differentiate outer (keep inner), multiply by derivative of inner ‚ÄĒ is all there is to the chain rule. A note on identifying the outer and inner functions: look for the "last operation" that would be performed if you were evaluating the function at a specific number. For ‚ąö(x¬≤+1), the last operation is taking a square root, so the outer function is u^(1/2) and the inner is x¬≤+1. For sin(5x¬≥), the last operation is taking the sine, so outer is sin(u) and inner is 5x¬≥. The chain rule extends to triple and longer compositions. For y = f(g(h(x))), the derivative is f'(g(h(x))) ¬∑ g'(h(x)) ¬∑ h'(x). Differentiate the outermost layer first, then the next, and so on, each time multiplying by the derivative of the layer below. This is like peeling an onion, one layer at a time. There are two equivalent forms of the chain rule. The Lagrange/function-composition form names the outer and inner functions explicitly and is cleaner for algebraic manipulation. The Leibniz form uses dy/du and du/dx and is more intuitive for applied problems involving rates. The chain rule combined with the power rule gives one of the most common differentiation formulas in all of calculus: d/dx[u‚ĀŅ] = nu‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑u', where u = g(x) is any differentiable function. This formula applies whenever you have "something to a power" ‚ÄĒ the power rule applies to the outer function, and the chain rule contributes the u' factor. The extended chain rule for triple compositions states that for y = f(g(h(x))), the derivative is f'(g(h(x))) ¬∑ g'(h(x)) ¬∑ h'(x). The pattern is clear: differentiate each layer from outside to inside, evaluate each outer derivative at everything inside it, and multiply all the factors together. Identifying the outer and inner functions correctly is a skill that develops with practice. The best approach is to always ask: "What is the last thing done to x if I evaluate this at a number?" That last operation is the outer function. Everything that comes before is the inner function. The most natural "proof" of the chain rule goes like this: form the difference quotient for (f‚ąėg) at x, multiply top and bottom by g(x+h) - g(x), and "cancel" to get [f(g(x+h)) - f(g(x))] / [g(x+h)-g(x)] times [g(x+h)-g(x)] / h. As h ‚Üí 0, the first factor approaches f'(g(x)) and the second approaches g'(x), giving the product f'(g(x))¬∑g'(x). This argument has a fatal flaw: we divided by g(x+h) - g(x), but this quantity might equal zero for infinitely many values of h near 0 (even if g'(x) ‚Č† 0), making the division illegal. A simple example of this flaw: take g(x) = x¬≤sin(1/x) for x ‚Č† 0 and g(0) = 0. Then g'(0) = 0, but g(h) = 0 for h = 1/(nŌÄ) for any integer n ‚Č† 0, which are values of h arbitrarily close to 0 where g(h) - g(0) = 0. If we divide by g(x+h) - g(x) in the naive proof at x = 0, we are dividing by zero for these special values of h. The correct proof uses the following approach. Define an auxiliary function ő¶(k) = [f(g(x)+k) - f(g(x))] / k for k ‚Č† 0, and ő¶(0) = f'(g(x)). Because f is differentiable at g(x), we have lim(k‚Üí0) ő¶(k) = f'(g(x)) = ő¶(0), so ő¶ is continuous at 0. Now write: f(g(x+h)) - f(g(x)) = ő¶(g(x+h)-g(x)) ¬∑ [g(x+h)-g(x)]. This is valid because when g(x+h)-g(x) ‚Č† 0, it follows the definition of ő¶ with k = g(x+h)-g(x), and when g(x+h)-g(x) = 0, both sides are 0. Dividing by h: [f(g(x+h))-f(g(x))]/h = ő¶(g(x+h)-g(x)) ¬∑ [g(x+h)-g(x)]/h. As h‚Üí0: ő¶(g(x+h)-g(x)) ‚Üí ő¶(0) = f'(g(x)) (because g is continuous, g(x+h)-g(x)‚Üí0, and ő¶ is continuous at 0); [g(x+h)-g(x)]/h ‚Üí g'(x). The product of the limits is f'(g(x))¬∑g'(x). This completes the rigorous proof. Chain Rule with Power Function f'(x) = 30x(3x¬≤-1)‚Āī. The chain rule contributed the factor of 6x (the derivative of the inner function), which is what distinguishes this from just differentiating u‚ĀĶ. Identify the outer and inner functions. The outer function is "raise to the fifth power" and the inner is 3x¬≤-1. Differentiate the outer function (power rule: bring down 5, reduce exponent to 4) and differentiate the inner function separately. Apply the chain rule: differentiate the outer function (giving 5u‚Āī) evaluated at the inner function (replacing u with 3x¬≤-1), then multiply by the derivative of the inner function. Multiply the constants 5 and 6 together: 5¬∑6 = 30. The (3x¬≤-1)‚Āī factor remains as is. Chain Rule with Square Root f'(x) = x/‚ąö(x¬≤+1). Note that the derivative is 0 at x = 0 (horizontal tangent at the bottom of the U-shaped curve) and grows toward ¬Ī1 as x ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě. Rewrite the square root as a power of 1/2 to clearly see the power function structure. Identify the outer function as the square root (or equivalently the 1/2 power) and the inner function as x¬≤+1. Differentiate outer (power rule with n=1/2) and inner separately. Chain rule: F'(g(x)) ¬∑ g'(x). Replace u with x¬≤+1 in the outer derivative, then multiply by 2x. Simplify: the factor of 2 in the numerator cancels with the 2 from the power rule denominator. Rewrite the negative power as a square root. Chain Rule with Sine f'(x) = 15x¬≤ cos(5x¬≥). This illustrates the general pattern: d/dx[sin(g(x))] = cos(g(x))¬∑g'(x). The argument of cosine is the same as the argument of sine was. The outer function is the sine function and the inner function is 5x¬≥. The derivative of sine is cosine (covered in the trig derivatives lesson). The derivative of the inner function 5x¬≥ is 15x¬≤ by the power and constant multiple rules. Apply the chain rule: differentiate the outer function (giving cos(u)), evaluated at the inner (replacing u with 5x¬≥), then multiply by the inner derivative 15x¬≤. Rewrite with the constant factor first for standard form. Chain Rule with Exponential (Preview) f'(x) = 2xe^(x¬≤). The function e^(x¬≤) grows enormously fast but its derivative is not just itself ‚ÄĒ the chain rule contributes the factor of 2x from the exponent. The outer function is e to the power u, and the inner function is x¬≤. The key property of e^u is that it equals its own derivative (proved in the exponential derivatives lesson). The inner derivative is 2x. Apply the chain rule: the outer derivative e^u evaluated at u = x¬≤ gives e^(x¬≤), times the inner derivative 2x. Rewrite with the constant factor first. Chain Rule: Do Not Confuse (sin x)‚Āī with sin(x‚Āī) f'(x) = 4(sin x)¬≥ cos x = 4 sin¬≥(x) cos(x). The position of the exponent completely changes the meaning: (sin x)‚Āī means sin^4(x) [sine, then power], while sin(x‚Āī) means the sine of x‚Āī [power, then sine]. In (sin x)‚Āī, the outer function is "raise to the fourth power" and the inner function is sin x. The entire sine is being raised to a power. Differentiate outer: 4u¬≥. Differentiate inner: d/dx[sin x] = cos x. Chain rule result. This is often written as 4 sin¬≥(x) cos(x). For sin(x‚Āī), the outer function is sine and the inner is x‚Āī. The derivative of sine is cosine evaluated at x‚Āī, times the inner derivative 4x¬≥. These are completely different functions. Triple Composition f'(x) = 8x sin¬≥(x¬≤) cos(x¬≤). Triple compositions peel away one layer at a time, each contributing its own derivative as a multiplicative factor. Rewrite explicitly to show the structure: we are raising sin(x¬≤) to the fourth power. This is a triple composition: the outermost is u‚Āī, the middle is sin, the innermost is x¬≤. The outermost function is the fourth power. The middle function is the sine function. The innermost function is x¬≤. Differentiate each layer independently. Apply the extended chain rule: outer derivative (evaluated all the way at the inside) times middle derivative (evaluated at innermost) times innermost derivative. Substitute: F'(G(H(x))) = 4[sin(x¬≤)]¬≥, G'(H(x)) = cos(x¬≤), H'(x) = 2x. Collect constants: 4¬∑2x = 8x. Write in standard form. Leibniz Form of Chain Rule This gives the same answer as the composition form: dy/dx = 30x(3x¬≤-1)‚Āī. The Leibniz form is particularly clear for related rates problems where the intermediate variable u has physical meaning. Differentiate y = u‚ĀĶ with respect to u, treating u as the independent variable. Differentiate u = 3x¬≤ - 1 with respect to x. Multiply the two rates together per the chain rule in Leibniz form. The du "terms" appear to cancel like fractions, though this is a notational convenience, not literal cancellation. Substitute back u = 3x¬≤-1 to express everything in terms of x. The final answer must be in terms of the original variable x. Chain Rule Combined with Quotient Rule f'(x) = ‚ąö(x¬≤+1) ¬∑ (2x¬≤+3x-1) / (x+1)¬≤. Factoring out the common (x¬≤+1)^(1/2) factor from the quotient rule numerator is a key simplification technique. Identify the numerator and denominator. The numerator requires the chain rule; the denominator is simple. Differentiate the numerator using the chain rule: outer is u^(3/2) giving (3/2)u^(1/2), inner is x¬≤+1 giving 2x. Simplify: (3/2)¬∑2x = 3x. The derivative of the numerator is 3x‚ąö(x¬≤+1). The derivative of the denominator x+1 is simply 1. Apply the quotient rule formula. Substitute N, N', D, D' into the quotient rule. Factor out (x¬≤+1)^(1/2) from both terms in the numerator, since both contain this common factor and the exponents differ by 1. Expand inside the brackets: 3x(x+1) = 3x¬≤+3x, then subtract x¬≤+1. Combine like terms in the numerator: 3x¬≤-x¬≤=2x¬≤. Rewrite (x¬≤+1)^(1/2) as ‚ąö(x¬≤+1). \text{Differentiate } f(x) = (2x+1)^{10}. \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \sqrt{\dfrac{x^2+1}{x-1}}. \text{If } f \text{ is differentiable and } g(x) = [f(x^2+1)]^2, \text{ find } g'(x) \text{ in terms of } f \text{ and } f'.

documents.title: Derivatives of Trigonometric Functions
documents.subtitle: Why the slope of the sine curve is cosine ‚ÄĒ and deriving all six trig derivatives
documents.tags: sine derivative cosine derivative tangent derivative trigonometric functions trig limits chain rule with trig
documents.content: Imagine tracing around the unit circle as the angle őł increases from 0. The y-coordinate follows the sine function. Let's watch what happens to the SLOPE of the sine curve as őł varies. At őł = 0: the point (1, 0) is moving straight upward (tangent to the circle is vertical here). The y-coordinate is increasing at maximum rate. The slope of sin(őł) at this point is 1. And cos(0) = 1. They agree. At őł = ŌÄ/2: the point (0, 1) is at the very top of the circle, moving horizontally (leftward). The y-coordinate has just reached its peak and is about to decrease ‚ÄĒ its rate of change is 0. The slope of sin(őł) is 0. And cos(ŌÄ/2) = 0. They agree. At őł = ŌÄ: the point (-1, 0) is moving straight downward. The y-coordinate is decreasing at maximum rate. The slope is -1. And cos(ŌÄ) = -1. They agree. At őł = 3ŌÄ/2: the point (0, -1) is at the bottom of the circle, momentarily moving horizontally (rightward). The y-coordinate is at its minimum and about to increase ‚ÄĒ rate of change is 0. And cos(3ŌÄ/2) = 0. They agree. The pattern is unmistakable: the rate of change of the sine function at every angle equals the value of the cosine function at that angle. This is not a coincidence ‚ÄĒ it is a theorem. The derivative of sin(őł) is cos(őł). There is also a beautiful geometric argument using the tangent to the unit circle. At the point (cosőł, sinőł) on the unit circle, the radius vector points in the direction (cosőł, sinőł). The tangent to the circle at this point is perpendicular to the radius, so it points in the direction (-sinőł, cosőł). This tangent direction has a "slope" component in the y-direction equal to cosőł, confirming that dy/dőł = cosőł. Now why does d/dx[cos x] = -sin x have a minus sign? At x = 0, cosine is at its maximum (value 1) and is about to decrease ‚ÄĒ its rate of change is negative. Specifically, cos(0) = 1 is decreasing and -sin(0) = 0... wait, that's 0, not negative. But at x = ŌÄ/4, the cosine is decreasing. Let's check: the slope of cos(x) at x = ŌÄ/4 should be negative, and indeed -sin(ŌÄ/4) = -‚ąö2/2 < 0. More precisely: at x = 0, the cosine reaches its maximum, so its derivative is 0 there. For x slightly positive, cosine begins to decrease, so d/dx[cos x] should be negative. And -sin(x) is negative for x ‚ąą (0, ŌÄ), which matches. The minus sign in d/dx[cos x] = -sin x reflects the fact that cosine is always decreasing when sine is positive (between 0 and ŌÄ) and increasing when sine is negative (between ŌÄ and 2ŌÄ). We state all six trigonometric derivative formulas. The first two (sine and cosine) are proved from the limit definition. The other four are derived using the quotient rule, since tan, csc, sec, and cot are all rational combinations of sine and cosine. Once we have d/dx[sin x] and d/dx[cos x], the quotient rule handles everything else. Recall that tan x = sin x / cos x, csc x = 1/sin x, sec x = 1/cos x, and cot x = cos x / sin x. The memory device for the four derived formulas: all three "co-" functions (cosine, cosecant, cotangent) have negative derivatives. The remaining three (sine, tangent, secant) have positive derivatives. Within each pair: sine and cosine swap (but cosine gets a minus sign), tangent becomes sec¬≤, cotangent becomes -csc¬≤, and secant and cosecant follow their own symmetric patterns. Every one of these formulas extends to compositions via the chain rule. For any differentiable function u = g(x): d/dx[sin(u)] = cos(u)¬∑u', d/dx[cos(u)] = -sin(u)¬∑u', d/dx[tan(u)] = sec¬≤(u)¬∑u', and so on. These extended forms are the versions used in 90% of calculations. We now prove d/dx[sin x] = cos x rigorously from the limit definition. This proof depends on two fundamental trigonometric limits, which we state but do not re-derive here (they were established in the limits chapter): lim(h‚Üí0) sin(h)/h = 1 and lim(h‚Üí0) (1 - cos h)/h = 0. PROOF that d/dx[sin x] = cos x: Using the limit definition, d/dx[sin x] = lim(h‚Üí0) [sin(x+h) - sin(x)] / h. Apply the sine addition formula: sin(x+h) = sin(x)cos(h) + cos(x)sin(h). So the numerator becomes: sin(x)cos(h) + cos(x)sin(h) - sin(x) = sin(x)[cos(h) - 1] + cos(x)sin(h). Dividing by h: sin(x) ¬∑ [cos(h)-1]/h + cos(x) ¬∑ sin(h)/h. As h ‚Üí 0: the first term has sin(x) (a constant with respect to h) times [cos(h)-1]/h. Rearranging, [cos(h)-1]/h = -(1-cos h)/h ‚Üí -0 = 0. The second term has cos(x) (constant) times sin(h)/h ‚Üí 1. Therefore the entire expression approaches sin(x)¬∑0 + cos(x)¬∑1 = cos(x). This completes the proof. PROOF that d/dx[cos x] = -sin x: Using the limit definition with the cosine addition formula cos(x+h) = cos(x)cos(h) - sin(x)sin(h): [cos(x+h)-cos(x)]/h = cos(x)¬∑[cos(h)-1]/h - sin(x)¬∑sin(h)/h. As h‚Üí0, the first term approaches cos(x)¬∑0 = 0 and the second approaches sin(x)¬∑1 = sin(x). So d/dx[cos x] = -sin(x). The minus sign comes from the minus sign in the cosine addition formula. The two key limits sin(h)/h ‚Üí 1 and (1-cos h)/h ‚Üí 0 as h ‚Üí 0 are the real engine of these proofs. The first was established geometrically by the squeeze theorem (the arc length h is squeezed between sin h and tan h for small h > 0). The second follows from the first via the identity (1-cos h)/h = sin¬≤(h) / [h(1+cos h)] = [sin(h)/h] ¬∑ [sin(h)/(1+cos h)] ‚Üí 1¬∑0/2 = 0. Sine with Chain Rule f'(x) = 3cos(3x). The chain rule "multiplies in" the factor of 3 from the inner function. Without the chain rule, the answer would be cos(3x) ‚ÄĒ missing the crucial factor of 3. Identify the composition. The outer function is sine; the inner is 3x. Derivative of sine is cosine; derivative of the inner function 3x is 3. Apply chain rule: outer derivative (cosine, evaluated at inner) times inner derivative (3). The argument of cosine is the same as the original argument of sine. Product Rule with Cosine f'(x) = 2x cos(x) - x¬≤ sin(x). The product rule gives two terms: the first represents the rate of change from the x¬≤ factor, and the second from the cos(x) factor. This is a product of two functions. Assign the first factor and second factor for the product rule. Differentiate each factor separately. d/dx[x¬≤] = 2x by the power rule; d/dx[cos x] = -sin x. Apply the product rule: (first)(second)' + (first)'(second). Simplify by writing the two terms separately. This is the fully simplified form. Optionally factor out x from both terms. This factored form can be useful for finding zeros. Tangent with Chain Rule f'(x) = 2x sec¬≤(x¬≤+1). The derivative of tangent is always sec¬≤, and when the argument is a function of x, the chain rule contributes the derivative of that argument. Identify the composition. The outer function is tangent; the inner is x¬≤+1. The derivative of tan(u) is sec¬≤(u). The inner derivative is 2x. Apply chain rule: outer derivative (sec¬≤, evaluated at x¬≤+1) times inner derivative (2x). Power of Sine ‚ÄĒ Chain Rule f'(x) = 2 sin(x) cos(x) = sin(2x). The double angle identity provides a beautiful simplification here. Rewrite sin¬≤(x) explicitly as (sin x)¬≤ to make the composition structure clear. The outer function is squaring; the inner is sine. Identify the layers. Differentiate each layer. Chain rule: 2u evaluated at u = sin x gives 2 sin x, times the inner derivative cos x. Apply the double angle identity: 2 sin(x) cos(x) = sin(2x). This is a nice simplification, though both forms are correct. Composition of Two Trig Functions f'(x) = -cos(x) sin(sin x). Note that the argument of the outermost sine is sin(x), not x ‚ÄĒ we evaluate the outer derivative at the inner function, preserving the inner function completely. Both outer and inner functions are trigonometric. The outer is cosine (acting on the result of sine) and the inner is sine. Differentiate each layer: derivative of cosine is negative sine; derivative of sin x is cos x. Apply chain rule: outer derivative -sin(u) evaluated at u = sin x gives -sin(sin x), times the inner derivative cos x. Product Rule with Chain Rule: Secant Times Tangent f'(x) = 5 sec(5x)[tan¬≤(5x) + sec¬≤(5x)]. This can be further simplified using the identity tan¬≤(x) + 1 = sec¬≤(x), giving tan¬≤+sec¬≤ = sec¬≤-1+sec¬≤ = 2sec¬≤-1. This is a product of two functions, each requiring the chain rule. Differentiate sec(5x) using the chain rule: d/dx[sec u] = sec u tan u times u'. Here u = 5x and u' = 5. Differentiate tan(5x) using the chain rule: d/dx[tan u] = sec¬≤ u times u'. Here u = 5x and u' = 5. Apply the product rule. Expand: tan(5x) ¬∑ tan(5x) = tan¬≤(5x) and sec(5x) ¬∑ sec¬≤(5x) = sec¬≥(5x). Factor out 5 sec(5x) from both terms. Quotient Rule with Sine f'(x) = 2cos(x) / (1-sin x)¬≤. The simplification of the numerator was made possible by factoring out cos x. Identify numerator and denominator for the quotient rule. Differentiate numerator: d/dx[1+sin x] = cos x. Differentiate denominator: d/dx[1-sin x] = -cos x. Apply the quotient rule. Distribute the negative sign on the second term: -(1+sin x)(-cos x) = +cos x(1+sin x). Factor cos x from both terms in the numerator. Simplify inside the brackets: (1-sin x)+(1+sin x) = 2. The sin x terms cancel. Tangent Line to Sine Curve The tangent line is y = (1/2)x + (‚ąö3/2 - ŌÄ/6). At x = ŌÄ/3 ‚Čą 1.047, the sine curve has slope exactly 1/2 ‚ÄĒ the curve is rising gently as it approaches its peak at x = ŌÄ/2. The derivative of sin x is cos x ‚ÄĒ no chain rule needed since the argument is just x. Evaluate the derivative at x = ŌÄ/3. From the unit circle, cos(ŌÄ/3) = 1/2. Find the y-coordinate of the tangent point. From the unit circle, sin(ŌÄ/3) = ‚ąö3/2. Write in point-slope form with point (ŌÄ/3, ‚ąö3/2) and slope 1/2. Distribute 1/2 across (x - ŌÄ/3) = x - ŌÄ/3, giving (1/2)x - ŌÄ/6, then add ‚ąö3/2. \text{Differentiate } f(x) = 3\cos x - 4\sin x. Prove from the quotient rule that \dfrac{d}{dx}[\sec x] = \sec x \tan x. \text{Find all } x \in [0, 2\pi] \text{ where } f(x) = 2\sin x - \sin(2x) \text{ has a horizontal tangent line.}

documents.title: Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
documents.subtitle: The function that equals its own derivative ‚ÄĒ and the logarithm that unlocks it
documents.tags: exponential derivative logarithm derivative natural log e^x ln x logarithmic differentiation a^x
documents.content: Let's understand e by starting from scratch. Consider the function b^x for different bases b. At x = 0, every such function equals 1 (since b‚Āį = 1). But what is the SLOPE of b^x at x = 0? For b = 2, the slope at 0 is approximately 0.693. For b = 3, the slope at 0 is approximately 1.099. As b increases from 2 to 3, the slope at x = 0 increases from 0.693 to 1.099. Somewhere between 2 and 3, the slope at x = 0 must equal exactly 1. That special base is called e. Numerically, e ‚Čą 2.71828... This definition ‚ÄĒ e is the base for which the slope of b^x at x = 0 is exactly 1 ‚ÄĒ has a stunning consequence. It means that for the function e^x, the derivative at x = 0 is exactly 1. But now we can use the law of exponents to compute the derivative at any other point x. We will see that d/dx[e^x] = e^x: the function e^x equals its own derivative everywhere, not just at x = 0. Why is this remarkable? It means that at every point, the slope of e^x equals its own height. The taller the curve is, the steeper it rises. The curve catches up to its own slope, then the increased slope makes it grow even faster, creating an accelerating feedback loop. This is why exponential growth "explodes" ‚ÄĒ it grows proportional to itself. Now for the natural logarithm. The natural log ln(x) is defined as the inverse of e^x: if e^y = x, then y = ln(x). Since e^x has derivative e^x, and ln(x) is its inverse function, we can find the derivative of ln(x) using implicit differentiation. Starting from e^y = x, differentiate both sides with respect to x: the right side gives 1, and the left side gives e^y ¬∑ dy/dx (by the chain rule, since y is a function of x). So e^y ¬∑ dy/dx = 1, giving dy/dx = 1/e^y = 1/x (since e^y = x). The derivative of ln(x) is 1/x. For other bases: if we want to differentiate a^x for any positive base a ‚Č† 1, we use the conversion a^x = e^(x ln a). This rewrites any exponential in base e, allowing us to use the chain rule: d/dx[e^(x ln a)] = e^(x ln a) ¬∑ ln(a) = a^x ¬∑ ln(a). The natural log of the base appears as a multiplicative factor. Logarithmic differentiation is a powerful technique that uses logarithms to convert hard differentiation problems into easier ones. The key idea: if y = f(x), take ln of both sides to get ln(y) = ln(f(x)), then differentiate implicitly. This works because the chain rule on the left gives (1/y)¬∑(dy/dx), and we can solve for dy/dx = y¬∑(d/dx)[ln(f(x))]. This technique is especially useful for (1) functions where the variable appears in both the base and exponent, like y = x^x, and (2) products of many functions, where logarithms convert multiplication into addition. We state all four exponential and logarithmic derivative formulas. Each extends to compositions via the chain rule. The formula d/dx[a^x] = a^x ¬∑ ln(a) shows why e is so special: when a = e, ln(e) = 1, so the formula gives d/dx[e^x] = e^x ¬∑ 1 = e^x. For any other base, there is an extra factor of ln(a). Similarly, d/dx[log_a(x)] = 1/(x ln a). When a = e, ln(e) = 1 and we recover d/dx[ln x] = 1/x. Logarithmic differentiation is the technique of applying ln to both sides of y = f(x) before differentiating. It works because (1) logarithms convert products to sums and powers to multiples, making the differentiation easier, and (2) the chain rule on ln(y) gives (1/y)(dy/dx), which we solve for dy/dx at the end. PROOF that d/dx[e^x] = e^x: By the limit definition, d/dx[e^x] = lim(h‚Üí0) [e^(x+h) - e^x] / h. Using the exponent law e^(x+h) = e^x ¬∑ e^h, factor out e^x: = lim(h‚Üí0) e^x ¬∑ [e^h - 1] / h = e^x ¬∑ lim(h‚Üí0) (e^h - 1)/h. By the defining property of e (or equivalently, by the definition of e as lim(n‚Üí‚ąě)(1+1/n)‚ĀŅ), the limit lim(h‚Üí0) (e^h - 1)/h = 1. Therefore d/dx[e^x] = e^x ¬∑ 1 = e^x. PROOF that d/dx[ln x] = 1/x using implicit differentiation: Let y = ln x. By definition of the natural log, this means e^y = x. Differentiate both sides with respect to x: d/dx[e^y] = d/dx[x]. The left side requires the chain rule since y depends on x: d/dx[e^y] = e^y ¬∑ dy/dx. The right side is just 1. So e^y ¬∑ dy/dx = 1, giving dy/dx = 1/e^y. Since e^y = x, this simplifies to dy/dx = 1/x. Therefore d/dx[ln x] = 1/x. For x < 0, we have d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x as well (the absolute value does not change the derivative for negative x, since ln|x| = ln(-x) for x < 0, and d/dx[ln(-x)] = (1/(-x))¬∑(-1) = 1/x). WHY LOGARITHMIC DIFFERENTIATION WORKS: If y = f(x) and f(x) > 0, then ln(y) = ln(f(x)). Differentiating both sides with respect to x and applying the chain rule on the left: d/dx[ln y] = (1/y)¬∑(dy/dx) and d/dx[ln(f(x))] = f'(x)/f(x). Setting equal: (1/y)(dy/dx) = f'(x)/f(x), so dy/dx = y¬∑f'(x)/f(x) = f(x)¬∑f'(x)/f(x) = f'(x). This is consistent but doesn't help for simple cases. The technique's power comes when f(x) involves products, quotients, and powers, because ln converts these into sums, differences, and multiples, which are much easier to differentiate. Exponential with Chain Rule f'(x) = 6x e^(3x¬≤). The key here is that the chain rule "brings out" the derivative of the exponent as a multiplicative factor, while e^(3x¬≤) itself remains unchanged. Identify the composition: the outer function is e^u and the inner is 3x¬≤. The derivative of e^u is e^u (e^x is its own derivative). The inner derivative of 3x¬≤ is 6x. Chain rule: e^u evaluated at u=3x¬≤ gives e^(3x¬≤), times the inner derivative 6x. Product Rule with e^x f'(x) = xe^x(x+2). The factored form immediately shows that f'(x) = 0 when x = 0 or x = -2, the critical points of this function. Identify the two factors for the product rule. Differentiate each factor. d/dx[x¬≤] = 2x, and d/dx[e^x] = e^x (its own derivative). Apply the product rule. Factor out e^x from both terms, then factor out x from the remaining expression. Natural Log with Chain Rule f'(x) = 2x/(x¬≤+1). The chain rule result for ln(g(x)) is always g'(x)/g(x) ‚ÄĒ this is sometimes called the "logarithmic derivative." Identify the composition. The outer is the natural log; the inner is x¬≤+1. The derivative of ln(u) is 1/u. The inner derivative is 2x. Chain rule: (1/u) evaluated at u=x¬≤+1 gives 1/(x¬≤+1), times the inner derivative 2x. This is the "u-prime over u" pattern. Quotient Rule with e^x f'(x) = e^x(x-3)/x‚Āī. Setting f'(x) = 0: e^x ‚Č† 0 always, and x‚Āī ‚Č† 0 for x ‚Č† 0, so the only critical point is x = 3. Identify numerator and denominator. Differentiate each: d/dx[e^x] = e^x (its own derivative); d/dx[x¬≥] = 3x¬≤. Apply the quotient rule. Factor e^x from the numerator. Factor x¬≤ from x¬≥-3x¬≤ = x¬≤(x-3), then cancel x¬≤/x‚Ā∂ = 1/x‚Āī. Log of a Trig Function f'(x) = cot(x). This elegant result d/dx[ln(sin x)] = cot x appears frequently in integration problems. Identify the composition: outer is ln, inner is sin x. Derivative of ln(u) is 1/u; derivative of sin x is cos x. Apply the chain rule: u'/u = cos x / sin x = cot x. Exponential with a Different Base f'(x) = 2(ln 5) 5^(2x). The factor of ln 5 ‚Čą 1.609 comes from the base being 5, not e. If the base were e, we would get d/dx[e^(2x)] = 2e^(2x), with no extra logarithm factor. Do not confuse 5^(2x) with (5¬≤)^x = 25^x. While equal in value, the chain rule approach is cleaner: treat 5^u with u = 2x. Identify the composition. Use d/dx[a^u] = a^u ¬∑ ln(a). Here a = 5, so d/du[5^u] = 5^u ln 5. Inner derivative of 2x is 2. Apply chain rule and collect constants. Logarithmic Differentiation: x^x dy/dx = x^x(1 + ln x). At x = e, this is e^e(1 + 1) = 2e^e. At x = 1, dy/dx = 1¬∑(0 + 1) = 1. Logarithmic differentiation converts the impossible-looking x^x into a manageable calculation. This function has x in both the base and the exponent. Neither the power rule (which requires a constant exponent) nor the exponential rule (which requires a constant base) applies directly. Take ln of both sides. Use the logarithm power rule: ln(x^x) = x¬∑ln x. Differentiate both sides with respect to x. The left side requires the chain rule since y is a function of x. The left side: d/dx[ln y] = (1/y)¬∑(dy/dx) by the chain rule (outer is ln, inner is y(x)). Differentiate x¬∑ln x using the product rule: (x)‚Äô(ln x) + (x)(ln x)‚Äô = 1¬∑ln x + x¬∑(1/x) = ln x + 1. Equate the two results. Multiply both sides by y, then substitute back y = x^x. Logarithmic Differentiation: Variable Base and Exponent The derivative is (x¬≤+1)^(sin x)¬∑[cos(x)¬∑ln(x¬≤+1) + 2x sin(x)/(x¬≤+1)]. This would have been completely intractable without logarithmic differentiation. Both the base x¬≤+1 and the exponent sin x are functions of x. This requires logarithmic differentiation. Take ln of both sides. Use ln(a^b) = b¬∑ln(a) to bring the exponent down. Differentiate both sides. Left side is (1/y)(dy/dx) by chain rule. Apply the product rule to sin(x)¬∑ln(x¬≤+1): (sin x)‚Äô¬∑ln(x¬≤+1) + sin(x)¬∑(ln(x¬≤+1))‚Äô. Use d/dx[ln(x¬≤+1)] = 2x/(x¬≤+1). Equate. Multiply both sides by y. Substitute y = (x¬≤+1)^(sin x) back in. \text{Differentiate } f(x) = e^{2x} \ln(3x). \text{Find all critical points of } f(x) = x\,e^{-x}. \text{Use logarithmic differentiation to find } \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{(x+1)^2(x^2+1)^3}{(x-1)^4}\right].

documents.title: Implicit Differentiation
documents.subtitle: Finding dy/dx when y cannot be (or is not) solved for explicitly ‚ÄĒ the chain rule in disguise
documents.tags: implicit differentiation implicit function dy/dx circle folium of Descartes chain rule tangent line to curve
documents.content: The key insight of implicit differentiation is deceptively simple: y is a function of x. Even if we have not solved for y explicitly ‚ÄĒ even if solving for y is impossible ‚ÄĒ we can still treat y as if it is some function y(x). We just do not know which function it is. That is fine, because the chain rule tells us how to differentiate any expression involving y with respect to x, regardless of what y's explicit formula might be. The chain rule says: to differentiate a function of y with respect to x, differentiate with respect to y first, then multiply by dy/dx. This is because y is the inner function and x is the outer variable. So d/dx[y¬≤] = 2y¬∑(dy/dx), not just 2y. And d/dx[sin(y)] = cos(y)¬∑(dy/dx). And d/dx[e^y] = e^y¬∑(dy/dx). In each case, we treat y as a function of x and apply the chain rule. The procedure is clean and algorithmic. Step 1: Write down the equation. Step 2: Differentiate both sides with respect to x, applying the chain rule to every y-expression. Step 3: Gather all terms containing dy/dx on one side of the equation. Step 4: Factor out dy/dx. Step 5: Divide to isolate dy/dx. The result expresses dy/dx in terms of both x and y ‚ÄĒ which is perfectly fine, since dy/dx is the slope of the curve at a specific point (x, y). Why does dy/dx involve both x and y? Because the slope of an implicit curve at a point depends on where you are on the curve, and to specify a location on a curve like a circle you need both coordinates. At the point (3, 4) on x¬≤+y¬≤=25, the slope is different from the slope at (4, 3), even though both x and y values are the same set of numbers. The tangent line to an implicit curve at a point (x‚āÄ, y‚āÄ) is found the same way as always: evaluate dy/dx at (x‚āÄ, y‚āÄ) to get the slope m, then use the point-slope formula y - y‚āÄ = m(x - x‚āÄ). The only new subtlety is that dy/dx may involve both x and y, so we must substitute both coordinates to get a number. The normal line to a curve at a point is perpendicular to the tangent line. If the tangent has slope m, the normal has slope -1/m. Normal lines appear in optics (reflections) and in differential geometry (evolutes and involutes). The formal basis for implicit differentiation is the Implicit Function Theorem (IFT), one of the fundamental theorems of multivariable calculus. We state it here without proof, as its full proof requires techniques from several variables calculus. The Implicit Function Theorem says: suppose F(x, y) = 0 defines a curve near a point (a, b) where F_y(a, b) ‚Č† 0 (the partial derivative of F with respect to y is nonzero). Then near (a, b), this equation implicitly defines y as a differentiable function of x, and dy/dx = -F_x(a, b) / F_y(a, b). The condition F_y ‚Č† 0 is exactly what prevents the tangent line from being vertical ‚ÄĒ if F_y = 0, the tangent would be vertical (infinite slope), and the equation would not define y as a function of x near that point. For second-order implicit derivatives, we differentiate the first implicit derivative dy/dx (which is an expression in x and y) with respect to x again, using the quotient rule and chain rule, and substituting the first derivative result wherever dy/dx appears. The full Implicit Function Theorem in two variables states: Let F(x, y) be a function with continuous partial derivatives near a point (a, b). If F(a, b) = 0 and the partial derivative F_y(a, b) ‚Č† 0, then there exists an open interval I containing a and a unique differentiable function y = g(x) defined on I such that g(a) = b and F(x, g(x)) = 0 for all x in I. Moreover, g'(x) = -F_x(x, g(x)) / F_y(x, g(x)). The condition F_y(a, b) ‚Č† 0 is essential. It rules out points where the tangent line would be vertical. At such a point, y is NOT a function of x near (a, b) ‚ÄĒ the curve doubles back on itself. However, at such points, x IS a function of y, and we could differentiate implicitly in the other direction to find dx/dy. The proof uses the Contraction Mapping Theorem (or Banach Fixed Point Theorem) to construct the implicit function g, and then uses the chain rule to derive the formula for g'. The proof is usually covered in a course on real analysis or advanced calculus. For our purposes, the practical consequence is: implicit differentiation as a procedure is rigorously justified at every point (x‚āÄ, y‚āÄ) on the curve where dy/dx is finite (i.e., where the tangent is not vertical). Whenever we carry out implicit differentiation and the algebra produces a well-defined dy/dx, we are on solid theoretical ground. Circle: dy/dx and Tangent Line dy/dx = -x/y. At (3, 4), the tangent line is y = -(3/4)x + 25/4. Notice that the radius to (3, 4) has slope 4/3, and the tangent has slope -3/4 ‚ÄĒ these are negative reciprocals, confirming that the tangent is perpendicular to the radius (as expected for a circle). Differentiate both sides of the equation with respect to x. The right side, a constant, gives 0. Differentiate term by term on the left: d/dx[x¬≤] = 2x. For d/dx[y¬≤], apply the chain rule: the outer is u¬≤ (giving 2u = 2y), and the inner is y(x) (giving dy/dx). So d/dx[y¬≤] = 2y¬∑(dy/dx). Move the 2x term to the right side by subtracting it from both sides. Divide both sides by 2y to isolate dy/dx. Substitute the point (3, 4) into dy/dx = -x/y to get the slope at that specific point. Use point-slope form with point (3, 4) and slope -3/4. Simplify: 9/4 + 4 = 9/4 + 16/4 = 25/4. Folium of Descartes dy/dx = (2y - x¬≤)/(y¬≤ - 2x). This equals 0 when 2y = x¬≤, giving horizontal tangents, and is undefined when y¬≤ = 2x, giving vertical tangents. The folium is a beautiful curve with a double point at the origin. Differentiate both sides with respect to x. The right side is a product: 6 times x times y. Left side: d/dx[x¬≥] = 3x¬≤, d/dx[y¬≥] = 3y¬≤(dy/dx) by chain rule. Right side needs the product rule. Apply the product rule to xy: (x)'(y) + (x)(y)'= 1¬∑y + x¬∑(dy/dx). Remember y is a function of x. Substitute the product rule result into the equation. Gather all dy/dx terms on the left: subtract 6x(dy/dx) from both sides, and move 6y to the right. Factor dy/dx from the left side. Divide both sides by (3y¬≤ - 6x), then simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by 3. Implicit Differentiation with Sine and Product dy/dx = (1 - y cos(xy)) / (x cos(xy)). This is valid wherever x cos(xy) ‚Č† 0. Differentiate both sides with respect to x. Left side: chain rule on sin(u) where u = xy gives cos(xy)¬∑d/dx[xy]. Right side: d/dx[x] = 1. Apply the product rule to xy: (x)'y + x(y)' = y + x(dy/dx). Substitute the product rule result. Distribute cos(xy) to both terms. Move the y cos(xy) term to the right side. Divide both sides by x cos(xy) to isolate dy/dx. Implicit Differentiation with Exponential dy/dx = -y/(e^y + x). This is valid wherever e^y + x ‚Č† 0. Differentiate both sides. d/dx[e^y]: chain rule gives e^y ¬∑ (dy/dx). d/dx[3] = 0. Apply the product rule to xy: y + x(dy/dx). Factor dy/dx from the first and third terms: (e^y + x)(dy/dx). Move y to the right. Divide both sides by (e^y + x). Second Implicit Derivative d¬≤y/dx¬≤ = -r¬≤/y¬≥. The negative sign indicates the circle is concave down in the upper half (y > 0) and concave up in the lower half (y < 0). We already computed the first derivative in Example 1 (with r replacing 5): dy/dx = -x/y. The second derivative is the derivative of the first derivative with respect to x. Apply the quotient rule to x/y: (numerator derivative ¬∑ denominator - numerator ¬∑ denominator derivative) / denominator¬≤. (Then apply the negative sign.) d/dx[x] = 1 and d/dx[y] = dy/dx. Substitute dy/dx = -x/y (the first derivative we already know). Simplify: -x ¬∑ (-x/y) = x¬≤/y. Combine the numerator into a single fraction: y + x¬≤/y = (y¬≤+x¬≤)/y. Divide by y¬≤. Use x¬≤ + y¬≤ = r¬≤ (the original equation) to replace x¬≤+y¬≤ with r¬≤. y = x^x via Implicit Differentiation dy/dx = x^x(1 + ln x), confirming the result from logarithmic differentiation in the previous lesson. This is implicit differentiation applied after taking ln of both sides ‚ÄĒ these two techniques are really the same method. We take ln of both sides to make the exponent algebraically accessible. Apply ln: ln(x^x) = x¬∑ln(x) by the logarithm power rule. Differentiate both sides with respect to x. Left side: chain rule on ln(y) gives (1/y)(dy/dx), because y is the inner function. Product rule on the right side: (x)‚Äô(ln x) + (x)(ln x)‚Äô = ln x + x¬∑(1/x) = ln x + 1. Equate. Multiply by y and substitute y = x^x. Tangent and Normal to an Implicit Curve Tangent line: y = -(4/7)x + 15/7. Normal line: y = (7/4)x - 5/2. The two lines are perpendicular at (2, 1), as required. Always check that the given point satisfies the equation before proceeding. Differentiate both sides with respect to x. Differentiate term by term on the left; right side gives 0. Apply the product rule to x¬≤y: (x¬≤)‚Äôy + x¬≤(y)‚Äô = 2xy + x¬≤(dy/dx). Apply the chain rule to y¬≥: 3y¬≤(dy/dx). Move the 2xy term to the right side. Factor dy/dx from the left side. Isolate dy/dx. Substitute x = 2, y = 1 to find the slope at our point. Tangent line in point-slope form, then slope-intercept. The normal line is perpendicular to the tangent, so its slope is the negative reciprocal of -4/7. Normal line in point-slope form, then slope-intercept. \text{Find } dy/dx \text{ for } 3x^2 + 2xy + y^2 = 7. \text{Find all points on } x^2 + xy + y^2 = 3 \text{ where the tangent line is horizontal.} \text{For the astroid } x^{2/3} + y^{2/3} = 4, \text{ find } dy/dx \text{ and find the equation of the tangent line at the point } (2\sqrt{2},\, 2\sqrt{2}).

documents.title: Related Rates
documents.subtitle: When two quantities are geometrically linked, their rates of change are linked too ‚ÄĒ by the chain rule
documents.tags: related rates chain rule implicit differentiation Pythagorean theorem geometry time derivatives optimization preview
documents.content: The key insight behind all related rates problems is this: two quantities are linked by a geometric or physical relationship, and that relationship does not just constrain the values ‚ÄĒ it also constrains the rates of change. If the position of the base of a ladder is x(t) and the position of the top is y(t), the Pythagorean theorem says x¬≤ + y¬≤ = L¬≤ (where L is the fixed ladder length). This equation holds for every value of t, not just one particular instant. It is an identity in t, valid throughout the motion. Because x¬≤ + y¬≤ = L¬≤ holds for all t, we can differentiate both sides with respect to t. The right side is constant, so its derivative is 0. The left side requires the chain rule: d/dt[x¬≤] = 2x¬∑(dx/dt) and d/dt[y¬≤] = 2y¬∑(dy/dt). So we get 2x(dx/dt) + 2y(dy/dt) = 0. This single equation is the "rate equation" ‚ÄĒ it links dx/dt and dy/dt at every moment. Given one rate, you can solve for the other. The chain rule is the engine driving every related rates calculation. Whenever you differentiate a function of a variable that itself depends on t, you must multiply by the derivative of that variable with respect to t. This is d/dt[f(x(t))] = f'(x)¬∑(dx/dt). In the ladder problem, x is a function of t, so d/dt[x¬≤] = 2x¬∑(dx/dt) ‚ÄĒ you cannot just write 2x. The (dx/dt) factor is essential because x itself is changing in time. Walk through the ladder problem conceptually to build intuition before any algebra. When the base is very close to the wall (x ‚Čą 0), the ladder is nearly vertical, and a small horizontal motion of the base produces almost no downward motion of the top. As the base slides further out, the geometry becomes more extreme: the top drops faster and faster. When the base is at 45¬į from the wall (x = y = L/‚ąö2), the top drops at exactly the same speed the base slides out. And as the base approaches L (the ladder is almost flat), the top plummets toward the floor at infinite speed ‚ÄĒ the constraint collapses to a degenerate triangle. The rate equation 2x(dx/dt) + 2y(dy/dt) = 0 captures all of this: dy/dt = -(x/y)¬∑(dx/dt), and as y ‚Üí 0 this ratio blows up. Radar tracking works by the same logic. An aircraft is at horizontal distance x(t) from the radar station and at altitude h (constant for simplicity). The slant range r = ‚ąö(x¬≤ + h¬≤). Differentiating: dr/dt = x/r ¬∑ (dx/dt). So dr/dt ‚ÄĒ the rate the range changes ‚ÄĒ depends on the current angle of depression, not just the aircraft's ground speed dx/dt. A slow plane flying directly toward the radar may have a very fast dr/dt when it is nearly overhead; a fast plane flying perpendicular to the line of sight may have dr/dt = 0. Air traffic controllers account for exactly this effect. The five-step procedure for related rates is reliable in every situation. Step 1: Draw and label a diagram. Put variables (not numbers) on all the changing quantities. Step 2: Write the equation that relates those variables ‚ÄĒ this is the geometric or physical constraint. Step 3: Differentiate both sides with respect to t, applying the chain rule to every variable-dependent term. Step 4: Substitute in the known numerical values at the specific instant ‚ÄĒ both positions and rates. Step 5: Solve algebraically for the unknown rate. The crucial discipline is in Step 4: you must differentiate FIRST, then substitute. Substituting before differentiating destroys the rate information. The most general setup uses the multivariable chain rule. If a quantity Q depends on several variables x, y, z that all vary with time t, then dQ/dt = (‚ąāQ/‚ąāx)(dx/dt) + (‚ąāQ/‚ąāy)(dy/dt) + (‚ąāQ/‚ąāz)(dz/dt). This is the chain rule applied to a function of multiple time-dependent inputs. In most Chapter 3 problems, only one or two variables change at a time, simplifying the computation considerably. For the volume of a sphere: V = (4/3)ŌÄr¬≥. Differentiating with respect to t: dV/dt = 4ŌÄr¬≤¬∑(dr/dt). The factor 4ŌÄr¬≤ is the surface area of the sphere ‚ÄĒ this is not a coincidence. The rate of volume increase equals the surface area times the rate of radius increase, because new volume is being added in a thin spherical shell of thickness dr/dt¬∑dt. This geometric interpretation deepens understanding: the rate equation is a statement about geometry, not just algebra. For a cone with fixed proportional dimensions: if the radius r and height h satisfy r/h = k (a fixed ratio from the tank's geometry), then r = kh and V = (1/3)ŌÄr¬≤h = (1/3)ŌÄ(kh)¬≤h = (ŌÄk¬≤/3)h¬≥. Now dV/dt = ŌÄk¬≤h¬≤¬∑(dh/dt). The simplification r = kh is crucial ‚ÄĒ it reduces the problem from two variables (r, h) to one variable (h), making differentiation immediate. This substitution must happen before differentiation, using the geometric constraint to eliminate a variable, not to substitute a specific number. Shadow problems use similar triangles. A streetlight of height H stands at the origin. A person of height h walks away from the base at speed dx/dt = v. Let x be the person's distance and s be their shadow length. The similar triangles give H/(x+s) = h/s (the light, tip of shadow, and top of person form similar triangles). Solving: Hs = h(x+s), so s(H-h) = hx, giving s = hx/(H-h). Therefore ds/dt = h/(H-h)¬∑(dx/dt). Notice the shadow length increases at a constant rate (since ds/dt depends only on dx/dt and fixed constants) ‚ÄĒ the person and their shadow accelerate apart at a constant rate, regardless of how far from the light post they are. The general geometric relationships and their rate forms are: Circle area A = ŌÄr¬≤ ‚Üí dA/dt = 2ŌÄr¬∑(dr/dt). Sphere volume V = (4/3)ŌÄr¬≥ ‚Üí dV/dt = 4ŌÄr¬≤¬∑(dr/dt). Cone volume V = (1/3)ŌÄr¬≤h ‚Üí dV/dt = (2ŌÄrh/3)¬∑(dr/dt) + (ŌÄr¬≤/3)¬∑(dh/dt). Pythagorean theorem x¬≤ + y¬≤ = L¬≤ ‚Üí 2x(dx/dt) + 2y(dy/dt) = 0 (when L is constant). These rate equations are the toolkit; the problem-specific geometry tells you which constraints apply and what substitutions to make. The theoretical foundation of related rates is the chain rule for functions of several variables. Suppose x(t) and y(t) are both differentiable at t‚āÄ, and F(x, y) is a function with continuous partial derivatives. Then the composite function h(t) = F(x(t), y(t)) is differentiable at t‚āÄ, and h'(t‚āÄ) = (‚ąāF/‚ąāx)¬∑x'(t‚āÄ) + (‚ąāF/‚ąāy)¬∑y'(t‚āÄ). This is a theorem from multivariable calculus, but in single-variable settings (where F depends on only one variable at a time), it reduces to the familiar chain rule d/dt[f(x(t))] = f'(x)¬∑x'(t). For the chain rule to apply, we need the geometric constraint F(x(t), y(t)) = C to hold for all t in some open interval around t‚āÄ, not just at the single instant t‚āÄ. This is a continuity requirement: the relationship must be maintained throughout the motion. In the ladder problem, x¬≤ + y¬≤ = 100 holds for all t from the moment the ladder starts moving until it hits the floor. We can differentiate it with respect to t at any interior point of that interval. The singularity in the ladder problem deserves careful attention. The rate equation is dy/dt = -(x/y)¬∑(dx/dt). As y ‚Üí 0‚Āļ, dy/dt ‚Üí -‚ąě for any fixed positive dx/dt. This is not a mathematical artifact ‚ÄĒ it reflects a real physical breakdown. As the ladder approaches horizontal, the constraint equation x¬≤ + y¬≤ = L¬≤ becomes increasingly ill-conditioned: small changes in x (near L) produce disproportionately large changes in y (near 0). The same phenomenon appears in the well-known "barn door" problem in mechanics and in the kinematics of robotic arms near singular configurations. A subtlety often overlooked: the rate equation 2x(dx/dt) + 2y(dy/dt) = 0 was derived by differentiating a constraint that assumed L is constant. If the ladder were also changing length (say, telescoping), the right side would not be zero ‚ÄĒ it would be 2L(dL/dt). The related rates method always differentiates the constraint as it actually holds, accounting for any quantities that are truly constant versus those that vary. Identifying which quantities are constant is therefore the first critical step in every problem. Ladder Sliding Down a Wall The top of the ladder slides down at 3/2 ft/s when the base is 6 ft from the wall. The ratio -(x/y) = -(6/8) = -3/4 shows that dy/dt = (3/4)¬∑(-dx/dt) ‚ÄĒ the top slides slower than the base at this configuration. When x = y = 5‚ąö2 (the 45¬į configuration), dy/dt = -dx/dt exactly. The Pythagorean theorem: base x, height y, and the fixed 10-ft hypotenuse. This holds for all t. Differentiate both sides with respect to t. The right side is 0 because 100 is constant. The chain rule gives the factor dx/dt and dy/dt on each term. Find y at the instant x = 6 using the original equation. We must know the current position of BOTH variables before substituting rates. Substitute x = 6, y = 8, and dx/dt = 2 into the rate equation. Simplify the left side. Solve for dy/dt. The negative sign means y is decreasing ‚ÄĒ the top slides DOWN. At this moment the top drops at 3/2 ft/s while the base slides out at 2 ft/s ‚ÄĒ the top moves slower than the base because the ladder is more horizontal than vertical (x > y). Inflating Spherical Balloon When r = 5 cm, the radius grows at 1/ŌÄ ‚Čą 0.318 cm/s. As the balloon inflates, r increases, so dr/dt = dV/dt/(4ŌÄr¬≤) decreases ‚ÄĒ a fixed rate of air input inflates the balloon more slowly as it gets larger, because the same volume of air produces a thinner shell. This is why it's harder to blow up a large balloon than a small one. Volume of a sphere. r and V are both functions of t. Differentiate both sides with respect to t. d/dt[(4/3)ŌÄr¬≥] = (4/3)ŌÄ¬∑3r¬≤¬∑(dr/dt) = 4ŌÄr¬≤¬∑(dr/dt). The factor 4ŌÄr¬≤ is the surface area ‚ÄĒ new volume is added in a thin surface layer. Substitute dV/dt = 100 and r = 5. Simplify: 4ŌÄ¬∑25 = 100ŌÄ. Solve for dr/dt. The radius grows at 1/ŌÄ cm/s at this moment. Water Draining from a Conical Tank The water level drops at 8/(9ŌÄ) ‚Čą 0.283 m/min when h = 2 m. Notice that as h decreases, dh/dt = -32/(9ŌÄh¬≤) would increase in magnitude ‚ÄĒ the water level drops faster as the cone narrows near the vertex, because each meter drop corresponds to less volume lost. Similar triangles: the tank has radius 3 when height is 4. At any water height h, the water surface radius r satisfies r/h = 3/4. This is the key geometric constraint that reduces two variables to one. Substitute r = 3h/4 into the cone volume formula. Now V is a function of h alone ‚ÄĒ a single-variable relationship. Differentiate V = 3ŌÄh¬≥/16 with respect to t. d/dt[3ŌÄh¬≥/16] = (9ŌÄh¬≤/16)¬∑(dh/dt). Substitute dV/dt = -2 (negative because draining) and h = 2. Simplify: 9ŌÄ¬∑4/16 = 9ŌÄ/4. Solve for dh/dt. The water level drops at 8/(9ŌÄ) m/min. Shadow Length Problem The shadow's tip moves at 50/7 ‚Čą 7.14 ft/s, faster than the person's 5 ft/s walking speed. The shadow stretches as the person walks, so the tip moves even faster than the walker. This rate is constant regardless of how far from the post ‚ÄĒ a consequence of the linear similar-triangles relationship. Define variables. Both x and s are functions of t. The tip of the shadow is at distance x + s from the base. Similar triangles: the streetlight, the tip of the shadow, and the person's top form similar triangles. The full triangle (from base to shadow tip, height 20) is similar to the small triangle (from person to shadow tip, height 6). Cross-multiply and simplify. This gives the constraint between s and x. Solve for s in terms of x. Differentiate both sides with respect to t. Since 3/7 is constant, ds/dt = (3/7)¬∑(dx/dt). The shadow length grows at a constant 15/7 ft/s. The tip moves at the person's speed plus the shadow growth rate. The shadow tip outpaces the person. Angle of Elevation of a Rising Balloon The angle of elevation increases at 1/100 rad/s when the balloon is at 500 ft. As the balloon continues to rise, őł approaches ŌÄ/2 and sec¬≤(őł) ‚Üí ‚ąě, which drives dőł/dt ‚Üí 0 for fixed dh/dt ‚ÄĒ the angle barely changes when the balloon is nearly overhead because the geometry is almost degenerate. The angle of elevation őł satisfies tan(őł) = opposite/adjacent = h/500. Both őł and h vary with t. Differentiate both sides with respect to t. d/dt[tan(őł)] = sec¬≤(őł)¬∑(dőł/dt) by the chain rule. The right side: d/dt[h/500] = (1/500)¬∑(dh/dt). At h = 500 ft, tan(őł) = 500/500 = 1, so őł = ŌÄ/4 (45¬į). Compute sec¬≤(ŌÄ/4). cos(ŌÄ/4) = 1/‚ąö2, so cos¬≤(ŌÄ/4) = 1/2, so sec¬≤(ŌÄ/4) = 2. Substitute sec¬≤(őł) = 2 and dh/dt = 10 into the rate equation. Solve for dőł/dt. The angle increases at 1/100 radian per second at this moment ‚ÄĒ about 0.57¬į/s. Two Cars Approaching an Intersection The cars are closing distance at ‚Čą96.9 mph. Note that this is NOT simply 60 + 80 = 140 mph ‚ÄĒ that would be the rate if they were driving toward each other on a straight road. Because they are on perpendicular roads, the geometry moderates the closure rate through the 5-12-13 triangle. Let x = Car B's distance from intersection, y = Car A's distance, z = distance between cars. The cars are on perpendicular roads, so the Pythagorean theorem applies. Differentiate both sides with respect to t. Compute z using the Pythagorean theorem. The 5-12-13 right triangle gives z = 13. Both cars approach the intersection, so their distances decrease: dx/dt = -80 mph (B moves east toward intersection), dy/dt = -60 mph (A moves north toward intersection). Substitute all values into the rate equation. Simplify the right side. The negative sign means the cars are getting closer. They approach each other at about 96.9 mph. Spreading Circular Oil Slick The radius grows at 5/(2ŌÄ) ‚Čą 0.796 m/hr when r = 10 m. As the slick spreads, r increases, and the same rate of area increase produces a smaller rate of radius increase ‚ÄĒ the circle spreads more slowly in radial terms the larger it gets. Area of a circle. Both A and r depend on t. Differentiate both sides with respect to t. d/dt[ŌÄr¬≤] = 2ŌÄr¬∑(dr/dt). Substitute dA/dt = 50 and r = 10. Solve for dr/dt. A kite flies at constant height 100 m. The string is let out at 5 m/s. Find the rate the horizontal distance from the flier increases when 200 m of string has been let out. Gravel is dumped onto a conical pile at 30 ft¬≥/min. The cone always satisfies h = 2r (height equals twice the base radius). Find dh/dt when h = 10 ft. A boat is pulled toward a dock by a rope passing over a pulley 12 ft above the water. The rope is pulled in at 3 ft/s. How fast is the boat moving toward the dock when 15 ft of rope extends from the pulley?

documents.title: Linear Approximation & Differentials
documents.subtitle: Zoom in on any smooth curve far enough and it becomes a straight line ‚ÄĒ and that line is enormously useful
documents.tags: linear approximation linearization differentials tangent line error analysis Taylor polynomial sensitivity
documents.content: Start with the central geometric fact: if you zoom into a differentiable function f at a point x = a, the curve eventually becomes indistinguishable from a straight line ‚ÄĒ the tangent line. This is not just a visual trick; it is the precise meaning of differentiability. A function is differentiable at a if and only if it "looks linear" at infinitely high magnification at a. The tangent line L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a) is the unique line that matches both the function value and the slope of f at the point (a, f(a)). Because the curve and the tangent line agree in both value and direction at x = a, they stay close to each other for x near a. Think of it as replacing f with its tangent line locally. The linearization (also called the linear approximation) of f at x = a is L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a). When you use L(x) as a surrogate for f(x) near a, you are making an approximation error f(x) - L(x). By Taylor's theorem, this error is approximately f''(a)(x-a)¬≤/2 ‚ÄĒ it grows like the square of the distance from a. This means the error is O((x-a)¬≤): if you halve the distance from a, you quarter the error. For x very close to a, the linear approximation is astonishingly accurate. To estimate ‚ąö26 without a calculator, choose a nearby perfect square: a = 25, f(x) = ‚ąöx, f(a) = 5. Compute f'(x) = 1/(2‚ąöx), so f'(25) = 1/10. The linearization is L(x) = 5 + (1/10)(x - 25). At x = 26: L(26) = 5 + (1/10)(1) = 5.1. The actual value is ‚ąö26 ‚Čą 5.0990..., so the error is about 0.001 ‚ÄĒ less than two parts in ten thousand. For ‚ąö24: L(24) = 5 + (1/10)(-1) = 4.9. Actual: ‚ąö24 ‚Čą 4.899... Error ‚Čą 0.001. The linear approximation is symmetric and equally accurate on both sides. Error analysis is built into the linear approximation framework through differentials. If y = f(x), the differential dy is defined as dy = f'(x)¬∑dx. Here dx represents a small change in x (not necessarily infinitesimal ‚ÄĒ just small), and dy is the corresponding change in y along the TANGENT LINE. This is different from őĒy, the actual change in f: őĒy = f(x + dx) - f(x). The linear approximation says őĒy ‚Čą dy, with error őĒy - dy = f''(a)(dx)¬≤/2 + higher-order terms. Engineers use dy to estimate how errors in measurements propagate to errors in computed quantities. The differential formalism provides a powerful tool for sensitivity analysis. If you measure a physical quantity x with absolute error ¬Īőīx, the resulting error in f(x) is approximately |f'(x)|¬∑őīx. The relative error in f(x) is approximately |f'(x)/f(x)|¬∑őīx = |(d/dx)[ln f(x)]|¬∑őīx. For the volume of a sphere, V = (4/3)ŌÄr¬≥, the relative error is 3¬∑(őīr/r) ‚ÄĒ a 1% error in radius measurement produces about a 3% error in calculated volume. This factor of 3 comes directly from the power in the formula, and the linear approximation makes it quantitative. The linearizations of the standard functions at x = 0 are the most important special cases.
