Near x = 0: sin(x) ‚Čą x (since sin(0) = 0 and cos(0) = 1). cos(x) ‚Čą 1 (since cos(0) = 1 and -sin(0) = 0). e^x ‚Čą 1 + x (since e^0 = 1 and e^0 = 1). ln(1+x) ‚Čą x (since ln(1) = 0 and 1/(1+0) = 1). (1+x)^n ‚Čą 1 + nx (by the binomial theorem / chain rule). These approximations are valid for |x| << 1 and appear constantly in physics, engineering, and probability. The simple pendulum formula T = 2ŌÄ‚ąö(L/g) relies on sin(őł) ‚Čą őł ‚ÄĒ the small-angle approximation. The formal definition: the linearization of f at x = a is L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a). We say f(x) ‚Čą L(x) for x near a. The approximation is exact at x = a (since L(a) = f(a)) and has the correct slope there (since L'(a) = f'(a)). No other linear function matches both simultaneously ‚ÄĒ the linearization is the unique best linear approximation to f near a. Differentials formalize the linearization algebraically. If y = f(x), define the differential dy = f'(x)¬∑dx, where dx is any real number (the "change in x"). Then dy is the corresponding change in y along the tangent line. The actual change in y is őĒy = f(x + dx) - f(x). The linear approximation says őĒy ‚Čą dy, with error őĒy - dy = f''(x)(dx)¬≤/2 + O((dx)¬≥). In Leibniz notation, df = f'(x)¬∑dx ‚ÄĒ this is the definition of the differential df, and it makes Leibniz's notation dy/dx = f'(x) look like a ratio (dy divided by dx) even though it is defined as a limit. Taylor's theorem provides the error bound: |f(x) - L(x)| ‚Č§ M|x-a|¬≤/2, where M = max_{t ‚ąą [a,x]} |f''(t)|. This bound shows the error is quadratic in the distance |x-a|. The bound is tight in general: if f''(a) ‚Č† 0, then f(x) - L(x) ‚Čą f''(a)(x-a)¬≤/2 near a. To achieve error less than őĶ in the approximation, we need |x-a| < ‚ąö(2őĶ/M). Relative error vs. absolute error: the absolute error in using L(x) for f(x) is |f(x) - L(x)|. The relative error is |f(x) - L(x)| / |f(x)|. For many engineering applications, relative error matters more ‚ÄĒ a 1% error in a large quantity may be acceptable, while a 1% error in a safety-critical small quantity may not be. The differential formalism handles both: absolute error ‚Čą |dy| = |f'(x)|¬∑|dx|, relative error ‚Čą |dy/y| = |f'(x)/f(x)|¬∑|dx|. The standard linearizations at x = 0 (where f(0) = 0 or 1 as appropriate): sin(x) ‚Čą x, cos(x) ‚Čą 1, tan(x) ‚Čą x, e^x ‚Čą 1+x, ln(1+x) ‚Čą x, (1+x)^n ‚Čą 1+nx, ‚ąö(1+x) ‚Čą 1+x/2, 1/(1+x) ‚Čą 1-x. These follow from the general formula L(x) = f(0) + f'(0)¬∑x with the appropriate f and f'. These approximations form the foundation of perturbation theory in physics ‚ÄĒ they allow analysts to solve nonlinear problems by treating deviations from a reference state as small. The precise statement of differentiability in terms of linear approximation: f is differentiable at a if and only if there exists a number m (the derivative f'(a)) such that f(x) = f(a) + m(x-a) + őĶ(x)(x-a), where őĶ(x) ‚Üí 0 as x ‚Üí a. This is an alternative (and equivalent) definition of the derivative. The function őĶ(x) measures the "error per unit distance from a" in the linear approximation ‚ÄĒ saying őĶ(x) ‚Üí 0 means the linear approximation is asymptotically exact near a, not just approximately correct. Proof that the tangent line is the unique best linear approximation: suppose two lines L‚āĀ(x) = f(a) + m‚āĀ(x-a) and L‚āā(x) = f(a) + m‚āā(x-a) both satisfy lim_{x‚Üía} [f(x) - LŠĶĘ(x)]/(x-a) = 0. Then lim_{x‚Üía} [L‚āĀ(x) - L‚āā(x)]/(x-a) = lim (m‚āĀ-m‚āā) = m‚āĀ - m‚āā = 0, so m‚āĀ = m‚āā. There is only one value of the slope m for which the error is o(x-a), and that value is f'(a). This justifies calling L the "best" linear approximation. Taylor's theorem (first-order version with Lagrange remainder): if f is twice differentiable on [a, x], then f(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a) + f''(c)(x-a)¬≤/2 for some c strictly between a and x. The term f''(c)(x-a)¬≤/2 is called the Lagrange remainder. It shows the error is not just O((x-a)¬≤) in order ‚ÄĒ it equals (x-a)¬≤/2 times the second derivative at some interior point c. This is the quantitative error bound that engineers use to certify approximations. The connection to complex analysis: a function of a complex variable is differentiable (in the complex sense, i.e., holomorphic) if and only if it is locally linear in the complex sense ‚ÄĒ C-linear, not just R-linear. This is a much stronger condition than real differentiability and implies that the function is automatically infinitely differentiable and equals its Taylor series everywhere in its domain. The simple real idea of linear approximation extends to the foundations of complex function theory. Linearize ‚ąöx at a = 25 The linearization L(x) = 5 + (x-25)/10 estimates square roots near 25 with error less than 0.001 for x within 1 unit of 25. Farther from a = 25, the error grows: L(29) = 5.4, but ‚ąö29 ‚Čą 5.385 ‚ÄĒ now the error is 0.015. The quadratic error bound |error| ‚Č§ M(x-25)¬≤/2 with M = max|f''| = max|{-1/(4x^{3/2})}| ‚Čą 1/500 at x near 25 gives bound (1/500)(4)/2 = 0.004 for x = 29 ‚ÄĒ a conservative but valid bound. Evaluate the function at the base point a = 25. Compute the derivative and evaluate at a = 25. Write the linearization L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a). Estimate ‚ąö26. Error ‚Čą 0.001. Estimate ‚ąö24. Error ‚Čą 0.001. Estimate ‚ąö25.1. Error < 0.0001 ‚ÄĒ much better for smaller displacement. Linearize sin(x) at a = 0 sin(x) ‚Čą x near 0 (small-angle approximation). Near ŌÄ/2, sin(x) ‚Čą 1 (the function is at a maximum with zero derivative). The accuracy of the linear approximation depends both on the distance from a and the magnitude of f'' at the base point ‚ÄĒ at a maximum, f'' = -1, so the quadratic correction is significant even for small displacements. Evaluate f and f' at a = 0. The linearization of sin(x) at 0 is simply L(x) = x. This is the famous "small-angle approximation." Estimate sin(0.1). Error ‚Čą 0.00017 ‚ÄĒ excellent for 0.1 radians (‚Čą5.7¬į). Choose a base point closer to ŌÄ/2 + 0.1 for better accuracy. Evaluate at the new base point a = ŌÄ/2. The linearization at a = ŌÄ/2 is just the constant 1. Estimate: 1. Actual: ‚Čą 0.995. Error ‚Čą 0.005. The zero-slope tangent is less accurate here. Linearize e^x at a = 0 e^x ‚Čą 1 + x for small x is the cornerstone of many calculations in finance, physics, and statistics. The relative error is approximately x¬≤/2 (from the quadratic term e^x ‚Čą 1 + x + x¬≤/2 + ...), so the approximation 1 + x improves rapidly as x ‚Üí 0. The exponential function equals its own derivative everywhere. At a = 0: f(0) = 1 and f'(0) = 1. Linearization of e^x at 0 is L(x) = 1 + x. This is one of the most important approximations in mathematics. Estimate e^0.2. Error ‚Čą 0.021 (1.7% relative error). Decent for x = 0.2. Estimate e^0.01. Error ‚Čą 0.00005 (0.005% relative error). Excellent for x = 0.01. Differentials: Comparing őĒy and dy The differential dy = 1.0 approximates the actual őĒy = 1.061 with error 0.061. The relative error is about 5.8% ‚ÄĒ noticeable because dx = 0.1 is not very small and f''(x) = 6x is large (= 12 at x = 2). For dx = 0.01, the error would be about 0.006 (squared factor) ‚ÄĒ much smaller. Differentiate y with respect to x. Evaluate the derivative at x = 2. The differential dy = f'(x)¬∑dx gives the change along the tangent line. Current function value at x = 2. Exact function value at x = 2.1. The actual change in y. The differential underestimates the actual change. The error is (dx)¬≤ times f''(x)/2 ‚Čą 6¬∑(0.1)¬≤/2 = 0.03 (slightly off because we're not exactly at x=2 for the second derivative). Error Propagation: Volume of a Sphere A 0.5% error in radius (0.05 cm out of 10 cm) produces approximately a 1.5% error in volume ‚ÄĒ exactly 3√ó the relative radius error, as expected from the differential of r¬≥. This factor-of-exponent rule generalizes: if V = r^n, then dV/V = n¬∑(dr/r). Volume of a sphere. Derivative of V with respect to r (surface area). The differential dV gives the approximate error in V due to error dr in r. Substitute r = 10 and dr = ¬Ī0.05. The relative error in V is 3 times the relative error in r (0.5%). This factor of 3 comes from the exponent 3 in V = (4/3)ŌÄr¬≥. Linearize ln(x) at a = 1 ln(x) ‚Čą x - 1 near x = 1 (equivalently, ln(1+u) ‚Čą u for small u). The approximation has about 5% error for u = ¬Ī0.1. The error is ln(1+u) - u = -u¬≤/2 + u¬≥/3 - ... ‚Čą -u¬≤/2 for small u, so for u = 0.1 the error is about -0.005 ‚ÄĒ the linear approximation overestimates |ln(0.9)| and underestimates ln(1.1). Evaluate at a = 1. Linearization: L(x) = x - 1. Equivalently, ln(x) ‚Čą x - 1 near x = 1, or ln(1+u) ‚Čą u near u = 0. Estimate ln(1.1). Error ‚Čą 0.0047 ‚ÄĒ about 5% relative error. Estimate ln(0.9). Error ‚Čą 0.005 ‚ÄĒ about 5% relative error. Error in sin(30¬į) from a 2¬į Angle Error A 2¬į error in measuring a 30¬į angle produces about ¬Ī0.030 error in sin(30¬į), a 6% relative error. The factor cos(ŌÄ/6) ‚Čą 0.866 moderates the error ‚ÄĒ if the angle were near 0¬į, cos(őł) ‚Čą 1 and the error in sin(őł) would equal the error in őł in radians. Near 90¬į, cos(őł) ‚Čą 0 and the error in sin(őł) is negligible ‚ÄĒ the function is nearly flat. Convert to radians. Calculus must be done in radians ‚ÄĒ the derivative formulas for trig functions only hold in radian measure. The relevant derivative. Write the differential. Substitute őł = ŌÄ/6 and dőł = ŌÄ/90. A 2¬į error (6.67% relative error in angle) produces about 6% relative error in the sine value. Use linearization to estimate (1.001)^{1000}. [Hint: use (1+x)^n ‚Čą 1+nx near x = 0 with x = 0.001, n = 1000.] Prove: if f is differentiable at a, then f is continuous at a. Use the linear approximation definition of differentiability. The period of a pendulum is T = 2ŌÄ‚ąö(L/g). If L is measured as 1 m with error ¬Ī1 cm, find the approximate error in T. Take g = 9.8 m/s¬≤.

documents.title: The Mean Value Theorem
documents.subtitle: If your average speed was 75 mph, then at some moment your instantaneous speed was exactly 75 mph ‚ÄĒ and the proof uses only derivatives
documents.tags: mean value theorem Rolle's theorem average rate instantaneous rate increasing functions antiderivatives Cauchy MVT L'H√īpital preview
documents.content: The geometric picture makes the MVT obvious once you see it. Draw any smooth curve from point A = (a, f(a)) to point B = (b, f(b)). The secant line from A to B has slope m = (f(b) - f(a))/(b - a) ‚ÄĒ the average rate of change over the interval. Now imagine a horizontal line sliding up from below, parallel to the secant. At some point, this sliding line first touches the curve ‚ÄĒ at a tangent point. At that tangent point c, the tangent line is parallel to the secant, meaning f'(c) = m. That is the entire content of the Mean Value Theorem: somewhere between a and b, the instantaneous slope equals the average slope. Rolle's Theorem is the special case where f(a) = f(b) ‚ÄĒ the starting and ending values are equal. In this case, the secant is horizontal (slope 0), and the tangent must also be horizontal somewhere. Geometrically: if you start and end at the same height, the graph must have a horizontal tangent somewhere in between. A ball thrown upward eventually falls ‚ÄĒ it must stop (instantaneous velocity = 0) at the peak. A pendulum swinging back to its starting position must momentarily stop at the peak. Rolle's Theorem captures this intuition precisely. To prove the MVT from Rolle's Theorem, define a new function g(x) that subtracts the secant line from f: g(x) = f(x) - [f(a) + ((f(b)-f(a))/(b-a))¬∑(x-a)]. This g measures how far f is above the secant line. Note that g(a) = 0 and g(b) = 0 (f equals the secant at both endpoints). So g satisfies Rolle's hypotheses. Rolle's Theorem gives a point c in (a,b) with g'(c) = 0. But g'(x) = f'(x) - (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a), so g'(c) = 0 means f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). That is the MVT. The trick is simply to subtract the secant line to create a function that satisfies Rolle's hypothesis. The most important consequence of the MVT is the characterization of constant functions. If f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then the MVT applied to any two points x‚āĀ < x‚āā in (a,b) gives: f(x‚āā) - f(x‚āĀ) = f'(c)(x‚āā - x‚āĀ) = 0¬∑(x‚āā - x‚āĀ) = 0. So f(x‚āā) = f(x‚āĀ) for all pairs ‚ÄĒ f is constant. This theorem, which feels obvious, is actually non-trivial to prove without the MVT. It is the first theorem about antiderivatives: if F and G are both antiderivatives of f (F' = G' = f), then (F - G)' = 0, so F - G is constant, so F = G + C for some constant C. This is why every antiderivative has "+ C". The MVT also proves that positive derivative implies increasing function. If f'(x) > 0 for all x in (a,b), then for any x‚āĀ < x‚āā in (a,b), the MVT gives f(x‚āā) - f(x‚āĀ) = f'(c)(x‚āā - x‚āĀ) > 0 (since f'(c) > 0 and x‚āā - x‚āĀ > 0). So f(x‚āā) > f(x‚āĀ): f is increasing. Similarly, f'(x) < 0 everywhere means f is decreasing. This result, which seems geometrically obvious, is proved rigorously only through the MVT. History: the Mean Value Theorem was proved by Joseph-Louis Lagrange in 1797 in his landmark book Th√©orie des fonctions analytiques, where he attempted to put calculus on a rigorous footing without infinitesimals. Lagrange called it the th√©or√®me des accroissements finis (theorem of finite increments). Rolle's Theorem was proved 100 years earlier, in 1691, by Michel Rolle ‚ÄĒ interestingly, as part of an argument AGAINST calculus. Rolle believed that infinitesimals were logically incoherent, and he published his theorem to demonstrate a paradox in the new calculus. The irony is that the theorem named after him is now used to prove the foundations of the subject he opposed. Rolle's Theorem: Suppose f is continuous on the closed interval [a, b], differentiable on the open interval (a, b), and f(a) = f(b). Then there exists at least one c ‚ąą (a, b) such that f'(c) = 0. The proof uses the Extreme Value Theorem (EVT): since f is continuous on [a, b], it attains its maximum M and minimum m on [a, b]. If M = m, then f is constant and f' = 0 everywhere ‚ÄĒ any interior c works. If M ‚Č† m, then at least one extreme value is attained at some interior point c (it cannot be both maximum and minimum at the endpoints, since f(a) = f(b)). At an interior maximum or minimum c, f'(c) = 0 (by Fermat's theorem: if f has a local extremum at an interior differentiable point, the derivative is zero there). This completes the proof. The MVT follows from Rolle's Theorem by a tilt transformation. Define the helper function h(x) = f(x) - L(x), where L(x) = f(a) + [(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)]¬∑(x-a) is the secant line through the endpoints. Then h(a) = f(a) - f(a) = 0 and h(b) = f(b) - [f(a) + (f(b)-f(a))] = 0. Also, h is continuous on [a,b] (difference of continuous functions) and differentiable on (a,b). By Rolle's Theorem, there exists c ‚ąą (a,b) with h'(c) = 0. But h'(x) = f'(x) - (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a), so h'(c) = 0 gives f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). That is the MVT. The corollaries of the MVT are its main applications. Corollary 1: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then f is constant on (a,b). Proof: for any x‚āĀ, x‚āā ‚ąą (a,b) with x‚āĀ < x‚āā, MVT gives f(x‚āā) - f(x‚āĀ) = f'(c)(x‚āā - x‚āĀ) = 0. Corollary 2: If f'(x) > 0 for all x ‚ąą (a,b), then f is strictly increasing on (a,b). Corollary 3: If F'(x) = G'(x) for all x ‚ąą (a,b), then F(x) = G(x) + C for some constant C. This is the uniqueness-up-to-constant of antiderivatives. Corollary 4 (Error bound): |f(b) - f(a)| ‚Č§ (b-a)¬∑max_{[a,b]}|f'|. This bound is used in Taylor polynomial error estimates and numerical analysis. Cauchy's Generalized Mean Value Theorem: If f and g are continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), with g'(x) ‚Č† 0 on (a,b), then there exists c ‚ąą (a,b) such that f'(c)/g'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(g(b)-g(a)). Proof: apply Rolle's Theorem to h(x) = f(x)(g(b)-g(a)) - g(x)(f(b)-f(a)). This generalization is the key to proving L'H√īpital's Rule: in the 0/0 case with f(a) = g(a) = 0, the Cauchy MVT gives f(x)/g(x) = [f(x)-f(a)]/[g(x)-g(a)] = f'(c)/g'(c) for some c between a and x. As x ‚Üí a, c ‚Üí a too (since c is trapped between a and x), giving lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(c)/g'(c) = f'(a)/g'(a) when the derivative ratio is continuous. Proof of Rolle's Theorem in full: Suppose f is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and f(a) = f(b). By the Extreme Value Theorem (which follows from the completeness of ‚ĄĚ), f attains a maximum value M and a minimum value m on [a,b]. Case 1: M = m. Then f is constant on [a,b], so f'(x) = 0 for all x ‚ąą (a,b). Any interior point c serves as the guaranteed point. Case 2: M > m. Then M ‚Č† m, and since f(a) = f(b), it is impossible for both M and m to be attained only at the endpoints a and b (at least one extreme value must occur at an interior point). Suppose (WLOG) that the maximum M is attained at c ‚ąą (a,b). Then f(c) ‚Č• f(x) for all x ‚ąą [a,b]. For h > 0 small enough: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ‚Č§ 0 (since f(c+h) ‚Č§ f(c)). Taking h ‚Üí 0‚Āļ: f'(c) ‚Č§ 0 (right derivative ‚Č§ 0). For h < 0: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ‚Č• 0. Taking h ‚Üí 0‚ĀĽ: f'(c) ‚Č• 0 (left derivative ‚Č• 0). Since f is differentiable at c, left and right derivatives are equal, so 0 ‚Č§ f'(c) ‚Č§ 0, giving f'(c) = 0. ‚ąé Proof of the MVT from Rolle's Theorem: Define h(x) = f(x) - f(a) - [(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)]¬∑(x-a). Then: h is continuous on [a,b] (linear combination of continuous functions). h is differentiable on (a,b). h(a) = f(a) - f(a) - 0 = 0. h(b) = f(b) - f(a) - [(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)]¬∑(b-a) = f(b) - f(a) - (f(b)-f(a)) = 0. So h satisfies all hypotheses of Rolle's Theorem. Therefore ‚ąÉc ‚ąą (a,b) with h'(c) = 0. Computing: h'(x) = f'(x) - (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). Setting h'(c) = 0: f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). This is the MVT. ‚ąé Proof that f'=0 everywhere implies f is constant (using MVT directly): Suppose f'(x) = 0 for all x ‚ąą (a,b) and let x‚āĀ, x‚āā ‚ąą (a,b) with x‚āĀ < x‚āā. By the MVT applied to f on [x‚āĀ, x‚āā]: f(x‚āā) - f(x‚āĀ) = f'(c)(x‚āā - x‚āĀ) for some c ‚ąą (x‚āĀ, x‚āā). But f'(c) = 0 by hypothesis. Therefore f(x‚āā) - f(x‚āĀ) = 0¬∑(x‚āā - x‚āĀ) = 0, giving f(x‚āā) = f(x‚āĀ). Since x‚āĀ and x‚āā were arbitrary, f is constant on (a,b). ‚ąé This proof is the archetype for many other MVT applications ‚ÄĒ find a function satisfying MVT's hypotheses, identify what its derivative is, and conclude about the function's behavior. Cauchy's MVT ‚ÄĒ proof and application to L'H√īpital: Define h(x) = f(x)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(x)[f(b)-f(a)]. Then h(a) = f(a)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(a)[f(b)-f(a)] = f(a)g(b) - f(a)g(a) - g(a)f(b) + g(a)f(a) = f(a)g(b) - g(a)f(b). Similarly h(b) = f(b)g(b) - f(b)g(a) - g(b)f(b) + g(a)f(b) = -f(b)g(a) + g(a)f(b)... Actually h(a) = h(b): verify directly: h(b) - h(a) = [f(b)-f(a)][g(b)-g(a)] - [g(b)-g(a)][f(b)-f(a)] = 0. So h satisfies Rolle's hypothesis. Therefore ‚ąÉc with h'(c) = 0: f'(c)[g(b)-g(a)] - g'(c)[f(b)-f(a)] = 0, rearranging: f'(c)/g'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/[g(b)-g(a)]. For L'H√īpital: with f(a) = g(a) = 0, the ratio [f(x)-f(a)]/[g(x)-g(a)] = f(x)/g(x), and Cauchy's MVT gives f(x)/g(x) = f'(c)/g'(c) where c is between a and x. As x ‚Üí a, c ‚Üí a, so f(x)/g(x) ‚Üí f'(a)/g'(a) if the latter limit exists. ‚ąé Verify MVT for f(x) = x¬≤ on [1, 3] For f(x) = x¬≤ on [1,3], the unique MVT point is c = 2, the midpoint of [1,3]. This is not a coincidence for quadratic functions ‚ÄĒ for any f(x) = x¬≤ on [a,b], the MVT point is always c = (a+b)/2 (the midpoint), because f'(x) = 2x is linear and hits the average value exactly at the midpoint. Compute the average rate of change (slope of the secant from (1,1) to (3,9)). Compute the derivative. Set f'(c) equal to the average rate and solve. c = 2 ‚ąą (1, 3) ‚úď. Verify: f'(2) = 4 matches the secant slope. The MVT is satisfied with c = 2. Verify MVT for f(x) = x¬≥ on [-1, 1] There are two MVT points for x¬≥ on [-1,1]: c = ¬Ī1/‚ąö3. The MVT promised at least one; we found two. The function x¬≥ is odd (symmetric about the origin), and the two c values are symmetric as well ‚ÄĒ another symmetry consequence. Compute the average rate of change. Derivative of x¬≥. Solve for c. Two solutions: c = ¬Ī1/‚ąö3. Both values are in the open interval (-1, 1), confirming the MVT. This illustrates that the MVT can have multiple c values. MVT Proof: |sin(x) - sin(y)| ‚Č§ |x - y| The sine function is Lipschitz continuous with constant 1 ‚ÄĒ it cannot change faster than the identity function. This is an MVT corollary (the error bound): |f(b)-f(a)| ‚Č§ |b-a|¬∑max|f'|. Here max|cos| = 1 gives the result. Apply the MVT to f(t) = sin(t) on the interval between x and y (WLOG x < y). By the MVT: f(x) - f(y) = f'(c)(x-y). Take absolute values of both sides. Use |ab| = |a|¬∑|b|. The cosine function satisfies |cos(c)| ‚Č§ 1 everywhere. Multiply the inequality by |x-y| ‚Č• 0. ‚ąé Speed Trap Application At time c (some moment during the 3 hours), the instantaneous speed was exactly 200/3 ‚Čą 66.7 mph > 60 mph. The car definitely exceeded 60 mph at that moment. The MVT makes this a mathematical certainty, not just a suspicion. Set up: s(t) is the position function. s is continuous (the car moves continuously) and differentiable (it has a velocity) on (0,3). Compute the average velocity over the 3-hour trip. Apply the MVT: since s satisfies the hypotheses, there exists c with s'(c) = 200/3. The instantaneous speed at time c exceeds 60 mph. ‚ąé Rolle's Theorem: Exactly One Solution in an Interval x‚Āī + x = 2 has exactly one solution in [0,1], namely x = 1. The MVT/Rolle's approach (via the derivative) proves both existence and uniqueness. Existence: g(0) < 0 < g(1) and IVT (or just g(1) = 0). Uniqueness: g'> 0 so g is strictly increasing, hence at most one zero. Reformulate: find zeros of g(x) = x‚Āī + x - 2. Evaluate at the endpoints. g(1) = 0 ‚ÄĒ so x = 1 is a solution! We need to show it is the ONLY one in [0,1]. g'(x) = 4x¬≥ + 1 ‚Č• 1 > 0 for x ‚ąą [0,1]. So g is strictly increasing on [0,1]. A strictly increasing function can cross zero at most once ‚ÄĒ it can only go from negative to positive once. So there is at most one solution. Since g is strictly increasing and g(1) = 0, the only zero in [0,1] is x = 1. MVT Inequality: ‚ąö(1+x) < 1 + x/2 for x > 0 The MVT can be used to prove inequalities by bounding f'(c) on the relevant interval. The key steps are: (1) write f(b) - f(a) = f'(c)(b-a), then (2) bound f'(c) using the known range of c. This technique appears constantly in analysis. Consider f(t) = ‚ąöt on [1, 1+x] for x > 0. MVT: ‚ąö(1+x) - 1 = f'(c)¬∑x for some c strictly between 1 and 1+x. Derivative of ‚ąöt. Since c > 1, ‚ąöc > 1, so 1/(2‚ąöc) < 1/2. The c is strictly interior to (1, 1+x), so the strict inequality holds. Multiply the inequality 1/(2‚ąöc) < 1/2 by x > 0. Add 1 to both sides. Prove that |e^x - e^y| ‚Č• |x - y| for all x, y ‚Č• 0. If f'(x) = f(x) for all x and f(0) = 0, prove that f is identically zero. Prove Cauchy's MVT: if f, g are continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then ‚ąÉc ‚ąą (a,b) with f'(c)(g(b)-g(a)) = g'(c)(f(b)-f(a)).

documents.title: Curve Sketching
documents.subtitle: From formula to complete graph: critical points, inflection points, concavity, and asymptotes ‚ÄĒ all from derivatives alone
documents.tags: curve sketching critical points local extrema concavity inflection points first derivative test second derivative test asymptotes sign chart
documents.content: The complete qualitative picture of a function requires three layers of analysis. The first layer is f itself: domain (where is f defined?), x-intercepts (where does f = 0?), y-intercept (f(0)), symmetry (even/odd/neither), and asymptotes (what happens at the extremes?). The second layer is f': where is f increasing (f' > 0) and decreasing (f' < 0)? Where does the function change direction (f' = 0)? These changes of direction are local maxima and minima. The third layer is f'': where is the function concave up (bending upward, f'' > 0) and concave down (bending downward, f'' < 0)? Where does the concavity change (inflection points)? Together, these three layers give a complete picture. Critical points are where f'(x) = 0 or f'(x) is undefined. They are candidates for local extrema ‚ÄĒ but only candidates. The key tool for deciding is the first derivative test: make a sign chart for f' by dividing the number line at the critical points and testing f' in each interval. If f' changes from positive to negative at c, then f has a local maximum at c (it was rising, now falling). If f' changes from negative to positive, local minimum (was falling, now rising). If f' does not change sign, neither ‚ÄĒ c is likely a saddle point or inflection point. The sign chart is indispensable and should be your default tool. Concavity tells you the shape of the curve between critical points. If f''(x) > 0, the function is concave up ‚ÄĒ the graph looks like a bowl opening upward, tangent lines are below the curve. If f''(x) < 0, concave down ‚ÄĒ tangent lines are above the curve, like a frown or an upside-down bowl. An inflection point is a point where the concavity changes ‚ÄĒ where f''(x) = 0 AND f'' changes sign. At an inflection point, the tangent line actually crosses the curve rather than staying on one side. The second derivative test offers a quicker (but less powerful) alternative to the sign chart for classifying critical points. At a critical point c where f'(c) = 0: if f''(c) > 0, then f has a local minimum at c (the function is concave up at c, like the bottom of a bowl). If f''(c) < 0, local maximum. If f''(c) = 0, the test is inconclusive ‚ÄĒ the critical point could be a local max, local min, or inflection point. When the second derivative test fails, you must fall back on the first derivative test (sign chart). The reason the second derivative test works is explained by the Taylor expansion: f(x) ‚Čą f(c) + f''(c)(x-c)¬≤/2 near a critical point c, so the sign of f''(c) determines whether f is bowl-shaped or hill-shaped there. Asymptotes come in three varieties. Horizontal asymptotes: compute lim_{x‚Üí+‚ąě} f(x) and lim_{x‚Üí-‚ąě} f(x). If either limit is a finite number L, then y = L is a horizontal asymptote. Vertical asymptotes: look for x values where f(x) ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě ‚ÄĒ typically where a denominator equals zero or where a logarithm argument reaches 0. Oblique asymptotes: if the degree of the numerator of a rational function is exactly one more than the degree of the denominator, perform polynomial long division to find the asymptote. Many functions (polynomials, for instance) have no asymptotes; the analysis of asymptotes is most important for rational functions, log functions, and exponentials. The complete procedure for curve sketching: (1) Find the domain. (2) Find intercepts (x and y). (3) Find and classify asymptotes. (4) Compute f', find all critical points, make a sign chart for f' ‚Üí determine increasing/decreasing intervals and classify critical points. (5) Compute f'', find all potential inflection points, make a sign chart for f'' ‚Üí determine concavity and inflection points. (6) Plot a few key points (intercepts, critical points, inflection points) and connect them with a smooth curve consistent with all the information gathered. The result is a qualitatively accurate sketch that reveals the function's complete behavior. First Derivative Test (theorem): Suppose f is continuous on (a,b) and differentiable on (a,b) except possibly at c. If f'(x) > 0 on (a,c) and f'(x) < 0 on (c,b), then f has a local maximum at c. If f'(x) < 0 on (a,c) and f'(x) > 0 on (c,b), then f has a local minimum at c. If f' does not change sign at c, f has neither a local max nor a local min at c. The proof follows directly from the MVT corollary: f' > 0 implies increasing (so f rises to c from the left), f' < 0 implies decreasing (so f falls away from c to the right) ‚ÄĒ that is the definition of a local maximum. Second Derivative Test (theorem): Suppose f'(c) = 0 and f'' is continuous near c. If f''(c) > 0, then f has a local minimum at c. If f''(c) < 0, local maximum. If f''(c) = 0, inconclusive. The proof uses the Taylor expansion: f(x) = f(c) + f'(c)(x-c) + f''(c)(x-c)¬≤/2 + O((x-c)¬≥). Since f'(c) = 0, this simplifies to f(x) - f(c) ‚Čą f''(c)(x-c)¬≤/2. If f''(c) > 0, then (x-c)¬≤ > 0 for x ‚Č† c, so f(x) - f(c) > 0 for x near c ‚ÄĒ local minimum. If f''(c) < 0, f(x) - f(c) < 0 ‚ÄĒ local maximum. Concavity theorem: f is concave up on an interval I if and only if f''(x) ‚Č• 0 for all x ‚ąą I. f is concave down if and only if f''(x) ‚Č§ 0. These follow from the MVT applied to f': f is concave up iff f' is increasing iff (f')' = f'' ‚Č• 0. The characterization via tangent lines: f is concave up on I iff for all a,b ‚ąą I and all t ‚ąą (a,b), the chord from (a,f(a)) to (b,f(b)) lies above f ‚ÄĒ this is the geometric definition and it is equivalent to f''‚Č•0 for smooth functions. Asymptote analysis for rational functions: horizontal asymptotes are determined by the leading terms. If deg(numerator) < deg(denominator), y = 0. If equal degrees, y = ratio of leading coefficients. If deg(numerator) = deg(denominator) + 1, oblique asymptote via long division. Vertical asymptotes at zeros of the denominator where the function is not defined (and the function ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě). For exponential and logarithmic functions: lim_{x‚Üí‚ąě} e^(-x) = 0 (horizontal asymptote y = 0), lim_{x‚Üí0‚Āļ} ln(x) = -‚ąě (vertical asymptote x = 0). Proof of the first derivative test from the MVT: Suppose f'(x) > 0 on (a,c) and f'(x) < 0 on (c,b). For any x ‚ąą (a,c), apply the MVT to f on [x,c]: f(c) - f(x) = f'(s)(c-x) for some s ‚ąą (x,c). Since s ‚ąą (a,c), f'(s) > 0 and c-x > 0, so f(c) - f(x) > 0, giving f(c) > f(x). For any x ‚ąą (c,b), apply MVT to f on [c,x]: f(x) - f(c) = f'(s)(x-c) for some s ‚ąą (c,x). Since s ‚ąą (c,b), f'(s) < 0 and x-c > 0, so f(x) - f(c) < 0, giving f(x) < f(c). Therefore f(c) ‚Č• f(x) for all x near c ‚ÄĒ this is a local maximum. The proof of the local minimum case is analogous. Why the second derivative test works ‚ÄĒ the Taylor expansion argument in full: Suppose f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0. We want to show f(c) is a local minimum. By continuity of f'', there exists őī > 0 such that f''(x) > 0 for all x ‚ąą (c-őī, c+őī). For any x ‚ąą (c-őī, c+őī) with x ‚Č† c, apply the MVT to f' on [c,x] (or [x,c]): f'(x) - f'(c) = f''(s)(x-c) for some s between c and x. Since s ‚ąą (c-őī, c+őī), f''(s) > 0. Also f'(c) = 0, so f'(x) = f''(s)(x-c). For x > c: x-c > 0 and f''(s) > 0, so f'(x) > 0. For x < c: x-c < 0 and f''(s) > 0, so f'(x) < 0. Therefore f' changes sign from - to + at c ‚ÄĒ by the first derivative test, c is a local minimum. ‚ąé This proof reveals the true logic: the second derivative test works because f''> 0 forces f' to be increasing, and f' going from - to + is exactly the first derivative test for a minimum. The second derivative test fails when f''(c) = 0 because f' might increase or decrease at c without f'' committing to a definite sign. The examples f(x) = x‚Āī (local min at 0, f''(0) = 0), f(x) = -x‚Āī (local max at 0, f''(0) = 0), and f(x) = x¬≥ (inflection, f''(0) = 0) all have f'(0) = 0 and f''(0) = 0 but different behavior. The first derivative test always works in these cases and is the definitive test. Sketch f(x) = x¬≥ - 3x f(x) = x¬≥ - 3x: rising on (-‚ąě,-1) ‚ą™ (1,‚ąě), falling on (-1,1). Local max at (-1,2), local min at (1,-2). Inflection at (0,0) ‚ÄĒ concave down for x < 0, up for x > 0. X-intercepts at 0 and ¬Ī‚ąö3. Odd function (symmetric about origin). As x ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě, f ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě. Derivative factored. Critical points at x = ¬Ī1. Sign chart for f': f' = 3(x-1)(x+1). Test x = -2: 3(3)(-1) < 0... actually + + = + > 0. Test x = 0: 3(-1)(1) < 0. Test x = 2: 3(1)(3) > 0. ‚úď Second derivative. Second derivative test: f''(-1) < 0 confirms local maximum. Local maximum value: f(-1) = 2. Point: (-1, 2). f''(1) > 0 confirms local minimum. Local minimum value: f(1) = -2. Point: (1, -2). f'' changes sign at x = 0 (negative for x < 0, positive for x > 0). Inflection point at (0, f(0)) = (0, 0). Factor to find x-intercepts. Sketch f(x) = x‚Āī - 4x¬≥ (the hook function) f(x) = x‚Āī - 4x¬≥: decreasing on (-‚ąě, 3), increasing on (3,‚ąě). Local (and global) minimum at (3,-27). Inflection points at (0,0) and (2,-16). Concave up on (-‚ąě,0) and (2,‚ąě), concave down on (0,2). As x ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě, f ‚Üí +‚ąě (even-degree leading term is positive). Y-intercept at (0,0). Factor the derivative. Critical points at x = 0 and x = 3. Test x = -1: 4(1)(-4) < 0. Test x = 1: 4(1)(-2) < 0. Test x = 4: 4(16)(1) > 0. Note: f' < 0 on BOTH sides of x=0. Since f' stays negative through x = 0, the first derivative test gives neither max nor min. This is an inflection-like point. Local minimum at x = 3. Local minimum value: -27. Point (3, -27). Second derivative. Potential inflection points. Sign chart for f''. Inflection points at x = 0 and x = 2, confirmed by sign changes. Inflection points: (0,0) and (2,-16). Sketch the Rational Function f(x) = x/(x¬≤+1) The function x/(x¬≤+1) is odd (f(-x) = -f(x)), bounded between -1/2 and 1/2. It resembles a scaled arctan curve: rises from 0, peaks at (1, 1/2), descends, returns to the asymptote y=0. It has three inflection points. The function is sometimes called the "witch of Agnesi" derivative. Horizontal asymptote: y = 0 (both directions). No vertical asymptotes since x¬≤+1 > 0 always. Quotient rule derivative. Critical points at x = ¬Ī1. Denominator is always positive. Sign of 1-x¬≤: positive for |x| < 1, negative for |x| > 1. So f increases on (-1,1) and decreases elsewhere. Local minimum at (-1,-1/2), local maximum at (1,1/2). Second derivative (simplified). Inflection points where numerator = 0: x = 0 or x = ¬Ī‚ąö3. Verify sign changes in f''. Sketch the Bell Curve f(x) = e^(-x¬≤) The bell curve e^{-x¬≤} has a global maximum at (0,1), inflection points at (¬Ī1/‚ąö2, e^{-1/2}), is concave down between the inflection points and concave up outside. It is symmetric, always positive, and approaches 0 as x ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě. The normal distribution's pdf is (1/‚ąö(2ŌÄ))¬∑e^{-x¬≤/2}, a scaled and stretched version. Y-intercept at (0,1). Horizontal asymptote y = 0 in both directions. No zeros (e^anything > 0 always). Even function (symmetric about y-axis). Chain rule: derivative of e^u is e^u¬∑u', where u = -x¬≤, u' = -2x. Only critical point at x = 0. Sign of -2x¬∑e^{-x¬≤}: the exponential is always positive. Sign = sign of -2x. So f' > 0 for x < 0, < 0 for x > 0. Differentiate f' using product rule. Inflection points at x = ¬Ī1/‚ąö2 ‚Čą ¬Ī0.707. Inflection points at (¬Ī1/‚ąö2, e^{-1/2}). Sketch f(x) = x - sin(x) on [0, 2ŌÄ] f(x) = x - sin(x) is always increasing (f' ‚Č• 0), ranging from 0 at x=0 to 2ŌÄ at x=2ŌÄ. Concave up on (0,ŌÄ) and concave down on (ŌÄ,2ŌÄ), with inflection point at (ŌÄ, ŌÄ). The function is the integral of 1-cos(x), which appears in the area under a cycloid. Derivative. Critical points only at the endpoints of the interval. f is non-decreasing on [0, 2ŌÄ]. It is strictly increasing except at x = 0 and 2ŌÄ where f' = 0 momentarily. Second derivative. Potential inflection points. Inflection point at x = ŌÄ. Key values. At x = ŌÄ, f(ŌÄ) = ŌÄ - sin(ŌÄ) = ŌÄ. Sketch f(x) = ln(x)/x on (0, ‚ąě) ln(x)/x rises from -‚ąě to a maximum of 1/e at x = e, then decreases toward 0 asymptotically. The x-intercept is at x = 1. The maximum at x = e is the key value ‚ÄĒ this function appears in the optimization of x^(1/x) (which has a maximum also at x = e, giving e^(1/e)). As x ‚Üí 0‚Āļ: ln(x) ‚Üí -‚ąě and 1/x ‚Üí +‚ąě, so the product ‚Üí -‚ąě. Vertical asymptote behavior at x = 0. As x ‚Üí ‚ąě: x grows much faster than ln(x) (by L'H√īpital: (1/x)/1 ‚Üí 0). Horizontal asymptote y = 0. Quotient rule. Numerator: (1/x)¬∑x - ln(x)¬∑1 = 1 - ln(x). Critical point at x = e. Sign of 1 - ln(x): positive when ln(x) < 1 (x < e), negative when x > e. Maximum value: 1/e at x = e. The function crosses zero at x = 1 (ln(1) = 0). All Local Extrema of a Cubic f(x) = 2x¬≥ - 3x¬≤ - 12x + 4 has a local maximum at (-1, 11) and a local minimum at (2, -16). The function rises on (-‚ąě,-1), falls on (-1,2), and rises on (2,‚ąě). The inflection point is where f'' = 0: 12x - 6 = 0, x = 1/2, f(1/2) = 2(1/8) - 3(1/4) - 6 + 4 = 1/4 - 3/4 - 2 = -5/2. Inflection at (1/2, -5/2). Factor the derivative. Critical points. Second derivative. Second derivative test: f''(-1) < 0 ‚Üí local max. Local maximum value: 11. Point (-1, 11). Second derivative test: f''(2) > 0 ‚Üí local min. Local minimum value: -16. Point (2, -16). Maximum Height of a Thrown Ball (Physics) Maximum height of 64 ft at t=2 s, confirmed by three methods: (1) h'(2)=0 is a critical point, (2) h''=-32<0 confirms local max, (3) sign chart shows h' changes + to -. The constant h''=-32 means gravity pulls the ball down with constant acceleration throughout. The graph of h(t) is a downward parabola ‚ÄĒ symmetric about its peak. The graph of h'(t)=64-32t is a line crossing zero at t=2. The graph of h''(t)=-32 is a horizontal line below the axis. Velocity: power rule gives d/dt[64t]=64 and d/dt[-16t¬≤]=-32t. This is v(t) = 64 - 32t, which decreases linearly ‚ÄĒ the ball slows as it rises. Maximum height when velocity = 0. The ball momentarily stops at t=2 s ‚ÄĒ this is the critical point. Set h'=0 and solve. Acceleration is constant at -32 ft/s¬≤ ‚ÄĒ this is gravitational deceleration (g ‚Čą 32 ft/s¬≤ downward). h''< 0 everywhere confirms every critical point is a maximum. Maximum height: substitute t=2 into h(t). The ball reaches 64 feet ‚ÄĒ same as the initial velocity numerically (not a coincidence: h_max = v‚āÄ¬≤/64). Ball returns to ground at t=4 s. Total airtime = 4 s = 2 √ó (time to peak), as expected by symmetry. Ball rises for t‚ąą(0,2) and falls for t‚ąą(2,4). The maximum at t=2 is confirmed by the sign change + to -. h''< 0 everywhere: h is concave down for its entire domain. This means h'(t) is always decreasing ‚ÄĒ the ball continuously decelerates under gravity. Find constants a and b such that f(x) = ax¬≥ + bx¬≤ has a local maximum at x = -1 with f(-1) = 4, and an inflection point at x = 1. Prove: if f'(c) = 0 and f changes from concave up to concave down at c, then c is a local maximum. Sketch f(x) = x^{2/3}(x - 4). Note that f' is undefined at x = 0 ‚ÄĒ this creates a cusp.

documents.title: Optimization
documents.subtitle: Finding the best ‚ÄĒ maximum area, minimum cost, optimal angle ‚ÄĒ using critical points and the Extreme Value Theorem
documents.tags: optimization extreme value theorem Fermat's theorem closed interval method objective function constraint global extrema applications
documents.content: All optimization problems share the same mathematical skeleton: identify the quantity you want to maximize or minimize (the objective function), identify the constraint that links your variables, use the constraint to reduce the objective to a single-variable function, then find the critical points of that reduced function and check which gives the global optimum. The geometry varies enormously ‚ÄĒ from rectangles to cylinders to light rays ‚ÄĒ but the procedure is always the same. Once you recognize this structure, optimization problems become systematic rather than mysterious. The Extreme Value Theorem (EVT) is the guarantee that global extrema exist on closed intervals. If f is continuous on [a,b], then f attains both a global maximum and a global minimum on [a,b]. This is a non-trivial theorem ‚ÄĒ it fails for open intervals (consider f(x) = x on (0,1), which approaches but never attains the values 0 and 1) and for discontinuous functions. The EVT relies on the completeness of the real numbers. For calculus problems, the EVT justifies the closed-interval method: evaluate f at all critical points in (a,b) and at both endpoints a, b; the largest value is the global max and the smallest is the global min. For open intervals or unbounded domains (which are more common in word problems), you cannot use the endpoint evaluation directly. Instead, you rely on physical reasoning: if the problem guarantees a maximum or minimum exists (physically, there must be some optimal value), and you find only one critical point that is a local min, then it must be the global min. This "one critical point" argument is valid for many engineering and economic problems but requires justification. More rigorously: if f is continuous on (a,b), lim_{x‚Üía‚Āļ} f(x) = +‚ąě (or approaches a limit below the critical value), and lim_{x‚Üíb‚ĀĽ} f(x) = +‚ąě, and there is one critical point with f''> 0, then that critical point gives the global minimum. The fencing problem illustrates the full procedure. Let x and y be the sides of the rectangle. Constraint: perimeter = 2x + 2y = 100, so x + y = 50. Objective: area A = xy. Substitute y = 50 - x to get A(x) = x(50-x) = 50x - x¬≤. Domain: x ‚ąą (0, 50) (both dimensions must be positive). Derivative: A'(x) = 50 - 2x = 0 gives x = 25. A''(x) = -2 < 0 confirms local (and global) maximum. Optimal dimensions: x = y = 25 m (a square). Maximum area: 625 m¬≤. The square emerges from calculus, not from intuition. With one side against a river, you only need 2x + y = 100 (three sides). Objective: A = xy = x(100 - 2x). A'(x) = 100 - 4x = 0 gives x = 25 m. Then y = 50 m. Area = 1250 m¬≤ ‚ÄĒ twice the area of the four-sided enclosure! The river allows a different optimal shape: 25m √ó 50m (not a square), because the asymmetry of the constraint breaks the square's optimality. The hardest part of an optimization problem is usually the setup: translating the word problem into an objective function and constraint. Experienced problem-solvers follow a disciplined procedure: (1) Draw a picture and label all quantities. (2) Identify the objective: what are you maximizing or minimizing? Write it as a formula. (3) Identify the constraint: what relationship must always hold? (4) Solve the constraint for one variable and substitute into the objective. (5) Differentiate and set equal to zero. (6) Verify it is a maximum or minimum (not a saddle point). (7) Answer the original question ‚ÄĒ report the optimal value AND the optimal input, with units. The AM-GM inequality (arithmetic mean ‚Č• geometric mean) provides a non-calculus proof that the square maximizes area for fixed perimeter. For positive x, y: (x+y)/2 ‚Č• ‚ąö(xy), with equality iff x = y. So given x + y = 50, we have 50/2 ‚Č• ‚ąö(xy), giving 625 ‚Č• xy, with equality iff x = y = 25. This is the fencing answer without calculus. But calculus generalizes: it solves non-symmetric, non-polynomial constraints where AM-GM cannot help. Extreme Value Theorem (formal statement): If f is continuous on a closed, bounded interval [a,b], then f attains its maximum value M and its minimum value m on [a,b] ‚ÄĒ that is, there exist c, d ‚ąą [a,b] with f(c) = M and f(d) = m, and m ‚Č§ f(x) ‚Č§ M for all x ‚ąą [a,b]. The hypotheses ‚ÄĒ continuous, closed, bounded ‚ÄĒ are all necessary. The EVT is proved using the Heine-Borel theorem (closed bounded intervals in ‚ĄĚ are compact) and the maximum principle for continuous functions on compact sets. Fermat's Theorem (local extrema occur at critical points): If f has a local maximum or minimum at c, and f is differentiable at c, then f'(c) = 0. Proof: suppose f has a local maximum at c. For h > 0 small: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ‚Č§ 0 (since f(c+h) ‚Č§ f(c)). Taking h ‚Üí 0‚Āļ: f'(c) ‚Č§ 0. For h < 0 small: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ‚Č• 0. Taking h ‚Üí 0‚ĀĽ: f'(c) ‚Č• 0. Therefore f'(c) = 0. The proof for local minimum is analogous. Fermat's Theorem says every differentiable local extremum is a critical point, but not every critical point is an extremum ‚ÄĒ the converse fails. Closed Interval Method: to find the global extrema of a continuous f on [a,b]: (1) Find all critical points of f in (a,b) ‚ÄĒ both where f' = 0 and where f' is undefined. (2) Evaluate f at each critical point and at the endpoints a and b. (3) The largest value is the global maximum; the smallest is the global minimum. This method is guaranteed to work by the EVT (global extrema exist) and Fermat's Theorem (differentiable local extrema have f' = 0). Standard optimization setups: Rectangle of fixed perimeter P: area A = xy, constraint 2x+2y = P. Max area = P¬≤/16 at x = y = P/4 (square). Cylinder of fixed volume V: surface area SA = 2ŌÄr¬≤ + 2ŌÄrh = 2ŌÄr¬≤ + 2V/r (using h = V/(ŌÄr¬≤)). Minimizing: SA' = 4ŌÄr - 2V/r¬≤ = 0 gives r¬≥ = V/(2ŌÄ), h = 2r (height = diameter). General: at the minimum surface area, height = diameter for any fixed volume V. Proof of the Extreme Value Theorem sketch: we want to show that a continuous function f on [a,b] attains its supremum M = sup{f(x) : x ‚ąą [a,b]}. First, we show f is bounded above: suppose not. Then for each n, there exists x‚āô ‚ąą [a,b] with f(x‚āô) > n. By Bolzano-Weierstrass, {x‚āô} has a convergent subsequence x‚āô‚āĖ ‚Üí c ‚ąą [a,b]. By continuity, f(x‚āô‚āĖ) ‚Üí f(c), contradicting f(x‚āô‚āĖ) > n‚āĖ ‚Üí ‚ąě. So f is bounded and M exists. Now find a sequence y‚āô ‚ąą [a,b] with f(y‚āô) ‚Üí M. By compactness (Bolzano-Weierstrass), y‚āô‚āĖ ‚Üí d ‚ąą [a,b]. By continuity, f(y‚āô‚āĖ) ‚Üí f(d), so f(d) = M. The function attains its supremum at d. ‚ąé Proof of Fermat's Theorem (complete): Suppose f has a local maximum at c and f is differentiable at c. By definition of local maximum, there exists őī > 0 such that f(c+h) ‚Č§ f(c) for all |h| < őī. For 0 < h < őī: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ‚Č§ 0/h = 0. The difference quotient is non-positive. By the limit property (limit of non-positive is non-positive): lim_{h‚Üí0‚Āļ} [f(c+h)-f(c)]/h ‚Č§ 0, i.e., f'(c) ‚Č§ 0 (the right derivative). For -őī < h < 0: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ‚Č• 0/h = 0 (h is negative and f(c+h) - f(c) ‚Č§ 0, so their ratio ‚Č• 0). lim_{h‚Üí0‚ĀĽ} [f(c+h)-f(c)]/h ‚Č• 0, i.e., f'(c) ‚Č• 0 (left derivative). Since f is differentiable, f'(c) = right derivative = left derivative, so f'(c) ‚Č• 0 and f'(c) ‚Č§ 0 simultaneously, giving f'(c) = 0. ‚ąé The closed interval method works because the global maximum must occur somewhere (EVT), and wherever it occurs is either (a) an interior point where f is differentiable (Fermat's Theorem forces f'= 0 there), or (b) an interior point where f is not differentiable, or (c) an endpoint. The closed interval method checks all three cases. This is a complete argument ‚ÄĒ nothing is missed. For unbounded domains: suppose we want to minimize f on (0,‚ąě) and f(x) ‚Üí ‚ąě as x ‚Üí 0‚Āļ and x ‚Üí ‚ąě. If f is continuous and has exactly one critical point c ‚ąą (0,‚ąě) with f''(c) > 0, then c gives the global minimum. The argument: since f ‚Üí ‚ąě at both "ends" and f is continuous, there must be a global minimum in the interior (by a version of EVT on the compactified line). Since f''(c) > 0 gives a local minimum, and it's the only critical point, it must be the global minimum. This is the standard "physical" argument used in most engineering optimization problems. Fencing Problem: Maximize Rectangular Area A 25m √ó 25m square encloses the maximum area of 625 m¬≤. The calculus confirms the geometric/algebraic insight: the square is uniquely optimal. Any deviation from equal sides (e.g., 20 √ó 30) gives A = 600 < 625. Draw a rectangle with sides x and y. Write the constraint from the fixed perimeter. The area is the objective function. Substitute the constraint to eliminate y. Differentiate and set equal to zero. Second derivative confirms maximum. Both sides are 25 m ‚ÄĒ a square. Maximum area is 625 m¬≤. Open Box from a Sheet of Cardboard Cutting squares of approximately 1.57 inches from each corner maximizes the box volume at about 67.6 in¬≥. The exact optimal cut is x = (10 - 2‚ąö7)/3 inches. Cutting too little wastes height; cutting too much shrinks the base. After cutting corners x √ó x, the box has dimensions (12-2x) √ó (8-2x) √ó x. Domain: x ‚ąą (0, 4) (need 8-2x > 0). Expand the product. Differentiate. Quadratic formula. The physically valid solution: x ‚Čą 1.570 in (the other root ‚Čą 5.097 is outside the domain (0,4)). Maximum volume is approximately 67.6 cubic inches. V''(1.570) ‚Čą 24(1.570) - 80 = 37.68 - 80 = -42.32 < 0. Maximum confirmed. Minimum Surface Area Tin Can The optimal 500 cm¬≥ can has height = diameter ‚Čą 8.6 cm, using ‚Čą349 cm¬≤ of material. Real soup cans are often taller than their diameter because of manufacturing constraints (top/bottom lids cost more per unit area than the side). The mathematical optimum is a cube-like short cylinder. Volume constraint: solve for h. Surface area = two circles (top/bottom) + lateral area. Substitute h = 500/(ŌÄr¬≤). Differentiate and set equal to zero. Solve for r¬≥. Optimal radius. The optimal height equals the diameter (h = 2r). This is a remarkable result ‚ÄĒ the optimal can is as tall as it is wide. Minimum surface area: ‚Čą349 cm¬≤. Nearest Point on a Parabola The nearest points to (0,3) on y = x¬≤ are (¬Ī‚ąö(10)/2, 5/2), at distance ‚ąö11/2 ‚Čą 1.66. The point (0,0) on the parabola is actually farther from (0,3) than the two optimal points ‚ÄĒ the parabola curves away from the y-axis faster than the straight-line distance decreases. Distance squared from (x, x¬≤) to (0,3). We minimize D¬≤ (equivalent to minimizing D, and easier). Expand (x¬≤ - 3)¬≤ = x‚Āī - 6x¬≤ + 9, then combine x¬≤ terms. Differentiate and factor. Three critical points. The solutions ¬Ī‚ąö(5/2) are the meaningful candidates. Evaluate D¬≤ at each critical point. D¬≤(0) = 9 (point (0,0), distance 3). D¬≤(¬Ī‚ąö(5/2)) = 11/4 (smaller!). Minimum distance. Nearest points: (¬Ī‚ąö(5/2), 5/2) ‚Čą (¬Ī1.581, 2.5). Snell's Law from Fermat's Principle Fermat's Principle (light takes the path of minimum time) and Snell's Law of optics are the same calculation: minimizing total travel time across two media. The lifeguard problem is physically identical to a light ray crossing from air to water. Calculus unifies optics, swimming, and running under the same optimization principle. Total time = (running distance)/8 + (swimming distance)/2. Running along the beach to point (x,0), then swimming to (4,-2). Differentiate and set equal to zero. Rearrange. If őł‚āĀ is the angle the running path makes with the normal to the beach and őł‚āā is the angle the swimming path makes: sin(őł‚āĀ) = x/‚ąö(x¬≤+9) and sin(őł‚āā) = (4-x)/‚ąö((4-x)¬≤+4). The condition T'= 0 becomes sin(őł‚āĀ)/8 = sin(őł‚āā)/2. This is Snell's Law of refraction: sin(őł‚āĀ)/v‚āĀ = sin(őł‚āā)/v‚āā. The optimal crossing point is about 0.5 m along the beach. The lifeguard runs mostly to the water's edge quickly, then swims at an angle to the swimmer. Economic Order Quantity The optimal order quantity is Q* = ‚ąö(60000) ‚Čą 245 units, ordered about 5 times per year. The minimum annual cost is about $490. The general formula Q* = ‚ąö(2DS/H) (where D = annual demand, S = setup cost, H = holding cost per unit per year) is the famous Economic Order Quantity formula, used in supply chain management worldwide. If Q units are ordered each time, the number of orders per year is 1200/Q. Inventory decreases linearly from Q to 0 between orders, so average is Q/2. Total annual cost = order costs + storage costs. Set C'= 0 and solve. C'' > 0 confirms this is a minimum. Minimum cost. Note: at the optimum, ordering cost = storage cost (both ‚Čą $245). This is always true for the EOQ model: C is minimized when the two costs are equal. Maximize the Area of a Norman Window The maximum area Norman window with perimeter 10 m has r ‚Čą 1.40 m and h ‚Čą 1.40 m. At the optimum, h = r: the rectangle height equals the semicircle radius. This is a general result for Norman windows ‚ÄĒ the optimal shape always satisfies h = r. The window width = 2r. The rectangle has width 2r and height h. The semicircle has radius r. Perimeter consists of: two vertical sides (height h each), the bottom edge (2r), and the semicircle (ŌÄr). Note: no top side ‚ÄĒ the semicircle replaces it. Solve for h. Area = rectangle (2r √ó h) + semicircle (ŌÄr¬≤/2). Substitute h. Simplify: -ŌÄr¬≤ + ŌÄr¬≤/2 = -ŌÄr¬≤/2. Differentiate and set equal to zero. Optimal radius. Optimal height ‚Čą 1.40 m. Notice h ‚Čą r at the optimum. Optimal Launch Angle for Maximum Range (Physics) 45¬į gives maximum range of v‚āÄ¬≤/g ‚Čą 91.8 m. The elegant result őł*=45¬į emerges because we are maximising sin(2őł), which peaks when 2őł=90¬į. Notice the symmetry: R(őł) = R(90¬į-őł), so 30¬į and 60¬į give the same range ‚ÄĒ the physics is symmetric about 45¬į. In reality, air resistance shifts the optimum below 45¬į (typically 30¬į-38¬į for bullets and baseballs), which requires a more complex model. The range formula from projectile kinematics. Note R depends only on sin(2őł) ‚ÄĒ all the physics is packed into this trig function. Differentiate with respect to őł. d/dőł[sin(2őł)] = 2cos(2őł) by the chain rule. Set derivative = 0. cos(2őł)=0 when 2őł=ŌÄ/2 (in the interval 0 < 2őł < ŌÄ). 45¬į maximises range. This is the famous result ‚ÄĒ every cannon ever built was aimed at 45¬į for maximum distance (ignoring air resistance). Second derivative for verification. R''(45¬į) < 0 confirms this is a maximum, not a minimum. Maximum range ‚Čą 91.8 m. This equals v‚āÄ¬≤/g = 900/9.8 ‚ÄĒ the range formula's prefactor. Boundary check: R=0 at both endpoints. Launching horizontally (0¬į) or straight up (90¬į) gives zero range ‚ÄĒ physically correct! Prove: (1) Among all rectangles with fixed perimeter P, the square has maximum area. (2) Among all rectangles with fixed area A, the square has minimum perimeter. Find the cylinder of maximum volume that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius R. A Norman window (rectangle + semicircle on top) has perimeter 10 m. Show the optimal height-to-radius ratio is h = r, where h is the rectangle height and r is the semicircle radius.

documents.title: L'H√īpital's Rule
documents.subtitle: When limits produce 0/0 or ‚ąě/‚ąě, differentiate numerator and denominator separately ‚ÄĒ a consequence of the MVT
documents.tags: l'hopital l'hopital's rule indeterminate forms limits 0/0 infinity over infinity Cauchy MVT indeterminate powers sinc function
documents.content: The basic idea is beautifully simple once you understand linear approximation. Suppose f(c) = 0 and g(c) = 0. Near x = c, both functions are well approximated by their linearizations: f(x) ‚Čą f'(c)(x-c) and g(x) ‚Čą g'(c)(x-c) (since f(c) = g(c) = 0, the constant terms vanish). Dividing: f(x)/g(x) ‚Čą f'(c)(x-c)/[g'(c)(x-c)] = f'(c)/g'(c). The (x-c) factors cancel! L'H√īpital's Rule is the formal version of this linear approximation argument. Near a zero of both numerator and denominator, both functions look like their derivatives times (x-c), and the (x-c) factors cancel in the ratio. The formal statement: if lim_{x‚Üíc} f(x) = 0 and lim_{x‚Üíc} g(x) = 0, and if lim_{x‚Üíc} f'(x)/g'(x) exists (or equals ¬Ī‚ąě), and g'(x) ‚Č† 0 near c (except possibly at c itself), then lim_{x‚Üíc} f(x)/g(x) = lim_{x‚Üíc} f'(x)/g'(x). The same rule applies when f(x) ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě and g(x) ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě (the ‚ąě/‚ąě form), and also for one-sided limits and limits as x ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě. The rule can be applied repeatedly: if f'/g' is still 0/0, apply it again. Each application simplifies the problem, typically by reducing the degree of the functions involved. The other five indeterminate forms (0¬∑‚ąě, ‚ąě-‚ąě, 1^‚ąě, 0^0, ‚ąě^0) all convert to 0/0 or ‚ąě/‚ąě through algebraic manipulation. For 0¬∑‚ąě: write f(x)¬∑g(x) = f(x)/(1/g(x)) = 0/0, or g(x)/(1/f(x)) = ‚ąě/‚ąě. Which form to use depends on which gives a simpler derivative. For the power forms 1^‚ąě, 0^0, ‚ąě^0: take logarithms. If L = lim f(x)^{g(x)}, then ln(L) = lim g(x)¬∑ln(f(x)). This converts the power form to a 0¬∑‚ąě limit in the exponent, which then converts to 0/0 or ‚ąě/‚ąě. After evaluating ln(L) = K, conclude L = e^K. The historical context is extraordinary. L'H√īpital's Rule appears in the 1696 book Analyse des infiniment petits pour l'intelligence des lignes courbes ‚ÄĒ the first textbook ever published on differential calculus, written by Guillaume de l'H√īpital, a French marquis and amateur mathematician. However, the rule was actually discovered by Johann Bernoulli, who was employed by l'H√īpital as a private tutor. L'H√īpital paid Bernoulli a retainer for the right to use his mathematical discoveries exclusively. The rule in the book was Bernoulli's. L'H√īpital died in 1704; Bernoulli only went public with his claim to the rule after l'H√īpital's death. Correspondence later found in the Bernoulli archive confirmed the story. L'H√īpital's Rule is one of mathematics' most famous cases of misattributed credit. Critical warning: L'H√īpital's Rule applies ONLY when the limit is genuinely indeterminate (0/0 or ‚ąě/‚ąě). It cannot be applied to a limit like lim_{x‚Üí1} (x¬≤-1)/(x-1) if you have not first verified that both numerator and denominator approach 0 (they do ‚ÄĒ it's 0/0 form). More dangerously, some students apply L'H√īpital to non-indeterminate limits: lim_{x‚Üí1} (x+2)/(x-1) approaches 3/0 = ¬Ī‚ąě, not 0/0. Applying L'H√īpital here gives lim 1/1 = 1, which is wrong. The correct answer is ¬Ī‚ąě (the limit does not exist). Always verify the indeterminate form FIRST. Another important limitation: L'H√īpital's Rule can fail even when it seems applicable. Consider lim_{x‚Üí‚ąě} (x + sin(x))/x. This appears to be ‚ąě/‚ąě. The rule would give (1 + cos(x))/1, which has no limit as x ‚Üí ‚ąě (since cos(x) oscillates). Yet the original limit clearly exists: (x + sin(x))/x = 1 + sin(x)/x ‚Üí 1 + 0 = 1 (since |sin(x)/x| ‚Č§ 1/x ‚Üí 0). L'H√īpital's Rule says: IF the limit of f'/g' exists, THEN lim f/g equals it. It says nothing about what happens when f'/g' has no limit. The failure of L'H√īpital does not imply the original limit fails ‚ÄĒ you must use another method. Formal statement ‚ÄĒ 0/0 case: Suppose f and g are differentiable on an open interval I containing c (except possibly at c), f(c) = g(c) = 0 (or both approach 0), and g'(x) ‚Č† 0 for x ‚ąą I with x ‚Č† c. If lim_{x‚Üíc} f'(x)/g'(x) = L (where L is finite, +‚ąě, or -‚ąě), then lim_{x‚Üíc} f(x)/g(x) = L. The rule applies equally for one-sided limits (x ‚Üí c‚Āļ or x ‚Üí c‚ĀĽ) and for limits as x ‚Üí ¬Ī‚ąě. Converting 0¬∑‚ąě to 0/0 or ‚ąě/‚ąě: if lim f(x) = 0 and lim g(x) = ‚ąě, write f(x)¬∑g(x) = f(x)/(1/g(x)) (now 0/0) or f(x)¬∑g(x) = g(x)/(1/f(x)) (now ‚ąě/‚ąě). Choose the form that gives a simpler derivative. Typically: if one function is a polynomial or algebraic (easy to differentiate repeatedly), put it in the denominator as 1/polynomial. If one factor is an exponential (which reproduces under differentiation), the algebra often simplifies after one or two applications. Converting ‚ąě-‚ąě to 0/0: write f(x) - g(x) = 1/(1/f(x)) - 1/(1/g(x)) and combine fractions, or find a common structure. Example: lim_{x‚Üí0‚Āļ} (1/sin(x) - 1/x) = lim (x - sin(x))/(x¬∑sin(x)) ‚ÄĒ now 0/0 form. Power forms ‚ÄĒ 1^‚ąě, 0^0, ‚ąě^0: let L = lim f(x)^{g(x)}. Take natural logs: ln(L) = lim g(x)¬∑ln(f(x)). This is a 0¬∑‚ąě form (for 0^0: g‚Üí0, ln(f)‚Üí-‚ąě; for 1^‚ąě: g‚Üí‚ąě, ln(f)‚Üí0; for ‚ąě^0: g‚Üí0, ln(f)‚Üí‚ąě). Convert to 0/0 or ‚ąě/‚ąě and apply L'H√īpital. After finding ln(L) = K, conclude L = e^K. The most important examples: lim_{x‚Üí‚ąě}(1+1/x)^x = e (1^‚ąě form, gives ln(L) = 1), and lim_{x‚Üí0‚Āļ} x^x = 1 (0^0 form, gives ln(L) = lim x¬∑ln(x) = 0). Proof of L'H√īpital's Rule via Cauchy's MVT (0/0 case): Since f(c) = g(c) = 0, the Cauchy MVT applies to f and g on [c, x] (or [x,c]) for x near c: f(x)/g(x) = [f(x)-f(c)]/[g(x)-g(c)] = f'(t)/g'(t) for some t strictly between c and x. As x ‚Üí c, t ‚Üí c (since t is trapped between c and x). Therefore lim_{x‚Üíc} f(x)/g(x) = lim_{t‚Üíc} f'(t)/g'(t) = L. ‚ąé Complete proof of L'H√īpital's Rule (0/0 case) using Cauchy's MVT: Let f, g be continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), with f(c) = g(c) = 0 and g'(x) ‚Č† 0 on (a,b). For any x ‚Č† c in (a,b), the Cauchy MVT applies to f and g on the interval [min(c,x), max(c,x)]: there exists t strictly between c and x such that f'(t)[g(x)-g(c)] = g'(t)[f(x)-f(c)]. Since f(c) = g(c) = 0: f'(t)¬∑g(x) = g'(t)¬∑f(x). Since g'(t) ‚Č† 0 (by hypothesis) and g(x) ‚Č† 0 for x near c (since g'‚Č†0 and g(c)=0, g is locally monotone, so g(x) ‚Č† 0 near c): f(x)/g(x) = f'(t)/g'(t). As x ‚Üí c, t ‚Üí c (since t is strictly between c and x). Therefore lim_{x‚Üíc} f(x)/g(x) = lim_{t‚Üíc} f'(t)/g'(t) = L. ‚ąé Why the ‚ąě/‚ąě case is harder: when f(x) ‚Üí ‚ąě and g(x) ‚Üí ‚ąě, we cannot write f(x)/g(x) = [f(x)-0]/[g(x)-0] because f(c) ‚Č† 0. The Cauchy MVT trick requires f(c) = 0 and g(c) = 0. For the ‚ąě/‚ąě case, a different argument is needed ‚ÄĒ typically via the Stolz-Ces√†ro theorem (the discrete analog) or by showing lim f/g = lim f'/g' using a more delicate limit argument involving writing f(x)/g(x) = [f(x)-f(a)]/[g(x)-g(a)]¬∑[1 - f(a)/f(x)]/[1 - g(a)/g(x)] and taking a ‚Üí c. The ‚ąě/‚ąě proof is found in Rudin's Principles of Mathematical Analysis. When L'H√īpital fails: the rule requires lim f'/g' to exist. If f'/g' oscillates without a limit, the rule gives no information ‚ÄĒ but the original limit may still exist. The canonical example: lim_{x‚Üí‚ąě} (x + sin(x))/x. Applying the rule: lim (1 + cos(x))/1 = lim (1 + cos(x)), which oscillates between 0 and 2 ‚ÄĒ no limit. Yet (x + sin(x))/x = 1 + sin(x)/x ‚Üí 1 + 0 = 1 by the squeeze theorem. L'H√īpital's rule said nothing wrong ‚ÄĒ it was inapplicable, since lim f'/g' does not exist. The lesson: if L'H√īpital gives an indeterminate result or oscillation, try another method. The failure of L'H√īpital does not imply the original limit fails. Repeated application: the rule can be applied as many times as necessary, as long as each intermediate limit is still 0/0 or ‚ąě/‚ąě. For example, lim_{x‚Üí0} (1-cos(x))/x¬≤ is 0/0. Apply: lim sin(x)/(2x) ‚ÄĒ still 0/0. Apply again: lim cos(x)/2 = 1/2. Each application removes one degree of the Taylor polynomial in both numerator and denominator, eventually leaving a well-defined limit. For polynomials, the number of applications equals the common order of vanishing at c. Classic: lim(x‚Üí0) sin(x)/x lim(x‚Üí0) sin(x)/x = 1, confirmed by L'H√īpital. This matches the squeeze-theorem result from Chapter 1. L'H√īpital is much faster but requires knowing the derivative of sin(x), which was itself proved using the squeeze theorem limit. Avoid circular reasoning. Always verify the indeterminate form before applying L'H√īpital. Apply L'H√īpital: differentiate numerator (sin(x))' = cos(x) and denominator (x)' = 1 separately. Substitute x = 0. No longer indeterminate. The limit is 1. lim(x‚Üí0) (e^x - 1)/x lim(x‚Üí0) (e^x - 1)/x = 1. This is the limit definition of e^x's derivative at x=0, confirming that (d/dx)[e^x]|_{x=0} = 1. L'H√īpital makes this immediate. Verify: both numerator and denominator approach 0. Differentiate: (e^x - 1)' = e^x. (x)' = 1. The limit is e^0 = 1. lim(x‚Üí1) (x¬≥ - 1)/(x - 1) The limit is 3. Both L'H√īpital and factoring work; the factoring approach is neater when the factorization is obvious. L'H√īpital is preferable for transcendental functions where factoring is impossible. Verify indeterminate form. Apply L'H√īpital: differentiate numerator 3x¬≤, denominator 1. Alternative: factor. Same answer confirms L'H√īpital is correct. lim(x‚Üí‚ąě) x¬∑e^(-x): 0¬∑‚ąě Form lim(x‚Üí‚ąě) x¬∑e^{-x} = 0. Exponential growth dominates polynomial growth. More generally, lim_{x‚Üí‚ąě} x^n e^{-x} = 0 for any fixed n ‚ÄĒ apply L'H√īpital n times, each time reducing the power of x by 1. Identify the indeterminate form. Rewrite as a ratio: move e^{-x} to the denominator as e^x. Apply L'H√īpital: (x)' = 1, (e^x)' = e^x. The result 1/e^x ‚Üí 0 as x ‚Üí ‚ąě. lim(x‚Üí0‚Āļ) x¬∑ln(x): 0¬∑‚ąě Form lim(x‚Üí0‚Āļ) x ln(x) = 0. Although ln(x) ‚Üí -‚ąě as x ‚Üí 0‚Āļ, the factor x ‚Üí 0 wins. This result appears in the entropy formula -x¬∑ln(x) ‚Üí 0 as x ‚Üí 0 (a probability of 0 contributes 0 to entropy), which is important in information theory. Identify: 0¬∑‚ąě indeterminate form. Rewrite: put 1/x in denominator. Now ‚ąě/‚ąě form. Differentiate: (ln x)' = 1/x, (1/x)' = -1/x¬≤. Simplify the ratio: (1/x)/(-1/x¬≤) = (1/x)¬∑(-x¬≤) = -x ‚Üí 0. lim(x‚Üí0‚Āļ) x^x: 0^0 Form lim(x‚Üí0‚Āļ) x^x = 1. The 0^0 indeterminate form resolves to 1 in this case. Note: 0^0 is not always 1 ‚ÄĒ the value depends on how the base and exponent approach 0. The function f(x) = x^x approaches 1 as x ‚Üí 0‚Āļ, and by convention 0^0 = 1 in combinatorics. Identify the 0^0 indeterminate form. Take logarithm of both sides: ln(x^x) = x¬∑ln(x). We computed this limit in Example 5 above. Since ln(L) = 0, exponentiate: L = e^0 = 1. lim(x‚Üí‚ąě) (1 + 1/x)^x = e: the 1^‚ąě Form lim(x‚Üí‚ąě)(1+1/x)^x = e. This is one of the most important limits in mathematics ‚ÄĒ it is sometimes taken as the definition of e. L'H√īpital makes the proof systematic. The limit (1+r/n)^n ‚Üí e^r as n ‚Üí ‚ąě is the basis of continuous compounding in finance. Identify the 1^‚ąě indeterminate form. Take logarithm. Now we have a 0¬∑‚ąě form: x ‚Üí ‚ąě and ln(1+1/x) ‚Üí 0. Rewrite as ratio (1/x in denominator). Both numerator and denominator ‚Üí 0. Apply L'H√īpital. Numerator derivative: d/dx[ln(1+1/x)] = (1/(1+1/x))¬∑(-1/x¬≤). Denominator derivative: d/dx[1/x] = -1/x¬≤. The -1/x¬≤ factors cancel. As x ‚Üí ‚ąě, 1/x ‚Üí 0, so the limit is 1. Exponentiate: L = e. This is the definition of e as a limit. Prove that lim(x‚Üí0) (1-cos(x))/x¬≤ = 1/2 in three ways: (a) geometric, (b) L'H√īpital, (c) power series. Find lim(x‚Üí0‚Āļ) (sin(x))^{tan(x)}. This is a 0^0 form. Show that L'H√īpital's Rule fails for lim(x‚Üí‚ąě) (x + sin(x))/x even though the limit exists. Explain WHY it fails.

documents.title: Area and Accumulation
documents.subtitle: The area under a rate curve equals the total accumulated quantity ‚ÄĒ the founding idea of integral calculus
documents.tags: accumulation area under curve rate functions signed area integral notation velocity displacement net change
documents.content: The simplest accumulation problem is constant rate: if water flows at a steady 5 litres per minute for 3 minutes, the total is 5 √ó 3 = 15 litres. On a graph of rate r versus time t, the rate is a horizontal line at r = 5. The total accumulated quantity is the area of the rectangle under that line: width 3 (time interval) times height 5 (rate) equals 15. This geometric observation ‚ÄĒ area under the rate curve equals total accumulated quantity ‚ÄĒ is the seed of integration. Now suppose the rate is not constant but changes linearly: r(t) = 3t m/s (a car accelerating from rest). In the first 4 seconds, the rate rises from 0 to 12 m/s. The velocity-time graph is a straight line through the origin. The region under the graph from t = 0 to t = 4 is a right triangle with base 4 and height 12. Its area is ¬Ĺ √ó 4 √ó 12 = 24. The car travels 24 metres. This is not a coincidence ‚ÄĒ it is the same logic. Distance is area under the velocity curve, because each thin vertical strip of the graph has width dt (a tiny time interval) and height v(t) (speed at that instant), contributing v(t) dt metres to the total. For curved rate functions, the triangle and rectangle formulas no longer apply directly. But the underlying logic is unchanged. Slice the time axis into many small intervals. In each tiny interval [tŠĶĘ, tŠĶĘ‚āä‚āĀ] of width őĒt, the rate barely changes ‚ÄĒ it is approximately r(tŠĶĘ). The amount accumulated in that slice is approximately r(tŠĶĘ) ¬∑ őĒt, the area of a thin rectangle. Sum all these rectangles, and you have an approximation to the total. The more slices you use, the finer the approximation and the closer you get to the true total. In the limit as the slice width approaches zero, the sum of rectangles becomes the area under the curve exactly. This limit of sums is the definite integral, written ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx. The elongated S symbol (‚ąę) stands for "sum" ‚ÄĒ Leibniz chose it to emphasize that the integral is a limiting summation. The symbol dx represents the infinitesimal width of each slice. The limits a and b mark the start and end of the interval. The entire expression means: "sum up f(x) ¬∑ dx for every x from a to b." This is the most compact and powerful notation ever invented for an accumulation process. The connection between area and accumulation has a directional aspect: signed area. If a velocity is negative (moving backward), the contribution to displacement is negative. If a rate function dips below the t-axis, the integral subtracts that portion. The definite integral computes net accumulation ‚ÄĒ positive contributions minus negative contributions. For total accumulation (ignoring direction), you integrate the absolute value |f(x)|. This signed vs. unsigned distinction is important in applications: displacement (net change in position) uses the signed integral, while total distance traveled uses the integral of |v(t)|. The historical roots of integration go back to Archimedes, who computed the area of a parabolic segment around 250 BC by exhausting it with triangles ‚ÄĒ a method strikingly close to modern Riemann sums. Cavalieri in the 1630s summed infinitesimal slices to compute volumes. But the real leap came from Newton and Leibniz in the 1660s‚Äď1680s: they independently discovered that the process of accumulation is the inverse of the process of differentiation. This Fundamental Theorem of Calculus ‚ÄĒ which we develop in Lesson 3 ‚ÄĒ transforms integration from a geometric puzzle into an algebraic calculation. Formally, suppose f is a rate function defined on [a, b]. We introduce the notation ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx for the total accumulation of f over [a, b]. This is called the definite integral of f from a to b. The variable x is a dummy variable ‚ÄĒ ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx and ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(t) dt and ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(u) du all denote the same number. The integral depends only on the function f and the limits a and b, not on the name of the variable of integration. Three basic properties follow immediately from the signed-area interpretation. First, ‚ąę‚āźŠĶÉ f(x) dx = 0: integrating over a zero-width interval accumulates nothing. Second, ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx = ‚ąí ‚ąęŠĶ¶ŠĶÉ f(x) dx: reversing the limits of integration changes the sign, because we are now "sweeping" in the opposite direction. Third, linearity: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá [c¬∑f(x) + g(x)] dx = c¬∑‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx + ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá g(x) dx for any constants c. These properties let us split, reverse, and scale integrals algebraically. The additive interval property is essential in applications: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx + ‚ąęŠĶ¶Š∂ú f(x) dx = ‚ąę‚āźŠ∂ú f(x) dx. Total accumulation from a to c equals accumulation from a to b plus accumulation from b to c, regardless of where b falls (even outside [a, c]). This property allows us to break complex intervals into simpler pieces and reassemble the result. Two comparison properties complete the foundation. If f(x) ‚Č• 0 on [a, b], then ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx ‚Č• 0 (the area of a non-negative function is non-negative). If f(x) ‚Č• g(x) on [a, b], then ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f ‚Č• ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá g. These allow bounding integrals without computing them exactly: if m ‚Č§ f(x) ‚Č§ M on [a, b], then m(b‚ąía) ‚Č§ ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f ‚Č§ M(b‚ąía). This sandwich estimate is useful in numerical analysis and in proving convergence of approximation methods. The signed-area interpretation of the definite integral is intuitive but not yet mathematically precise. To define ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx rigorously, we need to specify exactly what "area under a curve" means for a general function f, including functions with corners, kinks, and even discontinuities. The rigorous definition via Riemann sums ‚ÄĒ developed in Lesson 1 ‚ÄĒ fills this gap. For now, it suffices to note that the informal definition is consistent with all the properties stated above, and that a formal proof of each property follows directly from the limit definition once it is established. The property ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f = ‚ąí‚ąęŠĶ¶ŠĶÉ f is not merely a convention ‚ÄĒ it is required for the additive property to be consistent. If ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f + ‚ąęŠĶ¶ŠĶÉ f were required to equal ‚ąę‚āźŠĶÉ f = 0, then ‚ąęŠĶ¶ŠĶÉ f = ‚ąí‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f follows by algebra. So the reversed-limits sign rule is dictated by the interval additivity property. Once we define the integral via Riemann sums for a ‚Č§ b, we extend the definition to a > b by declaring ‚ąęŠĶ¶ŠĶÉ f = ‚ąí‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f. The linearity property ‚ÄĒ ‚ąę(cf + g) = c‚ąęf + ‚ąęg ‚ÄĒ has deep consequences. It means the integral is a linear functional on the space of integrable functions. The set of Riemann-integrable functions on [a, b] forms a vector space, and the map f ‚Ü¶ ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx is a linear map from that vector space to ‚ĄĚ. Linear functionals are central objects in functional analysis and underlie the theory of distributions (which extends integration to "generalized functions" like the Dirac delta). The simple linearity property of the integral is the entry point to one of the richest areas of modern mathematics. The bound m(b‚ąía) ‚Č§ ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f ‚Č§ M(b‚ąía) has a celebrated consequence: the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals. If f is continuous on [a, b], then the average value (1/(b‚ąía))‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f lies between m and M, and by the Intermediate Value Theorem applied to f, there exists a point c ‚ąą (a, b) where f(c) equals this average. This MVT for integrals is the integral analog of the derivative MVT and is used in the proof of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Distance from a Triangular Velocity Profile The car travels 24 metres in the first 4 seconds. The velocity-time graph is a right triangle, and its area gives the displacement. This confirms the core idea: distance = area under the velocity curve. The velocity-time graph is a straight line from (0,0) to (4,12). The region under it is a right triangle. Distance = area under v(t). The triangle has base 4 (time) and height 12 (speed at t=4). Verification using antiderivatives (from Lesson 3): the antiderivative of 3t is 3t¬≤/2. Evaluating at t=4 and t=0 gives 24 ‚ąí 0 = 24 m. The geometric and algebraic approaches are two faces of the same calculation. Water Tank: Linear Flow Rate 25 litres enter the tank over 5 minutes. The flow rate decreases linearly from 10 to 0, forming a right triangle in the rate-time graph. Area = ¬Ĺ √ó 5 √ó 10 = 25 L. The rate function r(t) is a straight line from 10 (at t=0) to 0 (at t=5). The flow is always non-negative on this interval, so no water flows OUT. Draw the graph: r(0)=10, r(5)=0. The region is a right triangle. Area of right triangle = ¬Ĺ √ó base √ó height = ¬Ĺ √ó 5 √ó 10 = 25 litres. Antiderivative confirmation: ‚ąę(10‚ąí2t)dt = 10t‚ąít¬≤. Evaluated at 5 minus at 0 gives 25. Estimating Distance from a Velocity Graph (No Formula) Without a formula, we estimate area using trapezoids ‚ÄĒ averaging adjacent velocity readings and multiplying by the time width. The runner traveled approximately 28 m. This is essentially the trapezoidal rule for numerical integration, covered more formally in Lesson 1. When the velocity function is given as a graph or table, we estimate the area by geometric methods. Trapezoidal rule: each pair of adjacent readings forms a trapezoid. Area = ¬Ĺ(v‚āĀ+v‚āā) √ó őĒt. Trapezoid areas for the first three intervals. Trapezoid areas for the last two intervals. Sum all trapezoid areas. The runner travels approximately 28 metres. Electricity Usage: Writing the Integral Total energy = 4.8 kWh; average power = 200 W. The cosine fluctuation (daytime peaks, nighttime troughs) contributes zero net energy over a full 24-hour cycle. This illustrates the key property: ‚ąęcos = sin, and sin(2ŌÄ)‚ąísin(0) = 0. Total energy (in watt-hours) is the definite integral of power over the 24-hour period. The constant term contributes 200 √ó 24 = 4800 watt-hours (using ‚ąęconstant = constant √ó width). The cosine term integrates to zero over a full period (24 hours = 2 full periods of the cosine). The oscillation contributes no net energy. Total energy is 4800 watt-hours = 4.8 kilowatt-hours over 24 hours. Average power = total energy / time. The oscillation averages out; the average power is just the constant term 200 W. CO‚āā Accumulation: Interpreting a Definite Integral 500 billion tonnes of CO‚āā were emitted over the decade. The integral converts a rate (tonnes/year) into a quantity (tonnes), just as integrating speed (m/s) gives distance (m). The units work out: [tonnes/year] √ó [year] = [tonnes]. The integral of an emission rate over time gives the total cumulative emissions over that period ‚ÄĒ in this case, from 2010 to 2020. Antiderivative: ‚ąę(40+2t)dt = 40t+t¬≤. Evaluate at t=10: 400+100=500. Subtract the value at t=0 (which is 0). The definite integral converts the time-varying emission rate into a total cumulative quantity. Area = ¬Ĺ(40+60)√ó10 = ¬Ĺ√ó100√ó10 = 500. Confirms the calculation. Physics Derivation: Why Displacement = ‚ąęv dt Displacement = ‚ąęv dt is not a formula imposed from outside ‚ÄĒ it follows directly from the definition v = dx/dt. The integral undoes the derivative. This is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus in physical disguise, and it motivates the entire development in Lesson 3. By definition, velocity is the derivative of position with respect to time. This is the starting point. Rearranging: dx = v(t) dt. The infinitesimal change in position equals velocity times the infinitesimal time step. This is just the linearisation of x(t) over dt. Sum all infinitesimal displacements over the full time interval [a, b]. This is a Riemann sum for v(t). As dt ‚Üí 0, the sum converges to the definite integral. This is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus applied to velocity and position. The same logic gives: velocity = integral of acceleration; momentum = integral of force (impulse-momentum theorem). A car moves at constant velocity v = 30 m/s for t ‚ąą [0, 5] s. (a) Compute the displacement using the rectangle formula. (b) Write this as a definite integral and verify using the antiderivative. A cyclist's velocity profile is v(t) = 6t ‚ąí t¬≤ m/s for t ‚ąą [0, 6]. (a) Sketch the velocity-time graph and identify when v = 0. (b) Use the geometric area of a parabolic region to estimate the distance traveled. The exact area under a parabola f(t)=6t‚ąít¬≤ from 0 to 6 is ‚ąę‚āÄ‚Ā∂(6t‚ąít¬≤)dt ‚ÄĒ compute this using antiderivatives. A particle moves with velocity v(t) = e^(‚ąít) m/s for t ‚ąą [0, ‚ąě). (a) Explain why the total distance traveled in infinite time is finite. (b) Compute ‚ąę‚āÄ^‚ąě e^(‚ąít) dt as the limit lim_{b‚Üí‚ąě} ‚ąę‚āÄŠĶá e^(‚ąít) dt. (c) Interpret the result physically.

documents.title: Riemann Sums
documents.subtitle: Approximating area with rectangles ‚ÄĒ and taking the limit to get the exact integral
documents.tags: Riemann sums left sum right sum midpoint rule trapezoidal rule partition limit of sums definite integral definition numerical integration
documents.content: The idea is transparent: divide the interval [a, b] into n equal subintervals, each of width őĒx = (b‚ąía)/n. On each subinterval, approximate the function by a horizontal line ‚ÄĒ its value at some representative point in the subinterval. The area of the resulting thin rectangle is f(representative point) √ó őĒx. Sum all the rectangles. This is a Riemann sum. There are three natural choices for the representative point in each subinterval. The left endpoint rule uses the left end of each subinterval: L‚āô = [f(x‚āÄ) + f(x‚āĀ) + ¬∑¬∑¬∑ + f(x‚āô‚āč‚āĀ)]¬∑őĒx. The right endpoint rule uses the right end: R‚āô = [f(x‚āĀ) + f(x‚āā) + ¬∑¬∑¬∑ + f(x‚āô)]¬∑őĒx. The midpoint rule uses the midpoint xŐĄŠĶĘ = (xŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ+xŠĶĘ)/2: M‚āô = [f(xŐĄ‚āĀ) + f(xŐĄ‚āā) + ¬∑¬∑¬∑ + f(xŐĄ‚āô)]¬∑őĒx. For an increasing function, the left sum underestimates (the rectangles all fall short of the curve) and the right sum overestimates (the rectangles all poke above the curve). The midpoint rule is typically the most accurate of the three for smooth functions.
