As n grows, all three methods converge to the same limit ‚ÄĒ provided f is integrable (which all continuous functions are). With n = 4 rectangles, L‚āĄ and R‚āĄ for ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx are 0.21875 and 0.46875 respectively. The true answer is 1/3 ‚Čą 0.3333. With n = 100 rectangles, R‚āô ‚Čą 0.3383 ‚ÄĒ much closer. With n = 1000, R‚āô ‚Čą 0.3343. The error decreases proportionally to 1/n, so doubling n halves the error. The trapezoidal rule T‚āô = (L‚āô + R‚āô)/2 is the average of the left and right sums. Geometrically, it replaces rectangles with trapezoids ‚ÄĒ connecting f(xŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ) and f(xŠĶĘ) with a straight line across each subinterval. The trapezoid area on [xŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ, xŠĶĘ] is ¬Ĺ(f(xŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ)+f(xŠĶĘ))¬∑őĒx. For T‚āĄ on ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx: T‚āĄ = (0.21875 + 0.46875)/2 = 0.34375. This is already much closer to 1/3 than either L‚āĄ or R‚āĄ alone, because the trapezoidal error is proportional to 1/n¬≤, not 1/n. For a concrete calculation of L‚āĄ on ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx: the four subintervals are [0, 0.25], [0.25, 0.5], [0.5, 0.75], [0.75, 1]. Left endpoints: 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75. Function values: 0, 0.0625, 0.25, 0.5625. Sum = 0.875. Multiply by őĒx = 0.25: L‚āĄ = 0.21875. For R‚āĄ, right endpoints: 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1. Function values: 0.0625, 0.25, 0.5625, 1. Sum = 1.875. Multiply by 0.25: R‚āĄ = 0.46875. Average: 0.34375. The true area 1/3 ‚Čą 0.3333 is sandwiched between L‚āĄ and R‚āĄ, as expected for an increasing function. An important conceptual point: the definition of the definite integral does not require equal-width subintervals. The general Riemann sum allows arbitrary partitions a = x‚āÄ < x‚āĀ < ¬∑¬∑¬∑ < x‚āô = b and arbitrary representative points cŠĶĘ ‚ąą [xŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ, xŠĶĘ]. The integral is defined as the limit of Riemann sums as the mesh size (width of the widest subinterval) approaches zero, provided this limit exists and is the same for all choices of partitions and representative points. For continuous functions ‚ÄĒ and even for bounded functions with only finitely many discontinuities ‚ÄĒ this limit always exists. This robustness is what makes the Riemann integral so useful in practice. Let f be a function defined on [a, b]. A partition of [a, b] is a finite sequence a = x‚āÄ < x‚āĀ < x‚āā < ¬∑¬∑¬∑ < x‚āô = b. For a uniform partition of n equal subintervals, őĒx = (b‚ąía)/n and xŠĶĘ = a + i¬∑őĒx for i = 0, 1, ‚Ä¶, n. The left Riemann sum, right Riemann sum, and midpoint Riemann sum are respectively: L‚āô = ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āÄ‚ĀŅ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ f(xŠĶĘ)¬∑őĒx, R‚āô = ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ f(xŠĶĘ)¬∑őĒx, M‚āô = ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ f(xŐĄŠĶĘ)¬∑őĒx where xŐĄŠĶĘ = (xŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ+xŠĶĘ)/2 is the midpoint. The definite integral is defined as the common limit of all Riemann sums (when the limit exists): ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx = lim_{n‚Üí‚ąě} R‚āô = lim_{n‚Üí‚ąě} L‚āô = lim_{n‚Üí‚ąě} M‚āô. More precisely, using general (non-uniform) partitions: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx = lim_{‚ÄĖP‚ÄĖ‚Üí0} ő£ f(cŠĶĘ)(xŠĶĘ‚ąíxŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ), where ‚ÄĖP‚ÄĖ = max(xŠĶĘ‚ąíxŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ) is the mesh size of the partition and cŠĶĘ ‚ąą [xŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ, xŠĶĘ] is any representative point. A function f is called Riemann integrable if this limit exists and is independent of the choice of partitions and representative points. For ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx, we can compute R‚āô exactly using the sum formula ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ i¬≤ = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6. With xŠĶĘ = i/n and őĒx = 1/n: R‚āô = ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ (i/n)¬≤¬∑(1/n) = (1/n¬≥)ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ i¬≤ = (1/n¬≥)¬∑n(n+1)(2n+1)/6 = (n+1)(2n+1)/(6n¬≤). As n ‚Üí ‚ąě: (n+1)(2n+1)/(6n¬≤) = (2n¬≤+3n+1)/(6n¬≤) ‚Üí 2/6 = 1/3. So ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx = 1/3 exactly. The error analysis for the trapezoidal rule is instructive. For a function f with bounded second derivative |f''| ‚Č§ M on [a, b], the trapezoidal rule error satisfies |T‚āô ‚ąí ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f| ‚Č§ M(b‚ąía)¬≥/(12n¬≤). The error decreases as 1/n¬≤, which is faster than the 1/n rate for the left and right sums. For ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx, f''= 2, so error ‚Č§ 2√ó1/(12n¬≤) = 1/(6n¬≤). With n = 10: error ‚Č§ 1/600 ‚Čą 0.00167. Actual error: T‚āĀ‚āÄ = 0.335 ‚ąí 1/3 ‚Čą 0.00167. The bound is tight. The formal definition of Riemann integrability requires that the limit of Riemann sums be independent of the choice of partition and sample points. This is a demanding condition. It is equivalent to requiring that, for every őĶ > 0, there exists őī > 0 such that for any partition P with mesh ‚ÄĖP‚ÄĖ < őī and any choice of sample points, |S‚āô ‚ąí I| < őĶ (where I is the proposed integral). Functions satisfying this are called Darboux integrable, and the Darboux integral (defined via upper and lower sums) is equivalent to the Riemann integral for bounded functions. The class of Riemann integrable functions on [a, b] is larger than one might expect. Every continuous function is integrable (this follows from uniform continuity on closed bounded intervals). Every bounded monotone function is integrable, even if it has countably many discontinuities. Every bounded function with finitely many discontinuities is integrable. Remarkably, a bounded function is Riemann integrable if and only if its set of discontinuities has Lebesgue measure zero (Lebesgue's criterion). This means that even highly irregular functions can be integrated, as long as they are "discontinuous only on a negligible set." Not all functions are Riemann integrable. The classic example is the Dirichlet function: D(x) = 1 if x is rational, D(x) = 0 if x is irrational. This function is discontinuous everywhere on [0,1]. For any partition, every subinterval contains both rationals and irrationals, so the upper Darboux sum is always 1 and the lower Darboux sum is always 0. The upper and lower sums never agree, so D is not Riemann integrable. The Lebesgue integral, a more powerful theory, can integrate D: ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ D dx = 0 (because the rationals form a set of measure zero, and D = 0 almost everywhere). The connection between Riemann sums and the definite integral is made rigorous by the following theorem: if f is continuous on [a, b], then for any sequence of partitions P‚āô with ‚ÄĖP‚āô‚ÄĖ ‚Üí 0 and any choice of sample points cŠĶĘ ‚ąą [xŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ, xŠĶĘ], the Riemann sums converge to ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx. The proof uses the uniform continuity of f on [a, b] (from the Heine-Cantor theorem): given őĶ > 0, choose őī such that |f(u) ‚ąí f(v)| < őĶ/(b‚ąía) whenever |u‚ąív| < őī. Then for any two Riemann sums with the same partition and mesh < őī, their values differ by less than őĶ. From this, one shows the limit exists and equals the Darboux integral. L‚āĄ, R‚āĄ, M‚āĄ for ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx With just 4 rectangles: L‚āĄ=0.219 (underestimates by 31%), R‚āĄ=0.469 (overestimates by 41%), M‚āĄ=0.328 (error <2%). The midpoint rule is far more efficient than left/right for smooth functions. Partition [0,1] into 4 equal subintervals. Width = 0.25. Endpoints at 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1. Left sum uses the LEFT endpoint of each subinterval: x‚āÄ=0, x‚āĀ=0.25, x‚āā=0.5, x‚āÉ=0.75. f(0)=0, f(0.25)=0.0625, f(0.5)=0.25, f(0.75)=0.5625. Sum = 0.875. Multiply by őĒx=0.25. Right sum uses the RIGHT endpoint of each subinterval: 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1. f(1)=1. Sum = 1.875. Multiply by 0.25. Since x¬≤ is increasing, R‚āĄ overestimates. Midpoints: 0.125, 0.375, 0.625, 0.875. Midpoint sum is between L‚āĄ and R‚āĄ and closer to the true value. All three bracket the true value 1/3. The midpoint rule is the most accurate here. L‚āÉ, R‚āÉ for ‚ąę‚āĀ¬≥ (1/x) dx L‚āÉ‚Čą1.352 (overestimates by 23%) and R‚āÉ‚Čą0.908 (underestimates by 17%). The average T‚āÉ=(1.352+0.908)/2=1.130 is already much closer. With 30 rectangles both sums would be within 2.5%. Partition [1,3] into 3 equal subintervals. Width = 2/3. Endpoints at 1, 5/3, 7/3, 3. f(x) = 1/x. f(1)=1, f(5/3)=3/5=0.6, f(7/3)=3/7‚Čą0.4286. Common denominator 105: 1=105/105, 3/5=63/105, 3/7=45/105. Sum = 213/105. Multiply by 2/3. Right endpoints: 5/3, 7/3, 3. f(3)=1/3=35/105. Sum=143/105. Multiply by 2/3. 1/x is decreasing on [1,3], so L‚āÉ overestimates and R‚āÉ underestimates. The true value is sandwiched. Trapezoidal Rule T‚āĄ for ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx T‚āĄ=0.34375 has error ‚Čą0.010 vs. L‚āĄ error ‚Čą0.115. The trapezoidal rule achieves O(1/n¬≤) accuracy, making it roughly n times more efficient than left/right sums for the same n. The trapezoidal rule is exactly the average of the left and right sums (using the same partition). The left sum is far from the true value 1/3 ‚Čą 0.3333. The right sum is even farther. The trapezoidal rule is 10‚Äď13 times more accurate than the one-sided sums, with the same number of function evaluations. Written out explicitly with endpoints doubled (except the first and last): T‚āĄ = (0.25/2)[0 + 2(0.0625) + 2(0.25) + 2(0.5625) + 1] = (0.125)[2.75] = 0.34375. Consistent. Riemann Sum from a Table (Discrete Data) The car traveled approximately 31 metres in 4 seconds. When only discrete data is available, the trapezoidal rule is the most natural approach: it assumes the velocity varies linearly between measurements, which is reasonable if measurements are frequent. The data points are equally spaced at őĒt = 1 second. No formula ‚ÄĒ we apply the trapezoidal rule directly. Trapezoidal rule: endpoints get weight 1, interior points get weight 2. Substitute: v(0)=0, v(1)=5, v(2)=9, v(3)=11, v(4)=12. Sum = 62. Multiply by ¬Ĺ. The car traveled approximately 31 metres. Exact Limit: ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx = 1/3 from R‚āô ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ x¬≤ dx = 1/3, proved rigorously from the definition as a limit of Riemann sums. The error of R‚āô is (1/2n)+(1/6n¬≤) ‚Čą 1/(2n), confirming the O(1/n) error rate for the right-endpoint rule. Uniform partition of [0,1] into n subintervals. Right endpoint of i-th interval is i/n. f(x)=x¬≤, so f(i/n)=(i/n)¬≤. Factor out 1/n¬≥. Apply the sum formula. Simplify: n/n¬≥ = 1/n¬≤. Expand numerator and split into fractions over 6n¬≤. As n ‚Üí ‚ąě, the terms 1/(2n) and 1/(6n¬≤) both ‚Üí 0. The limit is exactly 1/3. Error Analysis: How Many Rectangles for 1% Accuracy? Left/right sums need n = 500 for 0.1% accuracy. The trapezoidal rule achieves the same accuracy with just n = 13 ‚ÄĒ a 38√ó improvement. Higher-order methods (Simpson's rule, Gaussian quadrature) push this even further, enabling high accuracy with very few function evaluations. From the previous example: error ‚Čą 1/(2n) for large n. We use the cruder bound 1/n to simplify. Using the leading term 1/(2n): need n > 500 for the error to fall below 0.001. Compute R‚āÖ‚āÄ‚āÄ using the exact formula. 501√ó1001 = 501501. Error is exactly 0.001 at n=500. Confirmed. With the trapezoidal rule, error ‚Č§ 1/(6n¬≤). For error < 0.001: n > ‚ąö(1000/6) ‚Čą 12.9. So n = 13 suffices! Compute L‚āĄ and R‚āĄ for ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤ (x¬≤ + 1) dx. Identify which sum overestimates and which underestimates, and why. Using sigma notation, derive a closed-form expression for R‚āô for ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤ x¬≥ dx, and compute the exact limit to verify that ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤ x¬≥ dx = 4. Use the sum formula ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ i¬≥ = [n(n+1)/2]¬≤. Prove the following: for a function f that is increasing and continuous on [a, b], the error |R‚āô ‚ąí ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f| satisfies |R‚āô ‚ąí ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f| ‚Č§ [f(b) ‚ąí f(a)](b‚ąía)/n. Interpret this formula: the error depends on the "total rise" of f and the interval width, but decreases like 1/n.

documents.title: The Definite Integral
documents.subtitle: A single number encoding signed area ‚ÄĒ properties, geometry, and the average value of a function
documents.tags: definite integral signed area properties of integrals average value mean value theorem for integrals linearity additivity odd functions even functions
documents.content: The definite integral ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx is a single real number. It measures signed area: regions where f > 0 contribute positively, regions where f < 0 contribute negatively. The "signed" aspect is essential for physics ‚ÄĒ velocity can be negative (motion in the opposite direction), and the signed area gives displacement (net change in position), not total distance. Keeping track of signs is not a complication; it is the feature that makes the integral physically meaningful. Linearity is the most useful property in practice. ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá [c¬∑f(x) + g(x)] dx = c¬∑‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx + ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá g(x) dx. This means you can split a complicated integral into simpler pieces. To compute ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤ (3x¬≤ + 5‚ąöx) dx, you can compute ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤ 3x¬≤ dx and ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤ 5‚ąöx dx separately and add. Any polynomial, any sum of trigonometric functions, any linear combination of integrable functions can be integrated piece by piece. Linearity is the reason integration is "not that hard" for elementary functions. The interval additivity property ‚ąę‚āźŠ∂ú f = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f + ‚ąęŠĶ¶Š∂ú f allows you to break the domain of integration anywhere. To compute ‚ąę‚āč‚āā‚ĀĶ f, split at x = 0: ‚ąę‚āč‚āā‚Āį f + ‚ąę‚āÄ‚ĀĶ f. This is powerful when f behaves differently on different sub-intervals (for example, if f has a corner at 0, or changes sign at 0). For |f(x)| dx (total area), split at every zero of f and negate the negative pieces: ‚ąę|f| = ‚ąę‚āćpositive regions‚āé f ‚ąí ‚ąę‚āćnegative regions‚āé f. Symmetry is the most elegant shortcut for computing integrals. For an ODD function ‚ÄĒ one satisfying f(‚ąíx) = ‚ąíf(x) ‚ÄĒ the graph is symmetric about the origin. The area above the x-axis on [0, b] is exactly canceled by the equal-and-opposite area below the x-axis on [‚ąíb, 0]. Therefore ‚ąę‚āč‚āźŠĶÉ f(x) dx = 0 for any odd function integrated over a symmetric interval. For an EVEN function ‚ÄĒ f(‚ąíx) = f(x), symmetric about the y-axis ‚ÄĒ the areas on [‚ąía, 0] and [0, a] are equal, so ‚ąę‚āč‚āźŠĶÉ f(x) dx = 2‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f(x) dx. These shortcuts save enormous computational effort. The average value of f on [a, b] is defined as f_avg = (1/(b‚ąía)) ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx. This is the continuous analog of the arithmetic mean (average) of a list of numbers: instead of summing n values and dividing by n, we integrate the function and divide by the interval length. Geometrically, f_avg is the height of the rectangle with base [a, b] that has exactly the same area as the region under f. The rectangle is the "flattened-out" version of f. The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees that f_avg is actually attained by f somewhere in (a, b), not just an abstract average. If f is continuous, then there exists at least one point c ‚ąą (a, b) where f(c) = f_avg. This is deeply intuitive: the continuous function must pass through its average. For f(x) = x¬≤ on [0, 3], f_avg = (1/3)‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥ x¬≤ dx = (1/3)(9) = 3. The equation f(c) = 3 gives c¬≤ = 3, so c = ‚ąö3 ‚Čą 1.73. The function x¬≤ takes the value 3 at x = ‚ąö3, which is inside (0, 3), exactly as the theorem predicts. The formal properties of the definite integral follow from the limit-of-Riemann-sums definition. Linearity: for constants c, d and integrable functions f, g on [a, b], ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá [c¬∑f(x) + d¬∑g(x)] dx = c¬∑‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx + d¬∑‚ąę‚āźŠĶá g(x) dx. Proof sketch: the Riemann sums for cf + dg are exactly c times the sums for f plus d times the sums for g, and limits preserve linear combinations. Comparison properties: (1) if f(x) ‚Č• 0 on [a, b], then ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f ‚Č• 0; (2) if f(x) ‚Č• g(x) on [a, b], then ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f ‚Č• ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá g; (3) the absolute value inequality: |‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx| ‚Č§ ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá |f(x)| dx. Property (3) is the integral analog of the triangle inequality for sums: |ő£aŠĶĘ| ‚Č§ ő£|aŠĶĘ|. It is used to bound integrals without computing them: if |f(x)| ‚Č§ M on [a, b], then |‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f| ‚Č§ M(b‚ąía). Mean Value Theorem for Integrals: if f is continuous on [a, b], then there exists c ‚ąą (a, b) such that ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx = f(c)(b‚ąía). The proof uses the EVT: f attains its minimum m and maximum M on [a, b]. The bound m(b‚ąía) ‚Č§ ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f ‚Č§ M(b‚ąía) gives m ‚Č§ (1/(b‚ąía))‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f ‚Č§ M. By the Intermediate Value Theorem (f is continuous and attains m and M), f must take every value between m and M, including the average value. So there exists c with f(c) = (1/(b‚ąía))‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f. Symmetry properties: if f is integrable and odd on [‚ąía, a], then ‚ąę‚āč‚āźŠĶÉ f(x) dx = 0. If f is even, ‚ąę‚āč‚āźŠĶÉ f(x) dx = 2‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f(x) dx. Proof for the odd case: substitute u = ‚ąíx in ‚ąę‚āč‚āź‚Āį f(x) dx to get ‚ąí‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f(‚ąíu)(‚ąídu) = ‚ąí‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f(u) du = ‚ąí‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f (since f is odd). So ‚ąę‚āč‚āź‚Āį f = ‚ąí‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f, and ‚ąę‚āč‚āźŠĶÉ f = ‚ąę‚āč‚āź‚Āį f + ‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f = ‚ąí‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f + ‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f = 0. The comparison property |‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f| ‚Č§ ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá |f| deserves a careful proof, since it is used repeatedly in analysis. For any Riemann sum: |ő£f(cŠĶĘ)őĒx| ‚Č§ ő£|f(cŠĶĘ)|őĒx by the triangle inequality for finite sums. Taking the limit as n ‚Üí ‚ąě: the left side converges to |‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f| and the right side to ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá |f| (assuming both f and |f| are integrable, which follows from f being integrable ‚ÄĒ since |f| is integrable whenever f is). The inequality is preserved in the limit, giving the result. The MVT for integrals requires both the Extreme Value Theorem and the Intermediate Value Theorem. The EVT guarantees f attains m = min f and M = max f on [a, b] (requiring continuity + closed bounded interval). The bound m ‚Č§ f_avg ‚Č§ M follows from the comparison property. The IVT guarantees f takes every value between m and M on [a, b] (requiring continuity). Since m ‚Č§ f_avg ‚Č§ M, f must take the value f_avg somewhere ‚ÄĒ call it c. Thus f(c) = f_avg. The careful reader will note that the IVT gives c in [a, b], not necessarily the open interval (a, b). The strict interior can be guaranteed if f_avg is not equal to m or M (which holds when f is non-constant), by a sharper application of the IVT. The symmetry properties give a window into the general technique of change of variables for integrals. The substitution u = ‚ąíx transforms ‚ąę‚āč‚āź‚Āį f(x) dx as follows: when x = ‚ąía, u = a; when x = 0, u = 0; dx = ‚ąídu. So ‚ąę‚āč‚āź‚Āį f(x) dx = ‚ąę‚āź‚Āį f(‚ąíu)(‚ąídu) = ‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f(‚ąíu) du. For an odd function, f(‚ąíu) = ‚ąíf(u), so this equals ‚ąí‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f(u) du. Adding ‚ąę‚āÄŠĶÉ f(u) du gives zero. This is a special case of the general substitution rule for definite integrals: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(g(x))g‚Ä≤(x) dx = ‚ąę_{g(a)}^{g(b)} f(u) du, valid whenever g is differentiable and f is continuous. This rule (the integral version of the chain rule) is the theoretical basis for the substitution technique in antiderivative computation. The definite integral over a point ‚ąę‚āźŠĶÉ f = 0 deserves a moment's attention. In measure theory, the integral is defined as ‚ąę f dőľ where őľ is a measure. For the Lebesgue measure on ‚ĄĚ, a single point {a} has measure zero, so ‚ąę_{a}^{a} f = 0 regardless of f(a) ‚ÄĒ even if f has a spike at a. This reflects the fact that a single point contributes nothing to the Riemann integral either: in any Riemann sum, the subinterval containing a can be taken to have arbitrarily small width, and f(a)¬∑őĒx ‚Üí 0. The measure-zero sets play the same role in the Lebesgue theory that single points play here. ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥ (2x+1) dx by Geometry ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥ (2x+1) dx = 12. For any linear function, the integral equals the trapezoid area ‚ÄĒ no calculus machinery needed. This confirms our geometric intuition and sets up a check for Lesson 3 (FTC Part 2 will give the same answer: [x¬≤+x]‚āÄ¬≥ = 9+3‚ąí0 = 12). The integrand is linear. The region under the graph from x=0 to x=3 is a trapezoid. The "parallel sides" are the vertical heights at x=0 and x=3; the width is the length of the interval. Trapezoid area formula: average of parallel sides times width. Confirmed: the definite integral equals the geometric area of the trapezoid. ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤ ‚ąö(4‚ąíx¬≤) dx = ŌÄ Using Geometry ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤‚ąö(4‚ąíx¬≤)dx = ŌÄ. This elegant result requires no antiderivative (the antiderivative of ‚ąö(4‚ąíx¬≤) involves arcsin, which is not yet available). Geometric reasoning gives the exact answer immediately. This illustrates the power of the area interpretation. The equation y = ‚ąö(4‚ąíx¬≤) with y ‚Č• 0 describes the upper semicircle of radius 2 centered at the origin. On [0,2], the curve goes from (0,2) to (2,0), tracing a quarter-circle in the first quadrant. Area = ŌÄr¬≤/4 = 4ŌÄ/4 = ŌÄ. The definite integral equals the area of the quarter-disk: exactly ŌÄ. ‚ąę‚āč‚āĀ¬Ļ x¬≥ dx = 0 by Symmetry ‚ąę‚āč‚āĀ¬Ļ x¬≥ dx = 0 by symmetry. No computation required ‚ÄĒ recognizing the odd function on a symmetric interval gives the answer instantly. This is one of the most powerful time-saving tricks in integral calculus. Verify odd symmetry: f(‚ąíx) = ‚ąíf(x) for all x. By the symmetry theorem: ‚ąę‚āč‚āźŠĶÉ (odd function) dx = 0. The positive area on [0,1] is exactly canceled by the negative area on [‚ąí1,0]. The antiderivative of x¬≥ is x‚Āī/4. Both endpoints give 1/4, so the difference is 0. Using Additivity: Finding ‚ąę‚āÉ‚ĀĶ f from Partial Information ‚ąę‚āÉ‚ĀĶ f = 7. The additivity property lets us extract partial integrals from known combinations ‚ÄĒ like solving an equation. This technique is essential whenever you only have access to aggregate data. Interval additivity: the integral from 0 to 5 splits at x=3. Substitute the known values. Solve for the unknown integral. Average Value and the MVT for Integrals The average value of x¬≤ on [0,3] is 3. The function achieves this average at c = ‚ąö3 ‚Čą 1.73, which lies inside (0,3) as the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees. Average value formula: (1/(b‚ąía)) √ó integral. Antiderivative of x¬≤ is x¬≥/3. Evaluate at 3 and 0. Average value of x¬≤ on [0,3] is 3. Solve f(c) = 3 for c. Since c must be in (0,3), we take the positive root c = ‚ąö3. Confirmed: c = ‚ąö3 ‚Čą 1.73 is in the open interval (0,3), as the MVT for integrals guarantees. ‚ąę‚āč‚āā¬≤ |x| dx Using Geometry ‚ąę‚āč‚āā¬≤ |x| dx = 4. The absolute value function is even, so we can double the integral over [0,2]. Geometrically, the "V" shape creates two triangles with total area 4. The absolute value function forms a "V" shape with vertex at the origin. Split at x=0 where the behavior changes. On [‚ąí2,0], |x| = ‚ąíx; on [0,2], |x| = x. |x| on [‚ąí2,0] is a line from 2 to 0 (height at x=‚ąí2 is 2, at x=0 is 0). Same on [0,2]. Each triangle has area ¬Ĺ √ó 2 √ó 2 = 2. Total area = 4. Since |x| is even, we can use the symmetry shortcut: double the [0,2] piece. Displacement vs. Distance: Signed vs. Unsigned Integral Displacement = 0 (returns to start) but total distance = 4 (traveled 2 units forward and 2 units back). This distinction is fundamental in physics: displacement is a vector (signed); distance is a scalar (unsigned). Displacement is the signed integral of velocity. Antiderivative of sin(t) is ‚ąícos(t). Evaluate from 0 to 2ŌÄ. Displacement = 0 means net change in position is zero ‚ÄĒ it started and ended at the same place. Distance ignores direction ‚ÄĒ integrate the absolute value. sin(t) ‚Č• 0 on [0,ŌÄ]; sin(t) ‚Č§ 0 on [ŌÄ,2ŌÄ] so |sin| = ‚ąísin there. Split at the zero. First piece: ‚ąícos(ŌÄ)+cos(0) = 1+1=2. Second piece: cos(2ŌÄ)‚ąícos(ŌÄ) = 1‚ąí(‚ąí1)=2. The particle traveled 4 units total but ended where it started. Given ‚ąę‚āč‚āÉ¬≥ f(x) dx = 10 and ‚ąę‚āč‚āÉ¬≥ g(x) dx = 6, evaluate: (a) ‚ąę‚āč‚āÉ¬≥ [2f(x) ‚ąí 3g(x)] dx. (b) If f is even and ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥ f = 7, what is ‚ąę‚āč‚āÉ‚Āį f? Find the average value of f(x) = sin(x) on [0, ŌÄ]. Then find all c ‚ąą (0, ŌÄ) where f(c) = f_avg. Prove the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals from scratch using only the Extreme Value Theorem and Intermediate Value Theorem. Be explicit about where each theorem is used.

documents.title: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
documents.subtitle: Differentiation and integration are inverse operations ‚ÄĒ the deepest result in elementary mathematics
documents.tags: fundamental theorem of calculus FTC Part 1 FTC Part 2 antiderivative accumulation function Newton-Leibniz inverse operations evaluation theorem
documents.content: The Fundamental Theorem has two parts, and both parts are surprising. Part 1 says: if you define A(x) = ‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t) dt (the accumulated area from a to x), then A is differentiable and A'(x) = f(x). The derivative of the accumulated area is the original function. Differentiation undoes integration. Part 2 says: if F is any antiderivative of f (meaning F' = f), then ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). To compute the area under f from a to b, find any antiderivative of f and subtract its values at the endpoints. Integration undoes differentiation (up to a constant). Part 1 can be understood physically. Imagine driving a car and tracking position A(x) at every moment. Your speedometer reads f(x) ‚ÄĒ the instantaneous rate of change of accumulated position. Part 1 says: if you know the accumulated position A(x), its derivative A'(x) is exactly your current speed f(x). The rate at which the "area counter" advances is equal to the height of the function at that exact moment. This is compelling: adding a thin strip of width dx and height f(x) to the accumulated area increases A by approximately f(x)¬∑dx, so őĒA/őĒx ‚Čą f(x), and in the limit A'(x) = f(x). Part 2 follows from Part 1 by a beautiful algebraic argument. Suppose F is any antiderivative of f, so F' = f. We also know A'= f where A(x) = ‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t)dt. So A' = F' on [a, b], which means A ‚ąí F is a function with zero derivative ‚ÄĒ and by the Mean Value Theorem, A(x) ‚ąí F(x) = constant. Call it C. Evaluate at x = a: A(a) ‚ąí F(a) = C. But A(a) = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶÉ f = 0. So C = ‚ąíF(a), giving A(x) = F(x) ‚ąí F(a) for all x. At x = b: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(t)dt = A(b) = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). This derivation is the logical core of FTC Part 2. The notation F(b) ‚ąí F(a) is so common it has its own shorthand: [F(x)]‚āźŠĶá, read "F of x evaluated from a to b." The calculation ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥ x¬≤ dx = [x¬≥/3]‚āÄ¬≥ = 27/3 ‚ąí 0/3 = 9 is a typical application: find the antiderivative (x¬≥/3), evaluate at the upper limit (3¬≥/3 = 9), subtract the value at the lower limit (0¬≥/3 = 0). Result: 9. What would have taken a pages-long Riemann sum calculation is now three lines of algebra. Part 1 also has a chain rule version for when the upper limit of integration is a function of x rather than x itself. If g is differentiable, then d/dx[‚ąę‚āź^(g(x)) f(t) dt] = f(g(x))¬∑g'(x). This is FTC Part 1 composed with the chain rule: the outer derivative is f(g(x)) (the function evaluated at the upper limit), and g'(x) comes from the chain rule. For example, d/dx[‚ąę‚āÄ^(x¬≤) sin(t) dt] = sin(x¬≤)¬∑2x. This generalization appears constantly in differential equations and in the Leibniz integral rule. The historical importance of the FTC cannot be overstated. Before Newton and Leibniz, computing areas, volumes, and arc lengths required ad-hoc geometric arguments ‚ÄĒ brilliant but non-systematic. After the FTC, the problem of computing any definite integral was reduced to the problem of finding an antiderivative: a systematic algebraic task. The next century saw an explosion of mathematical physics, as Newton's laws of motion combined with the FTC to produce exact predictions for planetary orbits, projectile trajectories, wave propagation, and eventually Maxwell's equations of electromagnetism. Every model of physical reality that uses differential equations ultimately relies on the FTC. Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1: let f be continuous on [a, b], and define the accumulation function A(x) = ‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t) dt for x ‚ąą [a, b]. Then A is differentiable on (a, b) and A'(x) = f(x). Proof: A'(x) = lim_{h‚Üí0} [A(x+h) ‚ąí A(x)]/h = lim_{h‚Üí0} (1/h) ‚ąę‚āďň£‚Āļ į f(t) dt. By the MVT for integrals, (1/h) ‚ąę‚āďň£‚Āļ į f(t) dt = f(c_h) for some c_h between x and x+h. As h ‚Üí 0, c_h ‚Üí x, and by continuity of f, f(c_h) ‚Üí f(x). Therefore A'(x) = f(x). ‚ąé Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 2 (Evaluation Theorem): let f be continuous on [a, b] and let F be any antiderivative of f (F' = f). Then ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). Proof: let A(x) = ‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t) dt. By Part 1, A' = f. Also F' = f. So (A ‚ąí F)' = 0 on (a, b). By Corollary to the MVT: A(x) ‚ąí F(x) = C for some constant C. Evaluate at x = a: 0 ‚ąí F(a) = C, so C = ‚ąíF(a). Therefore A(x) = F(x) ‚ąí F(a). At x = b: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f = A(b) = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). ‚ąé The Chain Rule version of FTC Part 1: if g is differentiable and f is continuous, then d/dx[‚ąę‚āź^(g(x)) f(t) dt] = f(g(x))¬∑g'(x). This follows from writing the integral as A(g(x)) where A(u) = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶė f(t) dt, and applying the chain rule: d/dx[A(g(x))] = A'(g(x))¬∑g'(x) = f(g(x))¬∑g'(x). The lower limit can also depend on x: d/dx[‚ąę_{h(x)}^{g(x)} f(t) dt] = f(g(x))¬∑g'(x) ‚ąí f(h(x))¬∑h'(x). The notation [F(x)]‚āźŠĶá = F(b) ‚ąí F(a) is standard. An antiderivative F of f is sometimes called an "indefinite integral" and written ‚ąę f(x) dx = F(x) + C, where C is an arbitrary constant. The "+C" is essential: every antiderivative differs from every other by a constant, because if F' = f and G' = f, then (G ‚ąí F)' = 0 so G ‚ąí F = C. When applying FTC Part 2, any choice of C works because it cancels: (F(b)+C) ‚ąí (F(a)+C) = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). The proof of FTC Part 1 relies critically on two ingredients: the continuity of f (used in the MVT for integrals and in the passage to the limit f(c_h) ‚Üí f(x)) and the fact that A(x) = ‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t) dt is well-defined, which requires f to be integrable on [a, x] for each x ‚ąą [a, b]. Both conditions are guaranteed by f being continuous on [a, b]. If f is merely integrable but not continuous, A is still defined and still satisfies A(x‚āÄ)‚ÜíA(x) as x‚āÄ‚Üíx (A is continuous), but A may fail to be differentiable at points where f is discontinuous. The derivative A'(x) = f(x) holds at every point of continuity of f. The key step in the proof ‚ÄĒ that (1/h)‚ąę‚āďň£‚Āļ į f(t) dt ‚Üí f(x) ‚ÄĒ deserves a fully explicit argument. For any őĶ > 0, by continuity of f at x, there exists őī > 0 such that |f(t) ‚ąí f(x)| < őĶ whenever |t ‚ąí x| < őī. For |h| < őī, every t ‚ąą [x, x+h] satisfies |t ‚ąí x| ‚Č§ |h| < őī, so |f(t) ‚ąí f(x)| < őĶ. Therefore |(1/h)‚ąę‚āďň£‚Āļ į f(t) dt ‚ąí f(x)| = |(1/h)‚ąę‚āďň£‚Āļ į [f(t)‚ąíf(x)] dt| ‚Č§ (1/|h|)‚ąę‚āďň£‚Āļ į őĶ|dt| = őĶ. Since őĶ was arbitrary, the limit equals f(x). This argument works for both positive and negative h. FTC Part 2 is sometimes stated with the weaker hypothesis that F is continuous on [a, b] and F' = f everywhere on (a, b) (with the derivative at the endpoints one-sided). This covers cases where F has corners at the endpoints. The proof is identical: (A ‚ąí F)' = 0 on (a, b); by the MVT for derivatives, A ‚ąí F is constant on [a, b] (applying the MVT on any subinterval [a+őĶ, b‚ąíőĶ] and letting őĶ ‚Üí 0 using continuity of A ‚ąí F); the rest is unchanged. This slight weakening of hypotheses is rarely needed in practice but is theoretically important for piecewise-defined functions. A subtle issue: the statement "any two antiderivatives differ by a constant" requires a domain assumption. On a connected domain, if H' = 0 then H is constant (by the MVT). But on a disconnected domain like (‚ąí1, 0) ‚ą™ (0, 1), the function H(x) = 1 on (‚ąí1, 0) and H(x) = 0 on (0, 1) satisfies H' = 0 everywhere but is not constant. This is not a pathology ‚ÄĒ it matters for the antiderivative of 1/x: ‚ąę(1/x) dx = ln|x| + C is slightly misleading, because C can be different on (‚ąí‚ąě, 0) and (0, ‚ąě). The full answer is F(x) = ln|x| + C‚āĀ for x > 0 and F(x) = ln|x| + C‚āā for x < 0, with possibly different constants C‚āĀ and C‚āā. FTC Part 1: Differentiating an Integral d/dx[‚ąę‚āÄň£ sin(t¬≤)dt] = sin(x¬≤). FTC Part 1 applies even when the integrand has no elementary antiderivative. The theorem differentiates any continuous function ‚ÄĒ it does not require that an antiderivative be expressible in closed form. Identify the accumulation function. The integrand is f(t) = sin(t¬≤), which is continuous everywhere. Apply FTC Part 1 directly: the derivative of the accumulation function equals the integrand evaluated at the upper limit. Final answer. Note: sin(t¬≤) has no elementary antiderivative ‚ÄĒ without FTC Part 1, we could not differentiate this integral at all. Chain Rule + FTC Part 1 d/dx[‚ąę‚āÉ^(x¬≤) eŠĶó dt] = 2xe^(x¬≤). The chain rule contribution 2x comes from the x¬≤ upper limit. Without the chain rule, you would incorrectly get just e^(x¬≤). Always differentiate the upper limit and multiply. The upper limit is g(x) = x¬≤, not x itself. Use the chain rule: d/dx[A(g(x))] = A'(g(x))¬∑g'(x). The derivative of the accumulation function with respect to its upper limit is the integrand evaluated at the upper limit. Chain rule: A'(g(x))¬∑g'(x) = e^(x¬≤)¬∑2x. Final answer. FTC Part 2: Evaluating ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥ (2x+1) dx ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥(2x+1)dx = 12. FTC Part 2 confirms the geometric calculation. This integral is simple enough to verify geometrically, which builds confidence in the algebraic method. F'(x) = 2x+1 ‚úď. We need any antiderivative ‚ÄĒ C=0 is the simplest choice. Apply FTC Part 2: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). Evaluate F at the upper limit (3) and subtract F at the lower limit (0). Confirmed: FTC Part 2 gives the same answer as the trapezoidal area computed geometrically in Lesson 2. ‚ąę‚āÄ^ŌÄ cos(x) dx = 0: Geometric and Algebraic ‚ąę‚āÄ^ŌÄ cos(x) dx = 0. The cosine function has equal positive and negative areas on [0, ŌÄ], which cancel. This is a consequence of symmetry: cos(ŌÄ‚ąíx) = ‚ąícos(x), so the second half mirrors the first half with opposite sign. Antiderivative of cos(x) is sin(x). sin(ŌÄ) = 0 and sin(0) = 0. The positive area above the x-axis on [0,ŌÄ/2] equals the negative area below on [ŌÄ/2,ŌÄ]. They cancel exactly. Each half contributes ¬Ī1. Total = 1+(‚ąí1) = 0. ‚ąę‚āĀŠĶČ (1/x) dx and the Natural Logarithm ‚ąę‚āĀŠĶČ(1/x)dx = 1. This is actually the most elegant way to define the natural logarithm: ln(x) = ‚ąę‚āĀň£ (1/t) dt. The number e is then defined as the value where this integral equals 1. The logarithm defined by an integral has all the expected properties (ln(ab)=ln(a)+ln(b), etc.) which follow from properties of integrals. The antiderivative of 1/x is ln|x|. On [1,e], x > 0 so |x| = x. ln(e) = 1 (by definition of e). ln(1) = 0 (since e‚Āį = 1). The area under y=1/x from x=1 to x=e is exactly 1. ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ eň£ dx ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ eň£ dx = e ‚ąí 1 ‚Čą 1.718. The area under y = eň£ from 0 to 1 is e ‚ąí 1. This exact result from FTC contrasts dramatically with what would be required from Riemann sums. The antiderivative of eň£ is eň£ itself ‚ÄĒ the exponential function is its own antiderivative. Evaluate: e¬Ļ = e ‚Čą 2.718, e‚Āį = 1. Displacement and Distance for a Velocity with Sign Change Displacement = 0 m; total distance = 8 m. The particle travels 4 m in the negative direction (t‚ąą[0,2]) then 4 m in the positive direction (t‚ąą[2,3]), returning to its start. Always split the integral at velocity sign changes when computing distance. Displacement = ‚ąęv dt. Antiderivative of 3t¬≤‚ąí6t is t¬≥‚ąí3t¬≤. F(3) = 27‚ąí27 = 0. F(0) = 0. Displacement = 0 ‚ąí 0 = 0. Find when v changes sign. v < 0 on (0,2) (particle moves backward), v > 0 on (2,3) (moves forward). On [0,2], v < 0 so |v| = ‚ąív = 6t‚ąí3t¬≤. On [2,3], v > 0 so |v| = v. First piece: antiderivative is 3t¬≤‚ąít¬≥. Evaluate from 0 to 2. Second piece. Evaluate from 2 to 3. The particle moved 4 m backward then 4 m forward, ending where it started (displacement = 0) but traveling 8 m total. Spotting the FTC Error: The Discontinuous Integrand The calculation is invalid because 1/x¬≤ has a vertical asymptote at x=0 inside the interval [‚ąí1,1]. FTC Part 2 requires continuity on the entire closed interval. The true "integral" diverges to +‚ąě (not ‚ąí2). This is one of the most important errors to avoid in calculus. 1/x¬≤ is positive wherever it exists. A positive function cannot have a negative integral. The answer ‚ąí2 is immediately suspicious. The integrand has a vertical asymptote at x=0, which lies inside the integration interval [‚ąí1,1]. The hypothesis of FTC Part 2 is violated. The theorem does not apply. The integral diverges as the lower limit approaches 0. The integral ‚ąę‚āč‚āĀ¬Ļ 1/x¬≤ dx does not exist. Evaluate: (a) ‚ąę‚āĀ‚Āī (3‚ąöx ‚ąí 2/x) dx. (b) ‚ąę‚āÄ^(ŌÄ/2) (sin x + cos x) dx. Find d/dx[‚ąę_{x}^{x¬≤} cos(t¬≥) dt]. Use both the chain rule version of FTC Part 1 and the interval additivity property to handle both a variable lower and upper limit. Prove FTC Part 2 from Part 1, filling in every logical step. In particular: (a) show (A ‚ąí F)' = 0 using the MVT, (b) conclude A ‚ąí F = constant, (c) determine the constant, (d) derive ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). A particle has velocity v(t) = t¬≤ ‚ąí 4t + 3 m/s for t ‚ąą [0, 4]. (a) Find all times when the particle changes direction. (b) Compute the total displacement over [0, 4]. (c) Compute the total distance traveled over [0, 4].

documents.title: Indefinite Integrals
documents.subtitle: Building the antiderivative toolkit ‚ÄĒ the power rule reversed, and the essential +C
documents.tags: indefinite integrals antiderivatives power rule integration rules initial value problems +C constant trig integrals exponential integral differential equations
documents.content: An antiderivative of f(x) is any function F(x) with F'(x) = f(x). The process is differentiation run backwards. Since d/dx[x¬≥] = 3x¬≤, the antiderivative of 3x¬≤ is x¬≥. Since d/dx[sin(x)] = cos(x), the antiderivative of cos(x) is sin(x). Since d/dx[eň£] = eň£, the antiderivative of eň£ is eň£. Every entry in the differentiation table can be read backwards to give an antiderivative rule. The critical new feature is the constant of integration, written +C. If F(x) is one antiderivative of f(x), then F(x)+5, F(x)‚ąíŌÄ, and F(x)+C for any constant C are also antiderivatives, because d/dx[F(x)+C] = F'(x)+0 = f(x). Conversely, any two antiderivatives of the same function differ by a constant (proved using the MVT: if G'=F'=f, then (G‚ąíF)'=0, so G‚ąíF=constant). The indefinite integral ‚ąęf(x)dx denotes the entire family of antiderivatives: ‚ąęf(x)dx = F(x)+C. Forgetting the +C is a mathematical error ‚ÄĒ the antiderivative is a family of functions, not a single function. The power rule for integration inverts the power rule for differentiation. Since d/dx[x‚ĀŅ‚Āļ¬Ļ/(n+1)] = x‚ĀŅ (for n ‚Č† ‚ąí1), we have ‚ąęx‚ĀŅ dx = x‚ĀŅ‚Āļ¬Ļ/(n+1) + C. The exception n = ‚ąí1 is special: x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = 1/x has antiderivative ln|x|, not x‚Āį/0 (which is undefined). The power rule covers polynomials, roots (fractional powers), and negative powers ‚ÄĒ a vast class of functions. Any polynomial ‚ąę(a‚āôx‚ĀŅ + ¬∑¬∑¬∑ + a‚āĀx + a‚āÄ) dx is computed term by term. The linearity of differentiation ‚ÄĒ d/dx[cf+g] = cf'+g' ‚ÄĒ runs backwards to give linearity of integration: ‚ąę[cf(x)+g(x)]dx = c‚ąęf(x)dx + ‚ąęg(x)dx. This means integrals split across addition and constants factor out. To integrate a sum, integrate each term separately. To integrate a constant multiple, factor it out. Combined with the power rule, this handles any polynomial. The only restriction is that the split must happen BEFORE integrating ‚ÄĒ you cannot factor or split after integration without reintroducing constants. Initial value problems (IVPs) pin down the arbitrary constant C using an extra condition ‚ÄĒ typically a value of the function at a specific point. The procedure: integrate to find F(x) = G(x)+C (the general antiderivative), substitute the given condition F(x‚āÄ) = y‚āÄ to find C, and write the specific solution F(x) = G(x) + (y‚āÄ ‚ąí G(x‚āÄ)). IVPs are ubiquitous in physics: "a ball is thrown upward at 20 m/s from a height of 5 m" gives both the initial velocity and initial position, which together determine the height function x(t) = 5 + 20t ‚ąí 4.9t¬≤ completely. The integration table is finite and exact for elementary functions, but many seemingly simple functions have no elementary antiderivative. The function eň£¬≤ has no antiderivative expressible in terms of polynomials, trig functions, exponentials, and logarithms ‚ÄĒ despite being smooth and rapidly computable numerically. The same is true for sin(x)/x, ‚ąö(1‚ąíx‚Āī), and many others. This is not a temporary gap in our knowledge ‚ÄĒ it was proved rigorously by Liouville in 1835 using what is now called "differential Galois theory." For functions without elementary antiderivatives, Riemann sums or Taylor series must be used. FTC Part 1 still applies: d/dx[‚ąę‚āÄň£ eŠĶó¬≤ dt] = eň£¬≤ even though the antiderivative has no closed form. The standard antiderivative table for Calc 1: ‚ąęx‚ĀŅ dx = x‚ĀŅ‚Āļ¬Ļ/(n+1) + C (n ‚Č† ‚ąí1); ‚ąęx‚ĀĽ¬Ļ dx = ln|x| + C; ‚ąęeň£ dx = eň£ + C; ‚ąęaň£ dx = aň£/ln(a) + C (a > 0, a ‚Č† 1); ‚ąęsin(x) dx = ‚ąícos(x) + C; ‚ąęcos(x) dx = sin(x) + C; ‚ąęsec¬≤(x) dx = tan(x) + C; ‚ąęcsc¬≤(x) dx = ‚ąícot(x) + C; ‚ąęsec(x)tan(x) dx = sec(x) + C; ‚ąę1/(1+x¬≤) dx = arctan(x) + C; ‚ąę1/‚ąö(1‚ąíx¬≤) dx = arcsin(x) + C. Each rule is verified by differentiating the right side. Linearity of integration: ‚ąę[őĪf(x) + ő≤g(x)] dx = őĪ‚ąęf(x) dx + ő≤‚ąęg(x) dx. This follows because differentiation is linear: d/dx[őĪF(x)+ő≤G(x)] = őĪF'(x)+ő≤G'(x) = őĪf(x)+ő≤g(x) whenever F' = f and G' = g. Combined with the table, linearity allows integration of any linear combination of tabulated functions ‚ÄĒ which includes all polynomials, all linear combinations of trig functions, all polynomial-exponential sums. Differential equations of the form dy/dx = f(x) are solved directly by integration: y = ‚ąęf(x) dx = F(x)+C. The general solution has one free parameter C; an initial condition y(x‚āÄ) = y‚āÄ gives one equation for C, determining the unique particular solution. Higher-order DEs (e.g., d¬≤y/dx¬≤ = f(x)) require two integrations, introducing two constants C‚āĀ and C‚āā, determined by two conditions (typically the value and the derivative at a point). This structure ‚ÄĒ general solution = homogeneous solution + particular solution ‚ÄĒ extends throughout differential equations. A common technique is algebraic manipulation before integration: expand products, separate fractions, complete the square, or use trig identities. For example, ‚ąę(x+1)¬≤ dx = ‚ąę(x¬≤+2x+1) dx = x¬≥/3 + x¬≤ + x + C (expand before integrating). Or ‚ąę(x¬≥+x)/x dx = ‚ąę(x¬≤+1) dx = x¬≥/3+x+C (divide through first). These algebraic pre-processing steps are not integration techniques per se ‚ÄĒ they are arithmetic that converts non-standard forms into the standard table forms. The statement "all antiderivatives of f differ by a constant" is a theorem that requires proof. Suppose F' = f and G' = f on an interval (a, b). Define H = G ‚ąí F. Then H' = G' ‚ąí F' = f ‚ąí f = 0. By the Mean Value Theorem: for any x‚āĀ, x‚āā ‚ąą (a, b), H(x‚āā) ‚ąí H(x‚āĀ) = H'(c)(x‚āā ‚ąí x‚āĀ) = 0 for some c between x‚āĀ and x‚āā. Therefore H(x‚āĀ) = H(x‚āā) for all pairs, so H is constant. The proof requires that f (and hence F and G) are defined on a connected interval. On a disconnected domain, different constants can appear on different components (as discussed for ln|x| in Lesson 3). The "+C" in ‚ąęf dx = F(x)+C is a reminder of this theorem. It encodes the complete solution set of the equation F' = f: not a single function, but a one-parameter family. From the perspective of ordinary differential equations, the equation y' = f(x) is a first-order ODE, and its general solution is y = F(x)+C, a one-parameter family. One initial condition y(x‚āÄ) = y‚āÄ selects a unique solution. This is the Existence and Uniqueness Theorem for first-order linear ODEs: there is exactly one solution passing through each point (x‚āÄ, y‚āÄ), provided f is continuous. Liouville's theorem on non-elementary antiderivatives. A function built from constants, x, and the operations +, ‚ąí, √ó, √∑, n-th roots, exp, log, and trigonometric functions is called an "elementary function." Liouville proved in 1835 that many elementary functions ‚ÄĒ including eň£¬≤, sin(x)/x, 1/ln(x), and ‚ąö(1‚ąíx‚Āī) ‚ÄĒ have no elementary antiderivative. The proof uses the structure of differential fields (fields with a derivation satisfying the Leibniz product rule). Liouville's theorem implies that Risch's algorithm (1969) can decide in finite time whether any given elementary function has an elementary antiderivative ‚ÄĒ the theoretical basis for all computer algebra systems (Mathematica, Maple, Wolfram Alpha) that compute indefinite integrals. The distinction between "finding an antiderivative" and "evaluating a definite integral" is important. For definite integrals, FTC requires only that an antiderivative exist, not that it be expressible in closed form. Since every continuous function has an antiderivative (by FTC Part 1: A(x) = ‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t) dt is one), every continuous function is Riemann integrable and has a definite integral. But computing the definite integral as F(b) ‚ąí F(a) requires finding F in explicit form, which is only possible for functions in the antiderivative table (or reducible to it by substitution, parts, partial fractions, etc.). For functions like eň£¬≤, the definite integral must be computed numerically. Polynomial: ‚ąę(3x¬≤ ‚ąí 4x + 7) dx ‚ąę(3x¬≤‚ąí4x+7) dx = x¬≥‚ąí2x¬≤+7x+C. Always verify an antiderivative by differentiating ‚ÄĒ it is the easiest check in calculus. Linearity: split the integral term by term and factor out constants. Power rule: ‚ąęx¬≤dx = x¬≥/3, ‚ąęx dx = x¬≤/2, ‚ąędx = x. Simplify: 3¬∑(x¬≥/3) = x¬≥; 4¬∑(x¬≤/2) = 2x¬≤. Differentiate the answer to confirm. The +C disappears. Fractional and Negative Powers: ‚ąę(‚ąöx + 1/x¬≤) dx ‚ąę(‚ąöx + 1/x¬≤) dx = (2/3)x^(3/2) ‚ąí 1/x + C. The power rule works for all real powers except n = ‚ąí1. Check: d/dx[(2/3)x^(3/2)] = (2/3)¬∑(3/2)x^(1/2) = x^(1/2) = ‚ąöx ‚úď; d/dx[‚ąíx^(‚ąí1)] = x^(‚ąí2) = 1/x¬≤ ‚úď. Rewrite using exponent notation: ‚ąöx = x^(1/2), 1/x¬≤ = x^(‚ąí2). Power rule for each term: ‚ąęx‚ĀŅdx = x‚ĀŅ‚Āļ¬Ļ/(n+1)+C. Simplify the exponents. Simplify: 1/(3/2) = 2/3; 1/(‚ąí1) = ‚ąí1. Rewrite x^(‚ąí1) = 1/x and x^(3/2) = x‚ąöx. Initial Value Problem: Free Fall h(t) = ‚ąí4.9t¬≤ + 20t + 5 metres. Two initial conditions (v‚āÄ = 20, h‚āÄ = 5) determined two constants of integration. This is the standard free-fall equation ‚ÄĒ integrating twice from a = ‚ąíg gives the quadratic height formula. Integrate acceleration to find velocity. C‚āĀ is a constant. Apply initial velocity condition v(0) = 20 to find C‚āĀ. Specific velocity function. Integrate velocity to find height. C‚āā is a second constant. Apply initial height condition h(0) = 5 to find C‚āā. The complete height function. This is the familiar projectile equation. Trigonometric: ‚ąę(sin(x) + cos(x)) dx ‚ąę(sin x + cos x) dx = sin x ‚ąí cos x + C. Trigonometric antiderivatives are straightforward from the table; the key is remembering the sign: ‚ąęsin = ‚ąícos (not +cos). Split by linearity. ‚ąęsin x dx = ‚ąícos x, ‚ąęcos x dx = sin x. Combine constants into one C. d/dx[‚ąícos x] = sin x; d/dx[sin x] = cos x. Sum = sin x + cos x. ‚úď Expand Before Integrating: ‚ąęeň£(2 + 3eň£) dx ‚ąęeň£(2+3eň£)dx = 2eň£ + (3/2)e^(2x) + C. The key step is expanding the product first ‚ÄĒ there is no "product rule" for integration. Whenever you see a product, try expanding, simplifying, or using integration by parts (a later technique). Distribute: eň£¬∑2 = 2eň£, eň£¬∑3eň£ = 3e^(2x). Always expand products before integrating ‚ÄĒ there is no product rule for integrals. Split and factor by linearity. ‚ąęeň£ dx = eň£. For ‚ąęe^(2x) dx: guess e^(2x)/2, check d/dx[e^(2x)/2] = e^(2x) ‚úď (this is the substitution u=2x, du=2dx in action). IVP for Projectile: Finding Position from Acceleration x(t) = ‚ąí16t¬≤ + 64t ft. Maximum height = 64 ft at t = 2 s. This is the classic vertical throw under gravity formula (in feet). In SI units: x(t) = ‚ąí4.9t¬≤ + v‚āÄt + x‚āÄ. Integrate constant acceleration. Initial velocity condition pins down C‚āĀ. Integrate velocity. Initial position = 0 gives C‚āā = 0. Maximum height when v = 0. Maximum height = 64 ft, reached at t = 2 s. Simplify First: ‚ąę(x+1)¬≤ dx ‚ąę(x+1)¬≤ dx = x¬≥/3 + x¬≤ + x + C. Expanding before integrating is the easiest approach for polynomial expressions. Note: (x+1)¬≥/3 + C would be WRONG ‚ÄĒ the chain rule would add a factor of 1 in this case (since (d/dx)(x+1) = 1), making it accidentally correct here, but this coincidence breaks for (ax+b)¬≤. Expand the square before integrating. Do NOT try to apply a "power rule" with inner function (x+1) without accounting for the chain rule ‚ÄĒ that requires substitution. Integrate term by term: ‚ąęx¬≤dx = x¬≥/3, ‚ąę2x dx = x¬≤, ‚ąędx = x. Differentiate: x¬≤+2x+1 = (x+1)¬≤. ‚úď Find the following antiderivatives: (a) ‚ąę(5x‚Āī ‚ąí 3x¬≤ + 2x ‚ąí 1) dx. (b) ‚ąę(x^(‚ąí1/2) + 4x^(3/2)) dx. (c) ‚ąę(2sin x ‚ąí 3cos x) dx. A particle moves along a line with acceleration a(t) = 6t ‚ąí 4 m/s¬≤. At t = 0, the velocity is v(0) = ‚ąí2 m/s and the position is x(0) = 3 m. (a) Find v(t). (b) Find x(t). (c) At what time(s) is the particle at rest? (d) What is the position when the particle first comes to rest? Prove rigorously that the antiderivative is unique up to a constant: if F'(x) = 0 for all x in an interval (a,b), then F(x) = C (constant) on (a,b). Use only the Mean Value Theorem. Then explain why this fails on a disconnected domain, giving a concrete example.

documents.title: Applications of Integration
documents.subtitle: Net change, area between curves, average value, work ‚ÄĒ the integral as a universal measuring tool
documents.tags: net change theorem area between curves average value displacement vs distance work consumer surplus applications definite integral applications
documents.content: The Net Change Theorem is FTC Part 2 stated in physical language: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá F'(x) dx = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). In words: the integral of a rate of change over an interval equals the net change in the quantity. Integrate velocity to get displacement. Integrate acceleration to get change in velocity. Integrate marginal cost to get total cost increase. Integrate population growth rate to get population change. The net change theorem unifies all these under one principle: ‚ąę(rate) = net change. Area between two curves extends the basic area idea. If f(x) ‚Č• g(x) on [a, b], the area between them is ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá [f(x) ‚ąí g(x)] dx. This formula has a transparent geometric meaning: at each x, the "height" of the region is f(x) ‚ąí g(x) (the gap between the curves), and integrating these heights over [a, b] gives the total area. The formula is always "upper curve minus lower curve" ‚ÄĒ never worry about whether either function is above or below the x-axis. Even if both f and g are negative, f ‚ąí g is positive when f ‚Č• g, and the formula is correct. Finding the limits of integration for area between curves requires finding where the curves intersect. At an intersection, f(x) = g(x). Solve this equation to find the x-coordinates where the curves cross. Between adjacent intersections, one curve is consistently above the other ‚ÄĒ but the labeling may switch. Always check which curve is on top in each subinterval (evaluate at a test point). If the curves cross within your intended interval, split the integral at each crossing and negate the piece where the intended "upper" function is actually below. Average value is conceptually elegant: f_avg = (1/(b‚ąía)) ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx. For a temperature function T(t) = 70 + 10sin(ŌÄt/12) over 24 hours, the average temperature is (1/24) ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤‚Āī T(t) dt. The sinusoidal part integrates to zero over a full period, leaving the average equal to the constant term: 70¬įF. This is the "DC component" in signal processing, and it represents the baseline around which the temperature oscillates. Work done by a variable force is W = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá F(x) dx, where F(x) is the force at position x and the object moves from x = a to x = b. When the force is constant, W = F √ó d (force times distance). When it varies, we sum F(x) dx over the path ‚ÄĒ a Riemann sum in the limit. Lifting an object against gravity: if the object weighs mg Newtons and is lifted from x = 0 to x = h, the work is ‚ąę‚āÄ į mg dx = mgh (same as the constant-force formula, since gravity is constant near the Earth's surface). Compressing a spring: by Hooke's law, F = kx, and work = ‚ąę‚āÄň£ kx dx = kx¬≤/2 (the energy stored in the spring). Consumer surplus in economics: the demand function D(x) gives the maximum price a consumer will pay for the x-th unit. If the market price is P*, consumers buying at or below quantity x* = D‚ĀĽ¬Ļ(P*) receive "surplus" ‚ÄĒ they would have paid more than P* but only paid P*. The total consumer surplus is ‚ąę‚āÄ^(x*) [D(x) ‚ąí P*] dx: the area between the demand curve and the horizontal price line. Producer surplus is the analogous integral below the supply curve. Together, consumer + producer surplus = total economic surplus, a measure of the efficiency of a market ‚ÄĒ computed by integration. Area between two curves (general): if f and g are continuous on [a, b] and f(x) ‚Č• g(x) throughout, then Area = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá [f(x) ‚ąí g(x)] dx. If the curves cross at interior points c‚āĀ, c‚āā, ‚Ä¶, split the integral at each crossing and take the absolute value of each piece: Area = ő£|‚ąę between consecutive crossings [f ‚ąí g] dx|. Equivalently, Area = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá |f(x) ‚ąí g(x)| dx. This is a signed integral that handles all crossings automatically, but requires knowing the sign of f ‚ąí g on each piece. Average value and the mean value: f_avg = (1/(b‚ąía)) ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx. The MVT for integrals guarantees f(c) = f_avg for some c ‚ąą (a, b) when f is continuous. Average value has a physical interpretation: it is the constant value that, integrated over [a, b], gives the same total as f. In other words, the area of the rectangle with height f_avg and base (b‚ąía) equals the area under f. This is the "equal area" construction: a horizontal line at height f_avg cuts the area under f into two pieces of equal area above and below the line. Work in physics: for a constant force F over displacement d, work W = F¬∑d. For a variable force F(x) over [a, b]: W = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá F(x) dx. This follows from the same "sum of thin pieces" logic: over a tiny displacement dx, the work is approximately F(x)¬∑dx (since force is nearly constant over dx); summing gives the total. For Hooke's law: a spring with spring constant k, compressed or stretched by x from equilibrium, exerts force F = kx. Work to stretch from 0 to L: W = ‚ąę‚āÄŠīł kx dx = kL¬≤/2 = ¬ĹkL¬≤. This equals the potential energy stored in the spring. Consumer surplus: let D(x) be a demand function (price as a function of quantity), decreasing from D(0) > 0 to 0. If the market clears at price P* and quantity x*, then D(x*) = P* and CS = ‚ąę‚āÄ^(x*) [D(x) ‚ąí P*] dx. This measures total consumer welfare above what they actually paid. Similarly, if S(x) is a supply function (marginal cost), producer surplus is PS = ‚ąę‚āÄ^(x*) [P* ‚ąí S(x)] dx. Total surplus = CS + PS = ‚ąę‚āÄ^(x*) [D(x) ‚ąí S(x)] dx, maximized at the competitive equilibrium ‚ÄĒ this is the First Welfare Theorem of economics, proved by integration. The area-between-curves formula ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá |f‚ąíg| requires |f‚ąíg| to be integrable. Since f and g are continuous, f‚ąíg is continuous, and the absolute value of a continuous function is continuous ‚ÄĒ hence integrable. The formula Area = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá |f(x)‚ąíg(x)| dx is therefore well-defined for continuous f, g. For piecewise continuous functions (finitely many jump discontinuities), the absolute value formula still works: integrate separately on each piece where the sign of f‚ąíg is known, negate the pieces where f‚ąíg < 0, and sum. The justification for W = ‚ąęF(x)dx as work rests on a physical definition. Work is defined as the dot product of force and displacement: dW = F ¬∑ dx for infinitesimal displacement dx. For a one-dimensional problem with force F(x) varying with position, the total work over [a, b] is W = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá F(x) dx. This is both a physical definition and a mathematical consequence of the limiting Riemann sum: W ‚Čą ő£ F(xŠĶĘ)őĒxŠĶĘ over small displacements, and taking the limit gives the integral. The work-energy theorem (W = őĒKE) then follows from applying the fundamental theorem to F = ma = m(dv/dt) = m(dv/dx)(dx/dt) = mv(dv/dx), and integrating. The consumer surplus formula CS = ‚ąę‚āÄ^(x*) [D(x)‚ąíP*] dx requires D to be continuous (or at least integrable) and P* = D(x*). In the standard model, D is a strictly decreasing continuous function (the demand curve slopes downward) from D(0) > 0 to D(x*) = P*. Then [D(x)‚ąíP*] ‚Č• 0 on [0, x*] (since D is decreasing and equals P* at x*), and the area is positive. The total surplus CS + PS = ‚ąę‚āÄ^(x*)[D(x)‚ąíS(x)]dx requires S ‚Č§ D on [0, x*], which holds at the competitive equilibrium by the definition of market clearing. The First Welfare Theorem (competitive equilibrium maximizes total surplus) follows from showing that any other allocation produces a smaller value of the total surplus integral. A subtle but important point about the net change theorem: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá F'(x)dx = F(b)‚ąíF(a) is exactly FTC Part 2, but stated with the emphasis on the derivative F' being the integrand and F being the antiderivative. The theorem says: knowing the complete history of rates of change over [a,b] is equivalent to knowing the net change in F. This equivalence has deep implications in physics: if we know the velocity v(t) = x'(t) for all t ‚ąą [a,b], we know the displacement x(b)‚ąíx(a) exactly. Conversely, knowing only x(a) and x(b) gives us only the net displacement, not the detailed trajectory. The definite integral condenses the entire rate-function into a single net number. Area Between y = x and y = x¬≤ The area between y=x and y=x¬≤ is 1/6 square units. This is a classic result: the line and parabola intersect at (0,0) and (1,1), enclosing a region of area 1/6. Find intersections: set f = g. The curves meet at x=0 and x=1. Test a point: at x=1/2, x=0.5 and x¬≤=0.25. The line y=x is above the parabola on [0,1]. Area = ‚ąę(upper ‚ąí lower) dx over the interval between intersections. FTC Part 2: F(x) = x¬≤/2 ‚ąí x¬≥/3. F(1) = 1/2‚ąí1/3 = 1/6. F(0) = 0. Area Under sin(x) on [0, ŌÄ] Area = 2 square units. The exact area under one arch of the sine curve is 2. This elegant result is one of the most-cited definite integrals in mathematics. Sine is non-negative on [0,ŌÄ], so the area equals the (non-negative) integral directly. Antiderivative of sin is ‚ąícos. F(ŌÄ) = ‚ąícos(ŌÄ) = 1. F(0) = ‚ąícos(0) = ‚ąí1. F(ŌÄ)‚ąíF(0) = 1‚ąí(‚ąí1) = 2. Displacement vs. Distance: v(t) = t¬≤ ‚ąí 4t Displacement = ‚ąí25/3 ‚Čą ‚ąí8.33 m (net movement in the negative direction). Total distance = 13 m (32/3 m backward + 7/3 m forward). The particle ends 8.33 m behind its starting point. Factor v. The particle moves in the negative direction until t=4, then reverses. Displacement = signed integral over [0,5]. F(5)=125/3‚ąí50=(125‚ąí150)/3=‚ąí25/3. Displacement is negative: net movement in the negative direction. Piece on [0,4]: v ‚Č§ 0. Integral = ‚ąí32/3 (negative, as expected for backward motion). Piece on [4,5]: v ‚Č• 0. Integral = [‚ąí25/3] ‚ąí [‚ąí32/3] = (‚ąí25+32)/3 = 7/3. Total distance = |backward| + |forward| = 32/3 + 7/3 = 39/3 = 13 m. Average Temperature Over 24 Hours Average temperature = 70¬įF. The sinusoidal day-night variation (peak at 80¬įF, trough at 60¬įF) averages to zero, leaving only the baseline 70¬įF. This is exactly the "DC component" in signal processing ‚ÄĒ the average value of any zero-mean sinusoidal signal is zero. Average value formula with b‚ąía = 24. Constant term: 70 √ó 24 = 1680. ‚ąęsin(ŌÄt/12)dt = ‚ąí(12/ŌÄ)cos(ŌÄt/12). Evaluated from 0 to 24: ‚ąí(120/ŌÄ)(cos(2ŌÄ)‚ąícos(0)) = ‚ąí(120/ŌÄ)(0) = 0. A full period contributes nothing. The sinusoidal fluctuation averages to zero; the average temperature equals the constant baseline. Work Against Gravity: Lifting an Object W = 490 J. For constant gravity, the integral ‚ąęF dx = F¬∑d (force √ó distance), recovering the familiar formula. The integral approach becomes essential when the force varies ‚ÄĒ e.g., lifting an object on a spring, or computing gravitational work at heights where g varies significantly. Near Earth's surface, gravity is approximately constant. Force = weight = 49 Newtons. Work = ‚ąę F(x) dx. With constant force, this is just the rectangle under the force function. 49 √ó 10 = 490 Joules. For constant force: W = F √ó d (as expected). Confirms the integral gives the same result as the constant-force formula. Spring Work (Hooke's Law) The spring work is 3.75 J. Because force increases with stretch, this is not force times distance with a constant force; it is the area under the line F(x)=kx, a triangle with area 1/2 kL^2. Hooke's law: force grows linearly with displacement. Work is the integral of variable force over displacement. Antiderivative of x is x^2/2. Evaluate the definite integral. For stretching from 0 to L, integral gives the closed form. Area Between Two Parabolas Area = 64/3 ‚Čą 21.3 square units. The symmetry property can also be used: the integrand 8‚ąí2x¬≤ is even, so ‚ąę‚āč‚āā¬≤(8‚ąí2x¬≤)dx = 2‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤(8‚ąí2x¬≤)dx = 2[8x‚ąí2x¬≥/3]‚āÄ¬≤ = 2(16‚ąí16/3) = 64/3. Same answer. Find intersections: set the two curves equal. They meet at (‚ąí2, 0) and (2, 0). Upper parabola: y=4‚ąíx¬≤. Lower parabola: y=x¬≤‚ąí4. Upper minus lower: (4‚ąíx¬≤)‚ąí(x¬≤‚ąí4) = 8‚ąí2x¬≤. Antiderivative: ‚ąę(8‚ąí2x¬≤)dx = 8x ‚ąí 2x¬≥/3. F(2)=16‚ąí16/3. F(‚ąí2)=‚ąí16+16/3. Subtract: 2(16‚ąí16/3) = 32‚ąí32/3 = 64/3. Consumer Surplus Consumer surplus = $900. The demand function is linear, so the surplus region is a triangle with area ¬Ĺ √ó base √ó height = ¬Ĺ √ó 30 √ó 60 = $900. For a nonlinear demand function, the geometric area is not a simple shape, and the integral is essential. Find equilibrium quantity: set demand = price. 30 units sold. Consumer surplus = area under demand curve minus area of price rectangle. F(30)=60(30)‚ąí30¬≤=1800‚ąí900=900. F(0)=0. Consumers pay 30 √ó $40 = $1200 total but value the goods at $1200 + $900 = $2100. The $900 is "free surplus" they receive. Area of triangle = ¬Ĺ √ó 30 √ó 60 = 900. ‚úď Confirms the calculation. Find the area enclosed between y = x + 2 and y = x¬≤ (a line and a parabola). Find the intersection points first, then integrate. A particle moves with v(t) = t¬≥ ‚ąí 6t¬≤ + 8t m/s for t ‚ąą [0, 4]. (a) Find all times when the particle changes direction. (b) Find the total displacement. (c) Find the total distance traveled. A demand curve is D(x) = 50/(x+1) dollars per unit. At market price P* = 10, find (a) the equilibrium quantity x*, (b) consumer surplus, and (c) compare to a linear demand approximation through the same two points. Prove: for any continuous function f on [a,b], the average value f_avg = (1/(b‚ąía))‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f satisfies m ‚Č§ f_avg ‚Č§ M (where m,M are the min and max of f), and there exists c ‚ąą (a,b) with f(c) = f_avg. This is the MVT for Integrals ‚ÄĒ prove it from the EVT, comparison inequalities, and IVT.
